Three unlicensed teenage Animagi wandering the castle grounds exactly the way the Marauders did a generation earlier. Meanwhile, Neville is carrying the full weight of the prophecy, and it's crushing him. He fights bravely, the way Neville always fights, but somewhere in the middle of the war, before the hunt is even half finished, he falls. Harry steps in because that's what Potters do. He picks up the Horcrux hunt where Neville left off. Ron and Hermione shift forms to move undetected, and the three of them tear through the mission as a trio of unlicensed Animagi. When it comes down to the final battle, James Potter walks onto the field, kills Nagini himself, and gives his son the opening he needs. Voldemort falls to a boy the prophecy was never even about, and James finally gets to watch his son win and walk home alive. Voldemort, what if Lily never stepped in front of the crib? In this timeline, she runs, grabs a wand, fights, and loses. The sacrifice never happens, and without it, Harry is just a baby with a scar and zero magical protection. Voldemort kills both Potters the same way he kills everyone, efficiently and without thinking twice. But when he points his wand at the baby, something stops him. The prophecy says this child has the power to destroy him. So why destroy it when you can own it? He hands Harry to Bellatrix like a project with a deadline. Raise him, train him, make him useful. Bellatrix, thrilled to be trusted with a dark Lord's personal weapon, throws herself into it. Dark arts drills at sunrise, Occlumency at night, loyalty tests disguised as bedtime stories. Harry grows up calling Voldemort my lord, and Bellatrix the closest thing to a mother he's ever known, which is terrifying and also kind of sad. By 12, Harry is the most dangerous kid in Britain and Voldemort's proudest achievement. Bellatrix taught him everything, dueling, cruelty, survival, and somewhere in between she made the mistake of actually caring about him. Voldemort notices. Attachment is weakness, and Bellatrix is getting soft around the edges, questioning orders when Harry's involved, shielding him from missions that might leave marks. So Voldemort does what Voldemort always does with things that stop being useful. He kills her, casually, over dinner, in front of Harry. And that's when the weapon breaks. Harry switches sides that night for one reason. The only person who ever loved him is dead on the dining room floor, and the man who ordered her to raise him is the one who killed her. Harry walks out knowing every secret Voldemort spent 12 years trusting him with. Hideouts, passwords, weaknesses, all of it. The Dark Lord built his perfect weapon, aimed it at the world, and somehow forgot it could turn around. By the time he realizes the war is already over. Now, all these families could have shaped Harry, but some wizard shaped the entire wizarding world, and the movies barely mention them. All 18 legendary wizards that Harry Potter movies never fully explained. That's your next lesson here at Stick Man Hogwarts. Click now before Dumbledore adds another secret he forgot to mention.


=== All 12 Ways MUGGLES Could Have Killed Voldemort EXPLAINED in Detail ===
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=5DrQGQRUJQU

Pistol. The boy who lived spent his life trying to stop a noseless wizard. But if you think about it, killing Voldy is comically easy, and Muggles have several ways. Let's start with the first one. Now, remember when Dumbledore spent six books hunting Horcruxes, building a master plan, and grooming a teenager for a death mission? A Glock 17 costs 500 quid and fits in a jacket pocket. Wizards duel like it's the 1700s. One spell, dramatic pause, eye contact, maybe a little speech about destiny. Meanwhile, a 9-mm round crosses a room before Voldemort gets past the Ava in Avada Kedavra. And Protego blocks spells beautifully. Stops Stupefy like a charm, but a metal slug moving at 370 m per second is a different conversation entirely. And nobody in the wizarding world has ever tested that because the last time they cared about Muggle weapons, cannons were still a novelty. The funniest part is Voldemort grew up in Muggle London during World War II. He heard the bombs and saw the rubble. He watched an entire city get flattened without a single wand involved. And his big takeaway was, "I should get really good at hissing at snakes." Tom Riddle had front-row seats to the most destructive Muggle technology in history and walked away thinking a wooden stick was peak warfare. Wizards designed their entire defense systems around fighting other wizards, which is a bold strategy when sharing an island with 60 million Muggles. Food poison. Muggle history is full of creative poisonings. Alan Turing, arguably the greatest mind of the 20th century, the man whose poisoned apple inspired the Apple logo, was killed by a single bite of cyanide. So, what about Voldemort? Turns out opportunities to slip him something are everywhere. We see it in Deathly Hallows, regular Death Eater dinners at the Malfoy table. Voldemort sitting at the head while Charity Burbage hangs above them. House-elves prepare and serve every meal. One compromised elf, one drop of something Muggle in the soup, and the Dark Lord is having his last supper without even knowing it. The servants he's abused for decades are handling every plate and every course. And here's the part that makes it almost too easy. The man has no nose. Most poisons get caught by smell or taste. Cyanide smells like almonds. Arsenic changes the flavor of food. But two slits where nostrils should be aren't just cosmetic. They're a tactical disaster. He's the easiest poisoning target in history. Drive-by. Voldemort walks through Muggle London. This actually happens. Death Eaters stroll through Muggle streets in robes, masks, the whole costume party. They apparate into random locations, hold meetings in visible buildings, and generally act like Muggles are furniture. Which means a black SUV rolling up at 40 mph with three guys leaning out the window is a scenario exactly zero dark wizards have ever trained for. Dominic Toretto wouldn't even turn off the engine for this one. The mechanics are almost embarrassing. A wizard needs to see the threat, process it, raise a wand, and cast a shield. All of which takes about a second on a good day. A drive-by takes maybe 2 seconds total. Car pulls up, windows down, done. Car gone. Voldemort is standing on a sidewalk wondering why his chest feels weird while a Vauxhall Corsa disappears around the corner. Very dignified end for the most feared dark wizard of all time. And if guns feel like too much effort, a lorry at 70 mph just needs a street address and a loose interpretation of traffic law. Malfoy Manor has a long driveway, by the way. Worth mentioning. Calling 911. Someone in Wiltshire looks out the window and sees a group of people in black robes and skull masks walking into a country mansion. What do they do? They call 911. Hello, yes, there's some kind of cult gathering at the big estate on the hill. Masks, sticks, one of them has a giant snake. The call gets logged. A patrol car shows up. Two officers knock on the door. And Narcissa Malfoy has to explain why 30 people in matching outfits are sitting around a table with a body floating above it. There is no good answer to that in Muggle law enforcement terms. This already looks like a crime scene. From there, it escalates beautifully. Local police see the snake, the masks, the strange tattoos, and within the hour this is flagged as a dangerous armed cult. Tactical response units move in. Helicopters, armored vehicles, and if they resist, if a single officer gets hurt, this becomes a siege. And the British government does not lose sieges. Voldemort can operate, sure, but 30 Death Eaters can't all vanish at once. And the ones left behind are surrounded by an army with thermal cameras, dogs, and orders to shoot on sight. The most feared dark wizard in history brought down by a concerned neighbor with a phone. Air superiority. So, picture the Battle of the Seven Potters. Death Eaters on broomsticks chasing the Order across open English sky, throwing killing curses at people doing 150 mph on wooden sticks. Voldemort himself shows up flying without a broom, which, fair enough, is genuinely impressive for a guy with no nose and no aerodynamics to speak of. But a firebolt does 150. A RAF Eurofighter Typhoon does 1,500. That's the difference between a bicycle and a missile with a pilot inside. Two Typhoons scrambled from RAF Coningsby would have looked at the whole chase over Surrey and thought it was a flock of very confused birds. 90 seconds, sky cleared, home for tea. And the targeting is where it gets embarrassing for wizards. Broomstick riders radiate body heat against freezing high-altitude air like birthday candles in a dark room. An AIM-120 missile locks onto heat signatures from 50 miles out and steers itself. Remember that scene in The Incredibles? Syndrome's missile chasing Elastigirl's jet. Except broomstick riders can't dodge, can't outrun it, and definitely can't radio for help. The broom rider doesn't hear it, doesn't see it, and definitely doesn't have a spell for incoming supersonic projectile from behind. Protego again assumes the threat is magical, visible, and polite enough to arrive from the front. Voldemort's big scary no broom flying trick tops out at maybe 200 mph. The missile behind him is doing 3,000. So, that's a closed casket funeral at 30,000 ft. AC-130. The Battle of Hogwarts, hundreds of Death Eaters standing in formation outside the castle waiting for Voldemort's signal. Shoulder to shoulder in an open field with zero air cover. Every military textbook has a name for that. A concentrated ground target. An AC-130 gunship at 15,000 ft was built for exactly this. For anyone unfamiliar, an AC-130 is basically a cargo plane that someone strapped a 105 mm howitzer, a 40 mm cannon, and a 25 mm Gatling gun to the side of it. It flies in slow circles above the battlefield and fires straight down. The crew watches through thermal cameras, picks targets, and the ground just stops existing in that area. Strap Tom Cruise to this thing and Mission Impossible ends in about 4 minutes. Voldemort included. It was designed to support troops against fortified positions, which means a crowd of people in black robes standing in a field is almost insultingly easy. A 105 mm shell carries about 3 kg of high explosive and a 15 m blast radius. A wizard can block one spell at a time. An AC-130 sends dozens of rounds per second from an altitude no broomstick is patrolling. Voldemort can shield himself from Dumbledore's best magic, but continuous fire from something he can't see, can't hear until it's already hit, and has no spell to counter. That's a fundamentally different problem. Chemical weapons. Remember when Ron got poisoned in Half-Blood Prince and Harry shoved a bezoar down his throat? Saved his life in about 10 seconds. Bezoars cure most magical poisons. Keyword being magical. A bezoar against sarin gas is just a rock in a dead man's mouth. Wizards have an entire branch of magic dedicated to detecting potions, curses, and enchanted substances. Sneakoscopes, secrecy sensors, probity probes. Impressive toolkit until the threat is a colorless, odorless nerve agent developed in a Muggle lab that operates on chemistry wizards literally don't teach at Hogwarts. Snape could identify Veritaserum by smell alone, but hand him a vial of VX and he'd probably try to brew with it. One canister in a ventilation shaft and the room is done before anyone reaches for a wand. Sarin works in minutes. The body shuts down, pupils constrict, muscles seize, lungs stop. By the time someone thinks to operate out, most of the room can't stand. Voldemort survived killing curses, prophecy loops, and a decade without a body, but none of that helps when the air itself is the weapon and every breath pulls it deeper. Land mines. During the Battle of Hogwarts, Voldemort sets up camp in the Forbidden Forest, a known location, announced paths, and hours of warning. The Order knew exactly where he'd be and how long he'd stay. Dumbledore literally planned for this moment, and the best counter strategy was sending a teenager to walk in and die on purpose. Meanwhile, a Claymore mine weighs about 1 and 1/2 kg, fits under a pile of leaves, and fires 700 steel balls in a 60° arc when someone trips a wire. And the explosive inside, C4, a plastic military explosive Muggles use because it's stable, powerful, and completely undetectable by magic. Wizards can sense enchanted objects, cursed necklaces, jinxed brooms, but a plastic block with zero magical properties is invisible to every scanning spell ever invented. Muggle engineering wins before a single wand gets raised. Electricity. Magic disrupts electronics. Nothing digital survives near places like Hogwarts. Circuits fry, screens die, processors melt. So, wizards never bothered learning about electricity, but here's what they missed. Raw electrical current isn't a device, it's physics, and physics doesn't care about magic. Malfoy Manor has a big iron gate. Voldemort walks through it every day. Connect that gate to the national grid 400,000 volts running through the metal he touches with bare hands, and the dark lord is dead before his fingers leave the handle. At high voltage, muscles lock up. The body physically can't let go of the source, which means Voldemort is stuck gripping the gate while the current does the rest. When Death Eaters rush over to help, they grab him, complete the circuit, and drop right next to their master. Possibly the cheapest method on this entire list, a bit of wiring in the national grid does all the work. The best part is the defense against this is a pair of rubber gloves from a hardware store, and there is not a single wizard alive who knows that. Sniper. There's a moment in Goblet of Fire that any military sniper would watch and quietly lose their mind. Voldemort just got his body back, stands in the middle of an open field surrounded by tombstones, and spends a solid 5 minutes monologuing to a circle of Death Eaters about his glorious return. Arms spread wide, bald head glowing in the moonlight, standing completely still. A Muggle sniper on any hill within a kilometer and a half would have ended the entire franchise right there. Somewhere between, "I who have gone further than anybody along the path that leads to immortality," and the next dramatic pause, a British L115A3, the same rifle the Royal Marines actually use, puts a .338 Lapua Magnum round on target. Voldemort would be dead before hearing the shot. Every defensive spell in the wizarding playbook assumes the threat is visible, coming from the front and glowing green. A piece of metal from a hill he can't even see doesn't play by those rules. Honestly, Dumbledore could have skipped the whole Order of the Phoenix, the spy games, the Horcrux scavenger hunt, and just hired John Wick. One Muggle hit man with a contract and a clean line of sight, the Death Eaters would stand there in their little masks completely lost trying to figure out which curse makes a head do that. UK government. The first chapter of Half-Blood Prince is is of the most underrated scenes in the series. The Minister of Magic walks into the Muggle Prime Minister's office through a fireplace and casually briefs him on Voldemort. Bridges collapsing, Dementors breeding, mass prisons break out. The PM hears all of it and then just sits there feeling stressed. Meanwhile, this man commands MI5, MI6, the SAS, GCHQ, and the fifth largest military budget on Earth. One phone call to GCHQ, Britain's signals intelligence agency, the people who tap phones and run satellites. And every magical location mentioned in that briefing gets coverage within an hour. Malfoy Manor, visible from space. It's a giant estate in Wiltshire with peacocks in the garden. Not exactly a hidden bunker. MI5 starts tracking anyone in robes walking through central London, cross-referencing with the Brockdale Bridge incident. And within a week, there's a file on every Death Eater who ever apparated near a Muggle street. Then the SAS gets the call, Britain's elite special forces. Voldemort is sitting at Lucius's dining table feeling dramatic while a helicopter lands on his lawn. The Death Eaters can't even call for backup because wizards don't have phones. Their emergency communication system is a burning tattoo on the forearm, which is very intimidating and completely useless against a team that moves faster than an owl. Ground troops. Here's the thing about Horcruxes that wizards completely overthink. Voldemort can't die permanently. Fine. He comes back. So what? After Godric's Hollow, his big dramatic return took 13 years. A loyal servant, a very specific ritual with bones, flesh, and blood, and one extremely unfortunate Triwizard Tournament. 13 years to respawn. The British Army has 150,000 active personnel. They can wait. The Muggle strategy here is beautifully simple. Treat Voldemort like a recurring pest problem. He shows up, ground troops eliminate him. He spends another decade floating around Albania as an angry frog. He comes back again, another squad is already briefed and ready. The military literally trains for this. Sustained operations against a target that keeps resurfacing. They've been doing it in the real world for decades. Except those targets didn't eat a magic cauldron and a pet snake to function. And every time Voldemort dies, he comes back weaker, more desperate, with fewer followers willing to go through the whole circus again. Wormtail cut off his own hand for the resurrection ritual. Finding a second volunteer for that gets significantly harder when the last guy's reward was a silver hand and a lifetime of regret. Eventually, the Death Eaters just stop picking up when the Dark Mark burns. So, Muggles are more dangerous than wizards ever gave them credit for, and history proves it. All 12 Muggle events that affected the wizarding world explained. That's your next lesson here at Stickman Hogwarts. Click now or we're sending a lorry up your driveway.


=== What If All These Harry Potter Marriages Were TRUE? ===
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=ZZ3ToBllyj4

Draco and Hermione. If you've ever opened a single Harry Potter fanfic in your life, you've seen these two. The fandom wrote more Dramione content than Rowling wrote actual books. So, let's stop treating it as a joke and actually ask, what happens to the entire story if these two get together? Everything changes around book six. Draco gets the task to kill Dumbledore, and he's already falling apart under the pressure. Now, imagine he's secretly with Hermione, a Muggle-born. He can't go through with it. He flips sides early, goes straight to Dumbledore, and suddenly the Order has a spy inside Malfoy Manor, feeding them Death Eater movements, plans, and schedules. Basically, a teenage Snape, except people actually like him. Here's the real gossip. The Malfoy Manor scene in Deathly Hallows becomes a completely different nightmare. In canon, Bellatrix tortures Hermione on the drawing room floor while Draco stands there, pretending he can't identify Harry. If Draco loves Hermione, that scene either ends with him blowing his cover to save her, or it never happens at all, because Draco already warned the trio to stay away from snatchers. Either way, the Golden Trio now has a fourth member who knows every secret passage the Death Eaters use inside Hogwarts, and the final battle gets a lot shorter. Voldemort and Bellatrix. Let's be honest, Bellatrix was already Voldemort's wife in everything but paperwork. She talked about him like a god, fought harder for him than anyone with an actual last name in the Death Eaters. And the way she looked at him made Rodolphus Lestrange, her actual husband, the most invisible man in wizarding Britain. So, what if Voldemort loved her back and they had a daughter? Let's call her Serpens, because the Blacks name their kids after stars, and yes, we're ignoring Delphi from the Cursed Child, because so does most of the fandom. Serpens is born around the first year, half-blood daughter of the man who built his whole movement on blood purity, which is already the kind of hypocrisy that writes itself. Voldemort falls in 1981, Bellatrix goes to Azkaban, and baby Serpens gets passed to the Malfoys like a very dangerous Christmas present. She grows up with Draco, goes to Hogwarts, gets sorted into Slytherin, and nobody knows her dad is the most wanted wizard alive. Then Voldemort comes back, Bellatrix breaks out, and Dumbledore, who figures out everything, learns that the man with no weaknesses has a 15-year-old daughter sitting in the Slytherin common room. The Order doesn't need to hunt seven Horcruxes anymore. They need one girl. Voldemort starts pulling Death Eaters off missions to guard Serpens. Bellatrix loses her mind trying to protect both her master and her child, and the war machine that terrorized Britain grinds to a halt because the Dark Lord is doing school pickups. Voldemort without love had no weakness and almost won. Voldemort with love has a daughter in Hogwarts and can't focus on anything else. So, yeah, the war ends in months. Ron and Lavender. Everyone remembers this one. It actually happened. Sixth-year Ron and Lavender were a real couple for about six months. The Won-Won era. It ended when Ron got poisoned and whispered Hermione's name from his hospital bed, which is a pretty definitive breakup. But what if Won-Won just kept going? The problem is simple. Lavender adores Ron exactly as he is. Sounds sweet, but works terribly. She never tells him he's being thick, which, if you've read the book, someone needs to do roughly every 40 pages. Hermione made Ron better because she expected more. Lavender makes Ron comfortable, and comfortable Ron is the Ron who panicked under pressure, almost failed every exam, and never would have walked back into that frozen forest with the Deluminator. The Horcrux hunt either loses him or never gets him in the first place because Lavender would grab his sleeve and say, "Won-Won, it's dangerous." And honestly, he'd listen. Meanwhile, Hermione ends is with Krum, the man asked twice. He was nothing if not persistent and becomes the youngest Minister for Magic operating from Bulgaria's most famous living room. Ron runs the joke shop with George. Molly smiles at Lavender over Christmas dinner while silently mourning the daughter-in-law she almost had. Ron was always capable of being great. He just needed someone who refused to let him be one one forever. Harry and Hermione. Harry and Hermione, the couple that half the fandom was ready to bet money on. The movies practically advertised it. The tent dance scene in Deathly Hallows had zero business being that romantic and Warner Brothers knew exactly what they were doing. So, what if they actually got together? Ron leaves during the Horcrux hunt either way. The locket feeds on jealousy and if Harry and Hermione are actually a couple, that locket barely needs to try. Except in canon, Ron comes back because of the Deluminator, destroys the locket and pulls the sword of Gryffindor out of a frozen lake. In this timeline, Ron leaves and stays gone because there's nothing ambiguous about the situation anymore. He's just the third wheel who got confirmed. Most fans don't realize how much of the end game depends on Ron specifically. The trio loses the Deluminator, the sword moment, and the one person who knew how to get into the Chamber of Secrets to grab basilisk fangs for destroying Horcruxes. Harry and Hermione are smart, but Hermione overthinks and Harry rushes in. Ron was the weird middle ground that kept them functional. Without him, they're a couple that argues about strategy until Voldemort finds them. And then there's the Weasley problem. Harry loses his surrogate family. Ginny is standing somewhere being awkward and Christmas at the Burrow gets real quiet. Two best friends gained a relationship and lost the one person who made them a team. Narcissa and Snape. Narcissa Black had every rich pureblood in Slytherin lining up and she picked Lucius Malfoy. The blonde, the money, the manner. But what if she'd picked the half-blood genius sitting three rows behind him who was quietly becoming the most dangerous wizard in school? Narcissa and Snape marry, and their son Draco Snape grows up with black family gold and a father who can out-duel anyone in the room. He's sharp. He's got the attitude. But, Snape who actually got the girl he loved turned into a completely different man, and that man raised a Gryffindor. Draco lands in Harry's dormitory. They argue about Quidditch at breakfast, and by third year they're the kind of friends that Scorpius and Albus would have been a generation later. Now, Lucius marries someone else, has a son, and when Voldemort returns, that son gets the punishment job: kill Dumbledore. In canon, Snape swore the unbreakable vow to protect Draco Malfoy, shadowed him all year, and ultimately killed Dumbledore on that tower so the kid wouldn't have to. In this timeline, Snape married Narcissa and owes Lucius absolutely nothing. So, the vow never gets made, and Lucius's kid walks up to that tower alone with a shaking wand, and no one coming to save him. He fails, and Voldemort doesn't forgive failure. The boy doesn't survive the summer, and Dumbledore walks into seventh year still breathing, still planning, and still being the only wizard Voldemort ever feared. And then, there's Narcissa's sister. Bellatrix has never been good at keeping quiet. She talks about the Dark Lord the way other people talk about their kids. Over family dinners, Narcissa hears about a cup locked in Bellatrix's Gringotts vault, about objects the Dark Lord treats as more precious than any father's life. Narcissa passes everything to Snape. Snape passes it to Dumbledore. And suddenly, the Order has Horcrux locations handed to them across a dinner table instead of three teenagers guessing their way through a forest. Dumbledore alive and armed with a full list of Horcruxes. Narcissa feeding intel from her sister's living room. And the woman who lied to Voldemort's face once in canon now doing it every single week. The war wraps up before Harry's seventh year even starts. Hermione and Fred Weasley. Everyone knows opposites attract and there's no bigger opposite at Hogwarts than Hermione Granger, the girl who color codes her homework, and Fred Weasley, the guy who once turned a school corridor into a swamp and flew away on a broomstick. They start spending time together at the Burrow, Hermione laughing at things she'd normally report to a prefect, Fred actually listening to someone for the first time in his life, and the whole family sees it happening before Ron does. When Ron figures it out, something breaks. This is the kid who spent six years being the least special Weasley. No money, no fame, no talent that anyone notices. Harry gets the glory, Hermione gets the grades, and now his own brother gets the girl. Voldemort would kill for a Weasley, a pureblood from Dumbledore's most loyal family. Angry, humiliated, knowing every Order safehouse, every member, every plan. Ron doesn't show up at Malfoy Manor in a mask. He shows up broken, looking for someone who'll tell him he matters. The Death Eaters give him that. The Order loses its safehouses within a week. The Horcrux hunt collapses because Ron knows Harry is in the forest, and the Weasleys have to fight a war knowing their youngest son is on the other side of it. Hermione chose the fun brother, and the other one burned the world down over it. Turns out heartbreak and dark magic need the same thing to work, someone who feels like they have nothing left. Hagrid and Maxime. Everyone talks about Hagrid's love for dangerous creatures, but somehow forgets he also fell for the only woman in Europe who could look him in the eye without a step ladder. Madame Maxime, headmistress of Beauxbatons, half-giant, and the one person who made Hagrid put on a horrible hairy suit and try to comb his beard. In canon, they had a thing during the Triwizard Tournament, went on a mission to recruit giants for Dumbledore together, and then quietly fizzled out. So, what if it didn't fizzle out? The giant mission is where everything changes. In canon, Hagrid goes to the giants practically alone. Maxime splits off halfway through after they start arguing and the whole thing is a disaster. The giants side with Voldemort and at the Battle of Hogwarts, they're smashing through the castle walls fighting for the Death Eaters. Now, imagine Hagrid and Maxime show up together, united, a power couple who are living proof that half-giants can lead schools and earn the trust of wizards. The pitch to the giants goes from one weird gamekeeper mumbling about Dumbledore to a diplomatic mission. Maxime is a headmistress. The woman runs on entire school. She knows how to handle a room, even if the room is full of 20-ft tall giants with anger issues. The giants join Dumbledore. The Battle of Hogwarts gets 20-ft reinforcements on the right side of the wall. The Death Eaters lose their biggest weapon and Voldemort's army suddenly looks a lot smaller all because Hagrid finally learned to comb his beard. Fleur and Ron. Remember when Ron tried to ask Fleur to the Yule Ball and instead of actual words, something between a scream and a hiccup came out of his mouth? Fleur's Veela blood hit Ron so hard he forgot how sentences work and the whole school watched it happen in real time. In canon, Fleur married his older brother Bill, which means Ron had to sit through every family dinner watching his crush call his brother "my love" in a French accent. So, what if Ron's hiccup somehow worked? The Horcrux hunt collapses immediately. Ron with a Veela wife at home doesn't volunteer for months of camping in frozen forest with his two best friends. Hermione and Harry go alone, which we already covered. The duo overthinks everything and saves nothing. But the real damage is Shell Cottage. In canon, that house was safe because Bill Weasley is a professional curse-breaker from Gringotts. He wrapped that cottage in protective charm strong enough to hide from Voldemort himself. Ron and Fleur would probably still be living in the Burrow with Molly because Ron is Ron and a house costs money. When Dobby grabs the trio and apparates away from Malfoy Manor, there's no secret cottage by the sea with bank level security. There's the Burrow, which Death Eaters have been watching since September. So, Fleur picked the wrong Weasley, and Voldemort gets to keep his last Horcrux forever. Neville and Luna. Remember that moment in Deathly Hallows part two where Neville stops mid-battle to say he's mad about Luna? The books never wrote that, not once. The author herself admitted she felt a pull between them, but decided it was too neat. So, Neville married Hannah Abbott, and Luna married Newt's commander's grandson. The fandom has never recovered. So, what if the movies got it right? The whole seventh year changes. Neville is leading the DA from the Room of Requirement, taking beatings from the Carrows, turning into someone Hogwarts barely recognizes. Then Death Eaters grab Luna and lock her in the Malfoy Manor basement, and the quiet herbology kid who used to lose his toad every other week does exactly what love tells him to do, something incredibly stupid. He grabs a handful of DA members, no real plan, no backup, and goes after her. The Malfoys are expecting Harry Potter, so a group of teenagers sneaking through the garden barely counts as a Tuesday for their security. Neville gets caught in about 15 minutes. Now, both of them are locked in that basement. The DA has lost its leader, and Hogwarts has nobody holding the resistance together. The Carrows take the Room of Requirement back. Dumbledore's Army scatters, and when the Battle of Hogwarts finally comes, there's no one standing in front of Voldemort pulling a sword out of the hat. Nagini lives, and the last Horcrux survives. So, yeah, Voldemort wins. Turns out the bravest thing Neville ever did in canon was not being in love during a war. Snape and Lily. Snape and Lily, the love story that the actual books are secretly built on. Remove Snape's love for Lily, and seven books collapse like a Jenga tower missing every other block. So, what if she loved him back? Fifth year by the lake, the moment that ended everything in canon just doesn't happen. Friendship turns into something more, and Lily Evans becomes Lily Snape. A wedding that would make James Potter swallow his own broomstick. They have a son, Harry Snape, which already sounds like a potions accident. But the prophecy doesn't care about last names. Voldemort kicks down the door the same way he always does. Lily dies shielding her son. Snape dies beside her, and baby Harry survives with a scar and absolutely no one left. James is alive, but wants nothing to do with Lily's kid by his worst enemy. So, Narcissa Malfoy takes him in. Severus was practically family to the Malfoys, and Harry grows up at the manor, sleeping down the hall from Draco, wearing hand-me-down robes that actually fit for once. The Sorting Hat drops him into Slytherin before he even sits down. Voldemort spots a Snape raised by Malfoys with rage and zero guidance, and recruitment basically handles itself. The wizarding world survived because Severus Snape got his heart broken. Give him the girl, and Voldemort wins. Worst love story ever told for everyone except Snape, Harry, and Luna. Everyone at Hogwarts called Luna crazy. She believed in invisible creatures that only she could see. Luna was also the only person who could comfort Harry after Sirius died. In canon, Harry married Ginny. But what if he fell for the girl everyone underestimated? The first thing that changes is who Harry trusts. Luna doesn't compete with Ron and Hermione. She just exists slightly to the left of everyone, noticing things nobody else bothers to look at. And the creature she notices first is Dobby. Luna is the only student at Hogwarts who talks to house-elves like they're people, asks their names, sits on the kitchen floor eating pudding with them at midnight. Dobby adores her immediately, and suddenly Harry has a girlfriend with a direct line to the most loyal, magically gifted spy network in Britain. House-elves who can apparate through any ward, enter any building, and are invisible to every Death Eater because pure-bloods don't look down. When the war gets serious, Harry doesn't camp in a frozen forest with a broken radio. Luna sends Dobby into Malfoy Manor because Dobby already knows the layout, the schedules, the secret rooms. Dobby brings back information about the cup in Bellatrix's vault at Gringotts. Dobby brings back the sword of Gryffindor from wherever Snape hid it. And Dobby brings back Ollivander and the other prisoners weeks earlier than canon because Luna asked him to check on anyone who looks sad down there. Ron and Hermione are still around, still fighting, but the war's engine runs on Luna's weird friendships and Dobby's terrifying loyalty. The elf who died saving Harry in canon becomes the reason Harry wins months earlier in this one. Sirius and Marlene McKinnon. Marlene McKinnon barely gets a mention in the books. A name on the list, a photo in the old order, a family wiped out by Death Eaters before the war even ended. Most fans scroll right past her, but if Sirius Black loved this woman, the entire Harry Potter story unravels from page one. The night Death Eaters show up to kill the McKinnons, Sirius is there. He's one of the best duelists the Order has. And with Marlene fighting beside him, they survive. Barely, bloody, but alive. Hours later, the Potters die. Sirius wants to hunt Peter Pettigrew down and tear him apart with his bare hands. And the old Sirius would have done exactly that, sprinted off alone, no plan, straight into Peter's trap. But Marlene grabs him and says the one thing nobody said in canon, go to Dumbledore first. So, Dumbledore gets the real story. Peter is the traitor. The Aurors find him before he can fake his own death. And Peter Pettigrew goes to Azkaban instead of Sirius. Sirius walks free, picks up baby Harry from the wreckage, and raises him with Marlene in a house full of magic, motorcycle parts, and someone who actually loves him. Harry Potter arrives at Hogwarts with no scar trauma, no cupboard under the stairs, no Dursleys. He's confident, loved, trained by a godfather who taught him to duel before he could ride a broom. Meanwhile, Peter is rotting in Azkaban, which means when Voldemort needs a servant for the resurrection ritual in the graveyard, there's nobody coming. The Dark Lord stays a ghost, wandering Albanian forests forever, waiting for a follower who's never getting out of prison. The whole series, seven books, two wars, hundreds of deaths, doesn't happen because one woman told Sirius Black to calm down for 5 minutes. So, yeah, love could have changed everything. It just didn't. But, the story kept going. All 21 new wizards and witches you missed after the Deathly Hallows explained in detail. That's your next lesson here at Stick Man Hogwarts. Click now before the fandom writes another ending.


=== The FIRST Member of EVERY Family in Harry Potter EXPLAINED in Detail ===
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=LOkA1liC7l0

Percival Dumbledore. Percival is the earliest known member of the Dumbledore family. He was Albus Dumbledore's father. Yes, Hogwarts' favorite headmaster and the greatest wizard of his age. Percival lived with his wife, Kendra, a Muggle-born witch, and their three children, Albus, Aberforth, and Ariana, in the village of Mould-on-the-Wold. A good-looking man with twinkling eyes, the same ones Albus would later inherit. Everything was fine until his 6-year-old daughter, Ariana, was attacked by three Muggle boys who caught her doing magic. The assault left her traumatized, unable to control her powers, and changed the entire family's trajectory forever. Percival tracked down the boys and attacked them. The Wizarding press called it savage. The Ministry called it a crime. They sentenced him to Azkaban, where he died. And here's the part that makes the story sting. Percival never explained why he did it. If the Ministry had found out what the attack did to Ariana, what she had become, they would have locked her in St. Mungo's permanently. So, Percival kept his mouth shut. Accepted a life sentence and let the entire Wizarding world believe he was a Muggle-hating bigot. His reputation was never cleared, even after Albus became the most celebrated wizard in Britain. The Dumbledore name still carried that stain. Albus carried his father's name as his middle name, Albus Percival Wulfric Brian Dumbledore. And when he finally held the Resurrection Stone, one of the people he wanted to see and apologize to was his father. Armand Malfoy. Remember Malfoy Manor, the estate where Draco grew up? Well, it was Armand Malfoy who first got that house. He was Draco's earliest known ancestor, a French wizard born in the late 10th century. He studied at Beauxbatons, France's famous Wizarding school, the one we know from the Triwizard Tournament. Armand arrived in England in 1066 with William the Conqueror's invasion force and walked away with an entire estate in Wiltshire. Armand provided what the records carefully describe as unknown, shady, and almost certainly magical services to the future king. Nobody documented what those services actually were. For a man whose surname literally translates to bad faith in Old French, that tracks perfectly. King William thanked him by seizing a piece of Wiltshire from the locals and handing it straight to Armand. His descendants then expanded the property by quietly annexing their Muggle neighbors' land because why buy something when you can just absorb it? One estate turned into a compound, and the compound turned into a dynasty. That same piece of stolen Wiltshire land is where roughly a thousand years later Voldemort would be sitting at the head of the dining table, holding war councils and keeping prisoners in the cellar. So, every dark chapter of Malfoy history traces back to one shady deal between a French wizard and an English king in 1066. Linfred of Stinchcombe. Linfred of Stinchcombe is the 12th century healer whose nickname accidentally became the most famous surname in wizarding history. Linfred lived quietly in a small Gloucestershire village, kept to his cottage, and brewed potions for anyone who knocked on his door. His Muggle neighbors never questioned the potions because Linfred seemed perfectly harmless, gentle, eccentric, and always covered in something herbal. They started calling him the Potter, local slang for the strange old man always pottering around with bottles. The descendants leaned into it, and the surname stuck. Here's the embarrassing part. Linfred was secretly one of the wealthiest wizards of his century. While the village patted him on the back for being a sweet weirdo, he was quietly inventing the early prototypes of Skelligrow and Pepper-Up Potion and selling the recipes to the wizarding market for staggering sums. He charged the Muggles next to nothing and let his wizarding clients pay full price for the same brews. Linfred died rich, leaving comfortable inheritance to seven children. And the eldest, Hardwin, married Iolanthe Peverell, granddaughter of Ignotus. That wedding is the moment the Cloak of Invisibility quietly slid into the Potter vault and stayed there for roughly 700 years. Gormlaith Gaunt. Gormlaith Gaunt was the earliest known member of the same family that centuries later produced Marvolo, Merope, and Lord Voldemort himself. An Irish witch born around 1576, she owned Salazar Slytherin's original wand, spoke Parseltongue, attended Hogwarts as a Slytherin, and came back to Ireland with a single obsession, blood purity at any cost. When her sister started helping Muggle neighbors with healing potions, Gormlaith burned her cottage down with her and her husband inside. Charming. Then kidnapped their 5-year-old daughter Isolt and kept her locked away for 12 years. Banned from school, banned from owning a wand, forced to watch her aunt curse anyone who wandered too close to the house. Isolt eventually escaped, stole Gormlaith's wand, and fled across the Atlantic. Gormlaith tracked her down 14 years later, crossed the ocean disguised as a man, and attacked Ilvermorny, the school Isolt had built in Massachusetts. She put the family into enchanted sleep, deactivated Slytherin's wand with Parseltongue, and was killed by a Pukwudgie with a poison arrow before she could finish the job. The wand was buried on the school grounds and grew into a snakewood tree. The Gaunts spent the next few centuries marrying their own cousins until they ended up broke, unstable, and producing the darkest wizard in history, Thomas Riddle. Thomas Riddle was Voldemort's grandfather. And here's the thing, he was a Muggle, completely, entirely, boringly non-magical. Thomas was born in 1880, lived in the Riddle house, a handsome manor overlooking Little Hangleton, and held the title of village squire. He and his wife Mary were rich, snobbish, and genuinely disliked by basically everyone in town. Their son, Tom Riddle Sr., was apparently worse. The Riddles looked down on the Gaunts, the impoverished wizarding family living in a shack nearby, which is ironic considering the Gaunts thought they were the superior ones. Two families despising each other from opposite ends of the same road. Then Merope Gaunt, Marvolo's daughter, fell in love with Tom Senior and reportedly used a love potion to make him marry her. When the potion wore off, he fled straight back to his parents and the whole village had the scandal of the century. Thomas took his son back in and the Riddles carried on as if the whole thing had been temporary bout of insanity. They had no idea a grandson existed. 17 years later, that grandson showed up at the Riddle house. Thomas, Mary, and Tom Senior were found dead in the drawing room. Three bodies, zero explanation. The local doctor couldn't find a single thing wrong with them. So, Thomas Riddle died without ever knowing that magic was real, that his son had been bewitched, or that his own grandson was the one who killed him. Matilda Weasley. Matilda Weasley, the earliest known member of the Weasley family and Ron's probable great-great-great-great-aunt, was already everything a Weasley should be a century before Ron was born. Red hair, warm heart, and a talent for ending up in the middle of chaos. Born sometime before 1870, Matilda attended Hogwarts, got sorted into Gryffindor, obviously, and discovered the Room of Requirement with a house-elf named Deek during her second year. After graduating, she joined the Ministry of Magic as a curse-breaker, which basically means she got paid to deal with dangerously cursed objects. She was apparently so good at it that decades later another Weasley, Bill, would follow the exact same career path. The family really does have a type. At the Ministry, Matilda fell in love with a fellow curse-breaker named Paul and the two quit their jobs to travel the world together as freelancers. Romantic, adventurous, and completely doomed. Matilda eventually missed her family and wanted stability. Paul wanted to keep moving. They split up and Matilda returned to England alone. She never married, never had children, and channeled all of that energy into going back to Hogwarts, this time as Transfiguration Professor. She was good enough to get promoted to Deputy Headmistress, serving under Phineas Nigellus Black. Matilda's nephew, Gareth, attended Hogwarts at the same time, brewing experimental potions that routinely exploded and earning himself regular detention from his own aunt. If that doesn't sum up Weasley family dynamics in one sentence, nothing does. Like Corvus Black, like Corvus Black, great-great-great-grandfather of Sirius, Bellatrix, and Narcissa, is the earliest known member of the House of Black. Everything that family became, the motto, the mania, the burned-off faces on the tapestry, traces back to him. Like Corvus married a pureblood witch named Magenta Tripe, and yes, that's a real name in the wizarding world. And they had three children, a daughter, Misa Pinoe, and two sons, Cygnus and Arcturus, both named after constellations. Through Cygnus, Like Corvus became the grandfather of Phineas Nigellus Black, the most hated Headmaster in Hogwarts history, whose portrait still hangs in the Headmaster's office complaining about everything. The Blacks were also famous for their looks, tall, dark-haired, aristocratically elegant, the kind of family that carried themselves like wizarding royalty and genuinely believed they were. Like Corvus lived to 64, kept the bloodline pure, and left behind a dynasty that would spend the next century and a half tearing itself apart over the very principles he built it on. Corvus Lestrange the First. Corvus Lestrange the First, great-great-grandfather of Leta Lestrange from Fantastic Beasts, and distant ancestor of Rodolphus, the man Bellatrix Black married, is the earliest known member of the Lestrange family. A French wizard born in 1729, and the man who started a naming tradition so stubborn it lasted five generations. Corvus married Heloise Beaufort and had two sons, Corvus the Second and Falco. The firstborn got dad's name. So did the next three firstborns. Five consecutive generations of Corvus Lestrange. The family treated baby names the way most people treat passwords. Just reuse the same one and hope for the best. Corvus is Latin for crow, which explains the raven on the family crest and their motto, Corvus oculum corvi non eruit, meaning a crow will not pull out the eye of another crow. Basically, Lestranges protect Lestranges, unless they're swapping heirs across the Atlantic or pledging loyalty to the nearest dark wizard. Then all bets are off. The Lestranges were originally French, part of the Sacred Twenty-Eight, and famously particular about how they recorded their lineage. The family tree that shows up in Fantastic Beasts has detailed portraits of every male member and represents every woman as a flower. The men get faces, the women get petals. That tells you everything about Lestrange family values in one visual. Corvus the First died at 39, shockingly young even by Muggle standards. Canon says nothing about how or why. For a Lestrange, that might actually be a peaceful ending. Caractacus Burke. Caractacus Burke, the earliest known member of the Burke family and, fun fact, Lord Voldemort's first and only employer, co-founded Borgin and Burkes back in 1863. That Borgin and Burkes, the shop in Knockturn Alley where Harry tumbled out of a fireplace, where Lucius Malfoy sold his dark artifacts, and where Draco picked up the vanishing cabinet that let Death Eaters into Hogwarts. How was this place even legal? A small old man with a thatch of hair covering his eyes, Caractacus had one real talent, buying priceless objects from desperate people for almost nothing. His masterpiece? In December 1926, a pregnant witch stumbled into his shop half-starved, covered in rags, and carrying a heavy gold locket with Salazar Slytherin's mark on it. Caractacus ran a few spells, confirmed it was genuine, realized it was worth a fortune, and paid her 10 galleons. He later called it the best bargain he ever made. That witch was Merope Gaunt. About 18 years after that transaction, a charming young Hogwarts graduate walked into the shop looking for work. Caractacus hired him on the spot. Brilliant kid, great at persuading people to sell their heirlooms. The kid was Tom Riddle, Merope's son. Caractacus had no idea he'd just employed the child of the woman he'd swindled. Riddle worked there until he murdered a client, stole two priceless artifacts, and vanished without a resignation letter. Caractacus Burke, the man who built a career on knowing exactly what things were worth, never figured out what was standing behind his own counter. Antioch Peverell. Antioch Peverell, the eldest of the three Peverell brothers and the first known member of one of the most important bloodlines in wizarding history. The Peverell name eventually split into two family lines that shaped everything. Cadmus's descendants became the Gaunts, and through them Voldemort. Ignotus's descendants became the Potters, and through them Harry. Antioch was the oldest, supposedly the most powerful, and the only one who left behind no family at all. Born sometime in the 13th century, Antioch was described as a combative man who wanted power above everything else. When the three brothers encountered Death at the river, or as Dumbledore believed, simply invented the Hallows themselves, Antioch asked for a wand more powerful than any in existence. A wand that would always win. If Dumbledore was right, that makes Antioch the actual creator of the Elder Wand, the most dangerous magical object ever crafted. He used it immediately, traveled to a distant village, found a wizard he had an old quarrel with, and killed him on the spot. Then walked into an inn, got drunk, and bragged to the entire pub that he owned an unbeatable wand. That same night, another wizard crept into his room, stole the wand, and slit his throat. The eldest Peverell brother lasted roughly a week with the most powerful wand in history. What a surprise. The Elder Wand passed from owner to owner for seven centuries after that through Emeric the Evil, Gregorovitch, Grindelwald, Dumbledore until Harry Potter finally broke the cycle. Antioch built the weapon, everyone else spent 700 years killing each other over it. Harfang Longbottom, ever wonder why Neville Longbottom's grandmother Augusta was so obsessed with family honor? That attitude came from somewhere and it probably started with Harfang Longbottom, the earliest known member of the family. Harfang was a pureblood wizard who married Calidora Black. Born in 1915, daughter of Arcturus Black II. They had two children, a son and a daughter. Some fans theorize that Harfang's unnamed son was the wizard who married Augusta and fathered Frank Longbottom, which would make Harfang a direct grandfather to Neville. Now, here's what makes this marriage fascinating. Calidora's own sister Cedrella married Septimus Weasley around the same time and was immediately burned off the Black family tapestry for it. Harfang kept on the wall. Same generation, two marriages. One acceptable, one a disgrace. The Blacks considered the Longbottoms respectable enough to welcome into the bloodline. If that's the case, then through Calidora, Neville carries Black family blood, the same bloodline as Bellatrix Lestrange, the woman who tortured his parents into insanity. The wizarding world really is a small, cruel circle. The Blacks let Harfang onto the tapestry because the Longbottoms seemed safe and respectable. They really should have checked the long-term forecast on that one. Original Ollivander, over 2,000 years before Garrick Ollivander sold Harry his holly and phoenix feather wand. The original Ollivander, whose first name was never recorded, was already in the wand business. The date on the Ollivander sign says it clearly, "Makers of fine wands since 382 BC." That makes the family business older than Hogwarts, older than the Ministry, and older than almost every institution we associate with British wizarding life. Garrick himself believes his earliest ancestor came from somewhere in the Mediterranean, possibly with Romans. The surname gives it away. Ollivander translates roughly to he who owns the olive wand, and olive trees have never grown in Britain. So, the original Ollivander in ancient England took one look at the local wands, crude, unreliable, barely functional, and opened a stall selling proper ones. For ancient witches and wizards, that meant cleaner spellwork, steadier magic, and a level of precision their old wands had never offered before. Basically, the ancient world's first luxury brand. Except instead of handbags, it was sticks that could kill people. Within a generation, the Ollivanders were the wand makers of Britain. The line passed through Geraint Ollivander in the Middle Ages, then Gerbold in the 19th century, then his son Gervase, and finally Garrick, the old man Harry met in that dusty narrow shop on his 11th birthday. Cantankerous Nott. Cantankerous Nott, likely the grandfather of Theodore Nott, that quiet weedy-looking Slytherin from Harry's year, is the man who decided which wizarding families were pure enough to matter. In the early 1930s, he anonymously published the Pure-Blood Directory, a book listing the 28 British families he deemed truly pure-blood. That list became known as the Sacred Twenty-Eight, and it shaped wizarding politics for decades. Cantankerous included the Malfoys, the Blacks, the Lestranges, and conveniently his own family. He also included the Weasleys, who were mortified about it and spent the next 60 years trying to distance themselves from the list. He included the Ollivanders, despite the fact that Garrick Ollivander's mother was Muggle-born, which apparently slipped past his research. And he excluded the Potters, a pure-blood family going back to the 12th century, partly because Potter sounded too Muggle, and partly because Harry's great-grandfather Henry had publicly defended Muggle rights in the Wizengamot. Basically, if Cantankerous liked you, you were pure. If he didn't, you weren't. Peer review was not part of the process. The man's name literally sounds like Cantankerous, and everything about him suggests he earned it. Now, every one of these families started somewhere, but so did the witches and wizards born into them. The childhood of every wizard in Harry Potter explained. That's your next lesson here at Stickman Hogwarts. Click now before Gormlaith Gaunt shows up to home school you.


=== What If These Harry Potter Characters NEVER Died? ===
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=7SNRxECvm_k

Sirius Black. One of the most devastating moments of the series is when Bellatrix cast the killing curse towards Sirius. But what if Harry blocks it just in time to save Sirius? Sirius stumbles back from the veil alive. And Harry doesn't have to spend the next 2 years carrying that guilt. Sounds perfect, right? Yeah, about that. Because Sirius being alive means Harry finally has a home, a real one. Sirius starts showing up at Hogwarts to check on Harry, sending howlers to Snape during potions class, and generally treating parenting the same way he treated school rules with creative disregard. After losing James and Aszkaban, stealing 12 years from him, Sirius refuses to watch another Potter walk into a dangerous mission with a plan that sounds like it was made up on the back of a napkin, which honestly it kind of was. So when Dumbledore finally tells Harry he has to leave Hogwarts and hunt war horcruxes across the country, Sirius says absolutely not. He locks down Grimald place, calls in every favor the black name still has left, and refuses to let Harry walk into a death mission with two teenagers and a tent, which is touching, heroic, and a complete disaster. Because every week Sirius protects Harry, Voldemort gets stronger. The Horcrux hunt starts late. The Ministry falls anyway. Lupin dies before Harry ever reaches him. Tonx dies looking for Remis. And by the time Harry finally escapes Sirius's protection, the war has already moved past the point where bravery is enough. So Sirius spent his whole life trying to protect James Potter's son. And somehow in the end, that becomes the one thing Voldemort needed him to do. Seis Snape, the greatest potions master alive, spent years sitting 2 feet from a giant venomous snake and somehow didn't have an antidote in his pocket. Please. Snape crawls out of the shrieking shack covered in blood, furious about the state of his robes and alive. The most dramatic man in wizarding history survives in the most onbrand way possible by being overprepared. Harry still gets the memories from the pensive. The truth still comes out and suddenly the entire wizarding world has to sit with the fact that the guy they hated for seven years was on their side the whole time. awkward, especially for Harry, who now has to get back to Snape and say something along the lines of thank you, which might be the most uncomfortable conversation either of them has ever had. And that's saying a lot. But here's where it gets interesting. Narcissa Malfoy is also walking out of this war with a secret. She lied to Voldemort's face to save Draco. Lucius is headed to Aszkaban, and she's suddenly a single mother with a ruined reputation and a manner full of bad memories. two Slytherins who spent their entire lives wearing masks, suddenly standing in the open with nowhere to hide. One day, Snape just starts showing up at the manor to check on Draco because that's what he promised Dumbledore, and he keeps finding reasons to stay for tea. Narcissa never asks why. She already knows. The most Slytherin love story imaginable. Quiet, calculated, and completely free of anything as undignified as a love confession. Dobby, remember how Bellatrix threw that knife? In this version, the aim's a little off. Dobby lands on that beach alive, shaking and probably still holding something he stole on the way out. But Ron Weasley doesn't. The knife catches him in the chest. And by the time they reach Shell Cottage, there's nothing Bill or Flur can do. The trio becomes a duo. Harry and Hermione finish the Horcrux hunt together. And somewhere between Godric's Hollow and the Forest of Dean, they fall in love. Because after losing Ron, there's nobody in the whole world who feels closer than the person carrying the same grief. Dobby stays with them through all of it. Fights his way through the battle of Hogwarts alongside Creature and the kitchen elves, and somewhere in the middle of it becomes the most famous house elf in Britain. When the war ends, Harry and Hermione marry quietly. The Burrow welcomes Hermione, mourns Ron, and somehow makes both work. Sunday dinners are tender and impossible at the same time. and Dobby. Dobby walks into the ministry with Hermione. SPEW stops being a joke. House elves get wages, contracts, the right to refuse work, and a free elf in mismatched socks standing next to the most overqualified witch of her generation tells the Wiz and Gamut exactly how it's going to be. Not bad for an elf who spent his first 10 years of freedom collecting socks, Cedric Digory. Let's go back to the tri- wizard maze. Cedric and Harry reach the cup at the same time. And in this version, Cedric steps back. Go ahead, Potter. Harry grabs the cup alone. The port key drops him straight into the little Hangleton graveyard. He watches Voldemort come back and stumbles back to Hogwarts with a story nobody can cooperate. Cedric is still standing in the maze when the news breaks. The Daily Prophet, the Ministry, half the school, they all turn on Harry. And Cedric, Cedric watches the boy who stole his tri- wizard moment become the boy crying wolf about the dark lord. The jealousy that started small, just a flicker about losing the cup, gets fed every day for a year. Cedric starts speaking out against Harry quietly at first, then publicly. Then on the front page of The Prophet, the Death Eaters are watching and a respected champion calling Harry Potter a liar is a recruitment gold. By the time Harry realizes what's happening, Cedric is already wearing the mark under his sleeve. In the cursed child, depending on your canon tolerance, of course, Cedric becomes a death eater after a humiliation in the tri- wizard. This timeline finally makes that reveal land, and Harry spends his sixth year fighting a war on two fronts, including one where the enemy is wearing his old yellow scarf, but that's a completely different story. Madi Moody. All right, let's go back to the battle of the seven potters. Mandungas Fletcher panics, disappar, and leaves Moody alone in the sky against Voldemort. In this version, Moody takes the hit, loses his other leg, and still somehow crashes into a field alive because killing Madi Moody apparently requires more than the most powerful dark wizard in history. Constant vigilance is not a joke. He recovers just in time to hear that three teenagers are planning to hunt horcruxes with zero training, zero backup, and a tent. Moody shuts that down immediately. Within 2 weeks, the trio is safer, faster, and completely miserable. Remember how Ron left in Deathly Hallows and came back? With Moody around, Ron leaves and doesn't come back because Moody won't let him. Deserted once, compromised forever. Hermione takes Moody's side. Protocol over feelings. And Ron never forgives her for it. The locket amplifies everything. Moody's paranoia becomes psychotic. Hermione becomes cold. And the boy, who was already insecure about his place in the group loses it entirely. They destroy the Horcruxes faster win the war sooner. and Ron and Hermione never speak to each other again. Efficient, brutal, and absolutely no wedding at the end. James Potter, the killing curse hits Lily, the sacrifice protects Harry, and Voldemort's own spell destroys him. All of that stays the same. The only difference is that James Potter is still alive in the next room when it happens, which means he's the one who picks up Harry, gets to Dumbledore that same night, and tells him the one thing that rewrites everything. Peter Pedigrew was the secret keeper. That single conversation saves Sirius from 12 Years in Aszaban. Catches Pedigrew before he can disappear as a rat. And here's the part nobody sees coming. Saves the Longbottoms. Because remember what happened in the original timeline? After Voldemort vanished, Bellatrix and the Langranges went looking for their master. Couldn't find him and tortured Frank and Alice Longbottom into permanent insanity trying to get answers. With James alive and openly naming names, the Lrangers go after the Potters instead, and the Long Bottoms become an afterthought. Frank and Alice stay healthy, stay sharp, and stay parents, which means Neville Longbottom grows up as the son of two decorated orarsers instead of a boy who visits his mother at St. Mongo's. This Neville shows up at Hogwarts with a wand that actually chose him. Confidence his grandmother never had to force him into, and absolutely zero fear of Snape. Because when your parents have faced actual death eaters, a man in a cape being dramatic with an abnormally large nose doesn't really register. By seventh year, Hogwarts doesn't need Harry Potter anymore. It already has Neville Longbottom running the resistance from the room of requirement. And honestly, he might just be better at the job. When Harry finally shows up for the final battle, the school barely looks up. They've already found their hero, Fred Weasley. So, let's imagine that right before the wall collapses, George yells something from across the corridor, probably a joke, probably something deeply inappropriate for a battlefield. And Fred turns his head just long enough to dodge the blast. He's alive, he's dusty, and he's already making fun of Percy before anyone's finished crying. So, what happens next? Well, here's where it gets interesting, because Fred is still on his feet. He ends up in a different corridor two minutes later, rounds a corner, and sees Fenrir Greyback on top of Lavender Brown. In the original timeline, Hermione blasted Grayback off her. But whether Lavender survived was left horribly unclear in this one, Fred gets there first, hits Grayback with something that probably wasn't in any approved spell book, and Lavender survives. Now, the twins reopen the shop, expand to Hogsme, and business is booming. 40 howlers per week from parents whose children have mysteriously turned orange. Ministry inspectors leaving with nosebleleed nougat in their pockets and no memory of why they came. Classic Weasley operation. But the real twist, Fred starts dating Lavender. Yes, that Lavender. Ron's ex-girlfriend. The one Lavender. And if you think Ron ever let that go, you haven't met Ron. Sunday dinners at the burrow became the most passive aggressive event in Wizarding Britain. and Molly is absolutely living for it. Lily Potter. So, let's say Voldemort kills James, walks upstairs, and actually keeps his promise to Snape. He offers Lily the exit. She takes it. Sirius shows up seconds later, buys enough time for Lily and Harry to get out, and dies doing it because apparently protecting Potters is a full-time job with zero survivors. Voldemort gets caught, tried, and thrown into Aszkaban in the most satisfying court case the wizarding world has ever seen. Then, Snape shows up. Snape offers help awkwardly and Lily accepts because she has nowhere else to stand. Grief pulls them close and within a year they marry. Lily insists Harry keeps the Potter name and Snape agrees without arguing which might be the most romantic thing he's ever done. A year later they have a daughter, Iris Snape, half-blood princess of the most unlikely family in Wizarding Britain. Hogwarts is interesting. Snape is still the scariest teacher in the building. Harry still ends up in Gryffindor and family dinners during Christmas break are exactly as uncomfortable as you'd imagine. Then Voldemort breaks out because of course he does. The Horcrux hunt, the final battle, Dumbledore finishing what the prophecy never started. All of it happens faster and uglier than anyone planned. Voldemort dies for real this time. The wizarding world exhales and Lily quietly leaves Snape within the year. Too many ghosts and a marriage that was built on grief instead of a foundation. Lily and Harry visit James' grave together, and she tells him everything. Flee and Euphemia Potter. In case you didn't know, Flee and Euphemia Potter are Harry's grandparents. James' parents, who died of dragonpox shortly before Harry was born. So, in this scenario, them being alive means Harry grows up with actual family, actual magic, and the kind of childhood that involves bedrooms and Christmas presents. When Lily and James die, baby Harry skips the Derslley's entirely and goes straight to the Potter estate to grandparents who adore him. Fleemont doesn't believe for a second that Sirius betrayed his son. Sirius lived with the Potters after running away from the blacks. He was practically family. Flee uses money, lawyers, and whizzing gamma pressure until Sirius gets a trial. Pedigrew's name comes up. The rat gets hunted down years before Ron ever finds him in his bed. And the entire third book just disappears. Harry shows up at Hogwarts confident, loved, and already knowing what a Quidditch World Cup looks like. Ron is still his best friend. Hermione is still the smartest person in the room. But Harry doesn't need either of them the way he did in the original story. He already has family waiting at home. So, when Luna Loveg Good shows up wearing her radish earrings and talking about creatures nobody else can see, Harry actually listens because he knows what it's like to see things other people can't. Within a year, they're in love. And it might be the most romantic thing in the entire series. When the war comes, Harry fights like someone with everything to lose. He has Luna, he has his grandparents, and he ends it fast because this time he actually has a life worth getting back to. Bellatrix Lrange. So Molly's curse misses and in this new timeline, Bellatrix Lrange survives the one moment Canon never allowed her to see. Her noseless master dying in front of her. So by the time the war ends, Bellatrix is sent to the rebuilt Aszaban without dementors with a secret she's been carrying for 9 months. 6 weeks into her sentence, Delini Riddle is born in the cell. Half black, half dark lord, and probably the most uncomfortable birth certificate ever filed at the ministry. Kingsley Shacklebolt, the new minister and the only person in the room not terrified of paperwork, makes a decision nobody else is willing to make. He takes the child quietly off the record and brings her to Harry Potter. Harry says yes because he understands the danger better than anyone. A child raised as a threat can grow into one. It's the most insane piece of policy in wizarding history and it absolutely works. Harry raises her under the Potter name first as the child nobody is allowed to know about and later when his own children are born as the older sister no history book ever mentions. Bellatrix dies in Aszaban a few years later never having met her daughter again. And somewhere in the forest of Dean, the boy who lived is teaching the dark lord's daughter how to fly a broom. How touching. Hedwig. The killing curse clips her wing during the seven potters escape and she spirals down into the darkness somewhere over Siri. Harry sees her fall and thinks that's it. He grieavves her quietly the way he grieavves everything between catastrophes. But Hedwig is alive. A muggle family finds an injured snowy owl in their garden the next morning and takes her to a wildlife center because muggles are like that. She heals in a few weeks, flies out of an open window, and does what any owl would do. goes looking for her wizard. The problem is Harry is on the Horcrux hunt under every protection charm Hermione could think of. And even the most loyal owl in Britain can't track someone who doesn't want to be found. So Hedwig flies to the only place that ever felt like home, Hogwarts. She spends the winter in the owlery, waiting, and somewhere in the Scottish Highlands nearby, a wild phoenix is doing exactly the same thing. When the battle begins and magic explodes over the castle like a signal flare, Fox feels it. and Hedwig, who has been waiting for months with the patience of an owl who has watched Harry Potter do homework, leads him straight to the one person they both came back for. Phoenix tears hit the battlefield and wounds that should have been fatal start closing. Fred opens his eyes under the rubble. Remis keeps breathing. Tonx gets back on her feet. Lavender Brown survives Greyback's attack with scars she'll carry forever, but she carries them alive. All because a postal owl loved her boy enough to wait. Reheis Lupin and Nymphodora Tons. Imagine this. Remis and Tons walk out of the Battle of Hogwarts alive. Go home to baby Teddy and for about 3 weeks everything is perfect. Domestic Bliss werewolf edition. Then Kingsley Shakolt calls. The new ministry wants to reform werewolf legislation and they need a face for the campaign. A respectable war hero familyman werewolf who proves that lyanthropy doesn't make someone a monster. Guess who fits the description perfectly and hates every second of it? Remis Lupin spent his entire life making sure nobody noticed him and now his face is on the Ministry pamphlets. Tons thinks it's hilarious. Tons also thinks he should do it because if the guy who fought Voldemort can't change how people see werewolves, then who can? Remis knows she's right, which makes it significantly worse. And then Greyback resurfaces. He spent his whole life preaching that werewolves should be feared, that they're predators, that they should build an army and take over. And now his most famous victim is standing on ministry posters telling everyone that werewolves are perfectly safe members of society. So he starts attacking again, deliberately, publicly making sure every new bite lands right when Remis is making progress. Remis wanted a quiet life with Teddy and Tons. Instead, he got a second war against the man who made him what he is, fighting over what it means to be it. Regulus Black. Regulus Black was Sirius's younger brother. He died trying to destroy Voldemort's lock at Horcrux in a cave full of inferai. Pulled under the lake before he could finish the job. So this time, Creature grabs his hand. That's the only difference. One House elf pulling a little harder, dragging his master out of that lake before the Inferai close in. Regulus crawls out of the cave with a horcrux in his pocket and a decision to make. Disappear or walk into the Order of the Phoenix and tell them everything. He walks into Dumbledore's office with a locket and a word that changes the entire war. Horcrux. Dumbledore now knows what Voldemort's been making decades before he was supposed to. He starts hunting them himself quietly, carefully. When the gaunt ring surfaces, he destroys it from a safe distance. walks away with both hands intact and the astronomy tower scene never happens. Dumbledore lives. Snape never has to play assassin. By the time Harry walks into the forbidden forest, Dumbledore has already told him everything. The scar, the seventh horcrux, why he has to let Voldemort kill him. Harry goes, dies, comes back, and his old headmaster is still there on the other side. All because one boy walked out of the cave and decided to talk. Dumbledore. So, let's go back to the astronomy tower. Death Eaters Below. Draco frozen with his wand on Dumbledore and Snape walking through the door with the killing curse already in his lips. In this version, Snape lowers his wand says no, and Dumbledore stays alive barely. The tower erupts into chaos. The Death Eaters escape into the night. Draco disappears with them and Snape is left standing in the wreckage of the plan he was supposed to finish. For one terrible moment, he waits for Dumbledore's judgment. Instead, Dumbledore forgives him. calm, devastating, and already thinking three moves ahead. The old plan is gone. So, they build a new one. The curse is counting down his last months, and dying turns out to be the thing that finally makes Dumbledore brave enough to face the person he's been avoiding for half a century. He walks into Nurman Guard, sits across from Gellard Grindleald, and they have the conversation. Who really killed Dumbledore's sister, Ariana? Whatever wars between them, that was never quite just friendship. and everything they should have said 50 years ago. Meanwhile, Voldemort is hunting the Elder Wand and traces it to Dumbledore. The 1945 duel is hardly a secret. But even dying, Dumbledore is the one wizard Voldemort won't face headon. And by the time the curse finishes the job, the wand is already with Harry. Harry already had the cloak and the stone. Now he walks into the final battle as the master of death with the most powerful wand in existence against a dark lord carrying a borrowed one. Fewer people die at Hogwarts that night. The war ends cleaner and somewhere in Nurmanguard, Grindleald is still breathing. Now that we've brought everyone back, let's see who showed up next. === All 18 Legendary Wizards The Harry Potter Movies Never Fully Explained ===
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=CkNyA-V4-ME

Merlin. Ever heard a wizard scream, "Merlin's beard!" and wondered who the heck Merlin actually was? Because the films definitely weren't going to tell you. Eight movies and the most famous wizard in history never got a single scene. He was known as the prince of enchanters, a charm specialist so talented that the wizarding world literally named its highest award after him. He attended Hogwarts as part of one of its earliest generations, meaning Salazar Slytherin himself probably taught him. And yes, Merlin was a Slytherin. He proved himself a gifted seer while still a student. Mastered ancient magic that most modern wizards can't even identify and went on to establish rules preventing witches and wizards from using magic against Muggles centuries before the Statute of Secrecy existed. After Hogwarts, he joined King Arthur's court, not as a myth, as an actual job, and founded the Order of Merlin, originally an organization fighting for Muggle rights. And the green ribbon on the award, legend says it was chosen to reflect his Hogwarts house, because even a thousand years later Slytherin keeps the receipts. Morgana le Fay. She was one of the most powerful dark witches in recorded wizarding history. Medieval, politically motivated, operating at the highest levels of both the magical and Muggle world simultaneously. While Merlin sat at Arthur's court building laws to protect Muggles, Morgana built a kingdom. She became queen of Avalon, according to legend, one of the nine enchantresses who governed the island and the most dangerous of the group. She mastered the Animagus transformation centuries before the Ministry existed to regulate it, turning into a bird at will, healer, dark witch, and shapeshifter all in the same person. And at some point she challenged Merlin directly. The duel became so legendary that Hogwarts immortalized it in a stained glass window, right next to the Defense Against the Dark Arts classroom. A thousand years later, students still walk past it on the way to class. Linfred of Stinchcombe. This is the very first Potter. Harry's bloodline starts right here in a vegetable garden. Linfred lived in the village of Stinchcombe in Gloucestershire, where his Muggle neighbors thought it was just a harmless, absent-minded old man growing funny plants. They came to him for remedies when they were sick, and he always delivered. What they didn't know was that his cures were magical. Behind closed doors, he was inventing potions that would out last him by centuries. Early versions of Skele-Gro and Pepperup Potion, both still in use at Hogwarts today. His neighbors called him the Potterer. The nickname stuck, corrupted over time into Potter. That's the origin. That's where the name comes from.
