He had seven children, left them all a significant pile of gold from potion sales, and his eldest son, Hardwin, married Iolanthe Peverell, daughter of Ignotus Peverell. She brought the invisibility cloak into the family on marriage eight centuries ago, and Harry's most important possession was secured. Nicolas Flamel. Most wizards in this universe are fictional characters, but Nicolas Flamel was a real person. He was a 14th century French alchemist who inspired Rowling to build an entire plot around his life's work. In the wizarding world, Flamel discovered the Philosopher's Stone somewhere in the medieval era, the only known object capable of producing the elixir of life, and simply kept going. He attended Beauxbatons, met Perenelle there, and later funded the entire castle and grounds with alchemist gold. There's a fountain on the school's grounds still named after the couple. 665 years old, quietly settled in Devon with his wife, collaborating with Dumbledore on alchemy research between what appears to have been a great deal of opera, unhurried reading, and the kind of peaceful existence that only six centuries of practice can perfect. He even owned a phoenix embossed book that let him communicate with Dumbledore and other wizards across the world. Basically, a magical group chat 600 years before anyone else thought of it. During Grindelwald's rise, he fought alongside Newt Scamander and Dumbledore's allies. A 600-year-old alchemist joining a resistance movement because apparently retirement wasn't in the elixir side effects. There's one theory that's hard not to believe. Flamel was the invisible king of the wizarding world for centuries, quietly pulling strings from behind the scenes. And Dumbledore's political power only existed because Flamel backed him, which would explain why Dumbledore's influence collapsed so fast after the stone was destroyed. Herpo the Foul. Every Horcrux in the series, the diary, the ring, the locket, the cup, all of it, traces back to one person, an ancient Greek dark wizard whose name literally translates to creeping thing. He knew what he was. Herpo invented the Horcrux, the concept of fragmenting your soul and hiding the pieces inside objects to cheat death. Voldemort didn't originate the idea. He spent decades refining someone else's worst work, treating ancient notes as a personal blueprint. The basilisk came from Herpo the Foul, too, hatching a chicken's egg beneath a toad, which raises immediate questions about what the failed attempts looked like. The result was a snake capable of killing any living creature that met its gaze directly. Herpo was a Parseltongue, so he could control it. Everyone else just had to hope to inventions both still causing problems a thousand years later. Remarkable legacy for a man who never got a single second of screen time. Nagini. Everyone remembers the snake with the beautiful name, but do you know what her life looked like before Voldemort? She was a Maledictus, born with a blood curse passed down through generations, one that let her transform into a serpent. In 1927 Paris, she performed at Circus Arcanus, a traveling freak show where she was forced to shift into a snake on stage for paying crowds. That's where she met Credence Barebone, another person the wizarding world had used and thrown away. They escaped the circus together and she helped him search for his true identity. What neither of them knew at the time was that Credence was the son of Aberforth Dumbledore, Albus's brother, which made him one of the most valuable targets in the wizarding world. Grindelwald figured this out first and spent months manipu- -lating Credence with promises about his true identity, all to turn him into a weapon against Dumbledore. At the rally in Paris, Credence walked straight into it. Nagini saw through every word, but she couldn't stop him from believing. She lost the one person she had and ended up fighting alongside Newt Scamander and Dumbledore's allies instead, which means for a brief moment in wizarding history, Voldemort's future Horcrux was on the side of good. At some point after that, the curse completed. The woman disappeared. The snake remained, alone, unable to communicate with anyone, possibly for decades, until she wandered into an Albanian forest and met a bodiless dark wizard who appeared to speak her language. The rest of that story you already know. Salazar Slytherin. Salazar Slytherin was one of the greatest wizards of the medieval era, a Parseltongue, a pioneer of magical education, and one of the four founders of the most important institution in the wizarding world. Most people remember him for the house, the falling out with Gryffindor, and the secret chamber he built underneath the school with a basilisk inside and instructions for his heir to finish what he started. Slytherin was also a skilled Legilimens, reading minds on top of talking to snakes, and his wand was made of snakewood with a core of basilisk horn, probably from the very basilisk he left in the chamber. He used Parseltongue to put the creature into a deep sleep that prevented it from aging. A thousand years of suspended animation waiting for the right heir to wake it up. And remember Herpo the Foul, the wizard who invented both the Basilisk and the Horcrux? Some believe Slytherin found Herpo's research and did the same thing. Split his soul and place it inside the snake. That would explain why it only obeyed his direct heir and ignored every other Parseltongue including Harry. His locket survived for centuries becoming one of Voldemort's Horcruxes. If the theory holds, Voldemort got the idea from his own ancestors homework. Fleamont Potter. Remember that pile of gold in Harry's Gringotts vault? The one he stared at in disbelief on his very first visit? A man named Fleamont put most of it there. Fleamont Potter was Harry's grandfather, a wizard who got bullied mercilessly at Hogwarts for his name and responded by becoming one of the best duelists in the school. After graduating, he invented Sleekeazy's Hair Potion. Two drops tames even the most bothersome barnet and quadrupled the family fortune with it. Hermione actually used it for the Yule Ball, which is an irony the Potter family probably never anticipated. He married Euphemia and for years they couldn't have children. They had completely given up hope when late in life she fell pregnant. James was their miracle, their only son, and they spoiled him accordingly. Both Fleamont and Euphemia lived long enough to see James marry Lily, but Dragon Pox took them within days of each other before Harry was born. Godric Gryffindor. Everyone knows Godric Gryffindor as the founder of the house, the man behind the Sorting Hat, and the wizard who gave his name to Godric's Hollow, where Harry was born and where Dumbledore grew up. The story goes Gryffindor and Slytherin were genuinely close friends before the split. Well beyond colleagues building a school together. The falling out over Muggle-borns ended a real relationship, which makes the whole story considerably worse. Now, about the sword. Most people know it appears to worthy Gryffindors, but the backstory is wild. Gryffindor commissioned it from Goblin King Ragnok the First, who crafted it from pure silver with egg-sized rubies. When it was finished, Ragnok loved it so much he sent Goblins to steal it back, claiming Gryffindor had taken it from him. Gryffindor fought them off, bewitched the lot, and sent them back with a threat. The grudge never died. Goblins still consider the sword stolen property centuries later, and the dispute kicked off one of the Goblin rebellions. When Grip Hook demands the sword from Harry in Deathly Hallows, that's a thousand-year-old argument. And here's the detail nobody connects. Every other founder's most prized relic was turned into a Horcrux. Slytherin's locket, Hufflepuff's cup, Ravenclaw's diadem. Gryffindor's relics were the only ones Voldemort never touched. The sword destroyed three Horcruxes, and the hat delivered it every time. So, three founders' legacies were poisoned, and the fourth one cleaned it up. Wendelin the Weird. She was a medieval witch who discovered that the flame-freezing charm turned fire into a gentle tickling sensation, and apparently enjoyed it so much that she kept coming back. Not five or 10 times, but 47. In various disguises, she let Muggle witch hunters catch her, tie her up to a stake, and light it up. They thought they were executing a dangerous sorceress. She was having the time of her life. Remember that essay Harry had to write for History of Magic in third year? Witch burning in the 14th century was completely pointless. Discuss. Wendelin was the star example. The entire Muggle persecution of witches was, for anyone with a wand and basic charm knowledge, an inconvenience at worst, and a spa day at best. Wendelin just happened to treat it as a recurring appointment. There's even a misprinted chocolate frog card listing her total at 118. Whether that's an error or Gwendoline simply went off the books as anyone's guess. Rowena Ravenclaw, "Wit beyond measure is man's greatest treasure." She engraved those words on a diadem that would eventually destroy her family. Rowena was a Scottish witch, one of the four founders of Hogwarts, and by all accounts the most brilliant of the group. Her diadem was enchanted to enhance the wisdom of its wearer, and it worked. It also became the thing her daughter Helena could not stop thinking about. Helena grew up in her mother's shadow, and eventually the jealousy won. She stole the diadem and fled to Albania. Rowena never told the other founders, pretended she still had it, but the loss broke her. She fell fatally ill, and on her deathbed sent the Bloody Baron, a man who had loved Helena for years, to bring her daughter home. He found her, but she refused. He killed her in a rage, then killed himself. Rowena died without her seeing Helena again. Both of them ended up as Hogwarts ghosts, the Grey Lady and the Bloody Baron, haunting the same corridors for a thousand years. And centuries later, a young Tom Riddle charmed Helena into revealing where she'd hidden the diadem, and turned her mother's greatest creation into a Horcrux. Bathilda Bagshot. Most people remember Bathilda Bagshot as the unsettling old woman from Deathly Hallows. That's a terrible introduction to someone who shaped the entire century before it. She wrote A History of Magic, the textbook every Hogwarts student has carried for decades, plus at least 10 other books on top of that. She was the most celebrated magical historian of the 20th century, a friend of Dumbledore since he was a teenager, and the only person in Godric's Hollow that Kendra Dumbledore would even speak to. She was also Gellert Grindelwald's great aunt, the woman who introduced young Gellert to young Albus in the summer of 1899, unknowingly setting off the chain of events that ended with Ariana's death. And in the end, Voldemort killed her, used her death to create his final Horcrux, Nagini, and placed the snake inside her body as a trap for Harry. Adalbert Waffling. Every first year who ever walked into Diagon Alley bought his book, but almost none of them could tell you who he was. Adalbert Waffling was the father of magical theory, the wizard who formulated the fundamental laws of magic, the actual rules explaining why spells work the way they do. His textbook, Magical Theory, has been required reading at Hogwarts for generations. Every wand movement, every incantation, every piece of magic anyone has ever learned at that school rests on the framework he built. Dumbledore corresponded with him as a teenager, exchanging ideas about the nature of magic, which gives you some idea of the level this man was operating at. His first fundamental law, "Tamper with the deepest mysteries, the source of life, the essence of self, only if prepared for consequences of the most extreme and dangerous kind." And his surname literally means speaking vaguely at length. For the man who defined how magic works, that's either deeply ironic or the best inside joke in wizarding academia. Cliodna. She was the eldest daughter of the last druid of Ireland, a medieval witch who could transform into a seabird at will, sort of like Maui from Moana, but make it magical. And according to legend, could also turn herself into a wave. She kept three magical birds whose songs healed the sick, putting them to sleep and curing them in the process. She discovered the properties of moon dew, a potion ingredient still in use centuries later. And her favorite hobby, flying. In Irish mythology, Cliodna was a goddess of love and beauty from County Cork. Rowling pulled her straight out of Celtic folklore and dropped her into the wizarding world. A druidess, a healer, an Animagus, and a potioner, all in one person. The kind of witch who would have made the entire Hogwarts faculty nervous, impressed, and completely unsure what to do with her. Hesper Starkey. Ever wonder why Polyjuice Potion specifically requires fluxweed picked during a full moon? Someone had to figure that out. That someone was Hesper Starkey. She spent her career studying how the phases of the moon affected potion brewing, and the results are embedded in practically every advanced recipe in the Hogwarts curriculum. Veritaserum needs a full moon cycle to mature. The Animagus Potion requires a mandrake leaf soaked in moonlight for an entire month. Every time Snape wrote specific lunar conditions into his instructions, that was Starkey's research at work. She was born in 1881, the same year as Dumbledore, and lived to 92, spending decades quietly proving that the moon wasn't just for werewolves and romantic atmosphere. Her portrait still hangs at Hogwarts, guarding a secret room, and her name is almost too perfect. Hesper comes from Hesperus, the ancient Greek evening star. A woman named after a star who spent her life studying the moon. Some things just align. Beedle the Bard. The entire plot of Deathly Hallows, the Elder Wand, the Resurrection Stone, the Cloak of Invisibility, hinges on a children's bedtime story, and nobody ever stops to ask who wrote it. Beedle the Bard was a 15th century wizard from Yorkshire who wrote the most famous fairy tales in the wizarding world. For magical children, his stories are what Cinderella and Sleeping Beauty are for Muggles. Every kid grows up hearing them. Ron was genuinely shocked that Hermione hadn't. The Wizard and the Hopping Pot, Babbitty Rabbitty and Her Cackling Stump, The Fountain of Fair Fortune, all Beedle. He wrote everything in ancient runes, possibly to keep the stories out of Muggle hands. What makes him remarkable is what the tales reveal about the man. He liked Muggles, mistrusted dark magic, and believed the worst wizards weren't the ones with the darkest spells, but the ones with the most cruelty and arrogance. Essentially predicted Voldemort's entire personality flaw 500 years early. Dumbledore left his personal copy to Hermione in his will. The book changed everything. Bowman Wright before the Golden Snitch existed Quidditch players chased a real bird, a golden snidget, and regularly crushed it to death mid-game. The wizarding world watched this happen for centuries before anyone decided to fix it. Bowman Wright did. He crafted a walnut-sized golden ball with silver wings that mimicked the snidget's speed, weight, and flight patterns exactly without the part where a living creature died at the end. He called it the Golden Snitch, and it completed Quidditch as we know it. Every match and every film, every catch Harry ever made, every point Crum scored at the World Cup, all built on one man's invention. The Snitch also carries flesh memory. It remembers the first human who ever touched it. Makers wear gloves handling them for this exact reason, and that little detail is precisely why Dumbledore was able to hide the Resurrection Stone inside Harry's first Snitch. It would only open for him. Wright is buried in Godric's Hollow, the same graveyard Harry visited while carrying a Snitch with the Resurrection Stone hidden inside. Hengist of Woodcroft. You never saw Hengist of Woodcroft in a single Harry Potter film, but he's one of the most important wizards in British magical history. Every Hogwarts student owes him their favorite weekend trip. He was a medieval Hufflepuff, possibly taught by Helga Hufflepuff herself, who was driven out of his home in Gloucestershire by Muggle witch hunters. He fled north to Scotland, settled near Hogwarts, and founded what would become the only all-wizarding village in Britain. According to legend, the Three Broomsticks started out as his house. The pub where every student has had their first butterbeer began as a refugee shelter. A man who lost everything started over and built a community that has stood for a thousand years. His bust still sits on the fourth floor of Hogwarts. His statue stands in the village he created, and his chocolate frog card was one of the very first Harry ever collected on that train ride with Ron. So, a Hufflepuff built the place every student loves most, but that never gets mentioned enough. So, yeah, these wizards were never fully explained and neither were all 11 wizarding schools in Harry Potter. That's your next lesson here at Stickman Hogwarts. Click now before Beauxbatons starts to judging your posture.


=== All 12 Snape WHAT IF Scenarios in Harry Potter EXPLAINED in Detail ===
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=EAdkD_ygirU

What if Snape survived the war? Nagini's bite is bad, but Snape has been brewing antivenom in his office for years on the off chance Voldemort ever decided he'd outlived his usefulness. Harry finds him in the Shrieking Shack a few minutes earlier than the original story allows. Gets the memories and also gets him to St. Mungo's before the venom finishes the job. He wakes up 3 days later as a war hero, which is approximately the worst possible outcome for a man who spent two decades cultivating the persona of a deeply unpleasant double agent. Harry tells everyone the truth at the trials. The Order backs it up. Suddenly, the most hated professor in Hogwarts history is being called the bravest man Harry Potter ever knew on the front page of the Daily Prophet. And Snape has to read it over breakfast. The wizarding world cannot agree on what to do with him. Half the country wants him knighted, the other half wants him in Azkaban for every Death Eater meeting he ever attended, every curse he ever cast in front of Voldemort to maintain cover, every student he ever made cry in potions. Parents of dead Order members write open letters. Former students testify on both sides. The Wizengamot holds hearings that drag on for the better part of a year, and Snape sits through every single one of them with the same expression he used to use on Neville. He's eventually cleared, given an Order of Merlin he refuses to wear, and offered his old job back, which he turns down on the spot. He leaves Britain by the end of the year, settles in a quiet coastal town under a name nobody recognizes, and spends the rest of his life brewing potions for Muggle pharmacists who have absolutely no idea who he is. What if Snape never overheard the prophecy? The barman at the Hog's Head catches Snape outside the door before Trelawney even gets to the important part. Snape gets thrown into the alley with nothing useful to report, and Voldemort never learns that a child born at the end of July is supposed to end him. The Potters are never targeted. Snape stays a Death Eater because he has no reason not to. Lily is alive, married to James, raising Harry in a house he'll never be invited to, and the only thing left in his life is the cause that gave him status when nothing else did. He's good at it. Voldemort's first war drags on years longer than it should, and Lily Potter ends up in the middle of the resistance because she's brilliant, furious, and genuinely terrifying when motivated. She's the one who eventually puts the pieces together about the Horcruxes. She's the one who leads the team that hunts them down. And in the final battle, in a duel that becomes the kind of story children grow up hearing about, she takes Voldemort down herself with a curse he doesn't see coming. Snape dies in that same battle on the wrong side of the field, hit by a stray spell from someone he never saw. Lily learns about it from a casualty list a few days later. She remembers him as a boy from Cockworth who chose the worst possible side and stayed there. And that's where her thinking on Severus Snape ends. She never finds out he loved her. And she never had any particular reason to wonder. What if Snape refused to kill Dumbledore? On the Astronomy Tower with Dumbledore already dying, Snape lowers his wand. The Unbreakable Vow doesn't activate. The vow was about protecting Draco, not finishing the job himself. A technicality Snape figured out months earlier. Snape puts himself between Draco and the Death Eaters, and together with Dumbledore they take the tower back inside a minute. Bellatrix is the only one who makes it out, sprinting through the school and across the grounds before anyone can stop her. And she goes straight to Voldemort. So, Voldy knows Snape switched sides and Draco is behind Hogwarts wards. Dumbledore calls in the order and locks down every ward in the castle. With Snape and Draco both out of reach, Voldemort decides to kill Draco himself, which means going after Lucius and Narcissa first. Snape gets to Malfoy Manor before he does, warns Narcissa, and offers her the same protection. She takes it and under a minute. Lucius refuses to leave the only world he knows, and Voldemort kills him within the week as a public reminder of what defection costs. Dumbledore is still dying from the ring curse, but with time to plan, he pulls his brother Aberforth out of the Hog's Head and into the war. The final battle plays out differently with two Dumbledores in it. Aberforth duels Bellatrix in the Great Hall and finishes her himself, which means Molly Weasley keeps her temper and her son both. Fred lives. Dobby lives, too, because the Malfoys never become the safe house he has to die rescuing prisoners from. Half the casualty list simply doesn't happen. The war still ends at Hogwarts, just with significantly more people walking out of it than before. What if Snape never became a Death Eater? All right, let's rewind. Fifth year, the afternoon by the lake. James and Sirius are supposed to humiliate Snape in front of half the school. In canon, this is the moment that kills his friendship with Lily and pushes him straight to the Death Eaters, but it never happens. They can't find him. He's two floors up in the girls' bathroom brewing illegal potions with Moaning Myrtle as moral support. Narcissa Black finds him there. Sixth-year prefect, already half engaged to a man she can barely stand, doing rounds when she hears something steaming that absolutely shouldn't be. She walks in expecting to dock points and walks out three hours later having watched Severus Snape brew something even Slughorn couldn't. She comes back the next week. It becomes a routine. Two outcasts, an absurd amount of potion fumes, and a friendship that takes a year and a half to turn into something else. By the time Narcissa is meant to marry Lucius Malfoy, she's called it off in three letters and one truly memorable scene at her own engagement party. Snape marries Narcissa the summer after Hogwarts. The Death Eaters reach out, and he doesn't take the meeting. Voldemort has nothing on offer that beats a quiet life with Narcissa. They have a son. They name him Draco Snape. Lucius marries Bellatrix on the rebound, and that household is exactly as Mormon stable as it sounds. The Potters still die because the prophecy reaches Voldemort through someone else, but the war runs differently without his most talented potions master in the ranks, and with a Bellatrix whose loyalty now has competition at home. What if Snape saved Lily? Remember when Harry sees Snape's memories after he dies? In that scene, Snape arrives at Godric's Hollow too late. Lily already dead, James gone, Snape breaking down over her body in the wreckage. This time, he gets there earlier. He arrives at the Potters' door at midnight, tells them Voldemort is coming, and gives them the lead time Canon never did. They run. James buys his family the seconds they need by holding the front hallway alone, and dies the way he always was going to die, wand drawn in front of his son. Lily operates with him from the back garden and lands at Spinner's End an hour later, the one place Voldemort would never check. Snape opens the door and doesn't try to explain himself. There's nothing he could say to a woman who just watched her husband die, so he puts the kettle on instead. She stays. The forgiveness never quite lands. Lily is too sharp for that, but the safest place in Britain for the boy who lived turns out to be the home of the man who told Voldemort about him in the first place. Harry grows up in Spinner's End calling Snape something close to a stepfather. Lily spends the rest of the war as the resistance's most dangerous wand. James gets a headstone in Godric's Hollow that the three of them visit every year, and nobody ever tells Harry the man who raised him was the reason it's there. What if Snape raised Harry? Dumbledore turns up at Spinner's End on a Tuesday with a 1-year-old in his arms and a speech ready about duty and second chances. Snape doesn't let him finish. He takes the child, closes the door, and spends the next 17 years discovering that raising the son of the woman you loved and the man you hated is its own particular kind of life sentence. The first year nearly breaks him. Every time Harry laughs, it's Lily. Every time he gets stubborn, it's James. So, Snape does the only thing he knows how to do. Buries it, locks it behind Occlumency walls, and starts teaching Harry to do the same before Harry can spell his own name. By six, Harry can shield his thoughts. By nine, he can read the surface of someone else's. By 11, he walks into Hogwarts as the most dangerous first year in the castle, and Snape has accidentally raised him into the perfect weapon against a dark lord who hasn't even returned yet. Quirrell's hidden master gets clocked within a week. The diary, the rat, all of it gets caught early because Harry sees through people the way most kids see through windows. Slytherin takes him in, and Draco hates the competition. The war that should have lasted seven years ends in three with significantly fewer funerals. Snape lives long enough to watch Harry graduate, which means he lives long enough to feel exactly how much Lily would have loved this version of her son. He never says it out loud. He didn't have to. Harry was reading him the whole time. What if Snape was sorted into Gryffindor? Picture this, the Sorting Hat shouts Gryffindor, and Severus Snape walks over to sit directly across from James Potter. Same dormitory, same breakfast table for seven years. The rivalry that played out across two separate towers in canon now has no distance at all, and it gets uglier faster. Without Slytherin, Snape loses a community, people who recognized his particular brand of intelligence, who shared his interest in the magic everyone else found uncomfortable. Gryffindor gives him nothing. So, Lily watches the bullying escalate year by year, sees it closer than she ever did in canon, and starts feeling sorry for Snape somewhere around fifth year. She lets him apologize after the lake incident. He means it. By the time they leave Hogwarts, Snape has somewhere to belong that isn't a Death Eater recruiting pool. He joins the Order. James, who eventually grows up enough to apologize for who he was at 16, gets close enough that the Potters name Snape Harry's godfather. Sirius takes it about as well as you'd expect. The Potters still die anyway. Snape was never inside Voldemort's circle and never could have warned them. And the rest of the canon plays out the same. Sirius framed Harry on a Surrey doorstep, 14 years of waiting. Snape spends every one of those years being a godfather in the abstract until the night Harry needs him at the Department of Mysteries. This time Snape falls through the veil taking the curse meant for his godson. Sirius lives. Harry loses the closest thing to family he has left and never gets to know that the man who died saving him was the same boy his mother once stopped speaking to by a lake. What if Lily chose Snape by the lake in fifth year? The moment that ended everything in the original story simply doesn't happen. Snape catches himself before the word leaves his mouth, apologizes like a person who actually means it, and Lily decides to give him the chance she always was quietly wanted to give him anyway. They graduate together, marry quietly somewhere that doesn't involve James Potter throwing rice, and have a son they name Harry Snape. The prophecy doesn't care about last names. Voldemort still hears the part of it that matters, still arrives at their door, and still meets a mother willing to die in front of her child. Lily's sacrifice works the way it always would have. The ancient magic doesn't ask for paperwork. Severus dies beside her. Harry survives with a scar and absolutely nobody left. Petunia takes one look at the Snape-shaped infant on her doorstep and slams the door. But Dumbledore needs Harry behind blood wards, so a compromise gets worked out. The Dursleys keep him during the school year, and the Malfoys, practically family to Severus, take him for the holidays. 11 summers at Malfoy Manor and Harry knows exactly what he is long before any Hogwarts letter arrives. Narcissa teaches him etiquette, Lucius teaches him politics, and Draco grows up next to him as something between a brother and a rival who hexes him over breakfast. By nine Harry can throw curses most grown wizards can't pronounce. The Sorting Hat takes 1 second. Slytherin. Voldemort, freshly returned through Quirrell, watches a Snape by name and a Malfoy by upbringing walk into his house and decides to play the long game. Harry takes the mark in 5th year and means every syllable. He fights on the wrong side of the war and lands in Azkaban with a sentence long enough to outlive the people who put him there. Lily died saving the wrong version of her son. What if Snape told Harry the truth early? Sometime in Harry's first year, Snape pulls him aside after a potions lesson and tells him everything. The prophecy, Lily, the debt, the reason behind every detention and every cutting remark. Harry, 11 and starving for any adult who'll be honest with him, believes him. The hatred everyone expected to define them for 7 years simply doesn't form. They cooperate. By 3rd year, they're meeting weekly and Harry trusts Snape more than he trusts anyone except Ron and Hermione. The problem is the scar. The connection in Harry's head is leaking everything he sees and hears straight back to Voldemort. And Harry has no Occlumency training to stop it. Snape tries to teach him, but the trust between them makes Harry too relaxed in those sessions to ever build a real wall. Voldemort can see Snape sitting across from Harry and see the strategy meetings, the maps, the names. The most valuable double agent in the war is being broadcast live to the enemy and nobody in the room knows it. Voldemort doesn't move on the information. He plays it. He starts feeding Snape false intelligence at Death Eater meetings. A fake Horcrux location here, a wrong rumor about Nagini there. Knowing every word will reach Harry by the end of the week and Dumbledore by morning. The Order spends 2 years chasing ghosts. By the time Snape realizes what's been happening, Harry has already led an expedition to a cave that doesn't contain what he thinks it does and Voldemort is waiting inside it. Harry dies in that cave. Snape dies trying to get him out. The trust that should have saved them both became the exact mechanism that killed them. Voldemort wins the war on the back of one honest conversation. What if Snape was Harry's father? Lily picks Severus over James in seventh year, marries him quietly two summers later, and dies in the hallway at Spinner's End with a 1-year-old wrapped in her arms. Snape gets to the house an hour after Voldemort vanishes and pulls his son out of the rubble with hands that won't stop shaking. He takes Harry, disappears from Britain entirely, and starts planning something that doesn't fit inside anyone's larger strategy. For 10 years, nobody knows where they are. Snape raises Harry across half a continent, Bulgaria, Albania, Greece, anywhere with old magic and quiet villages, and turns him into something the wizarding world has never seen before, a weapon designed specifically for one target. Harry learns Occlumency before he learns to ride a bike. He learns dark arts because Snape has decided that fighting Voldemort cleanly is a luxury they can't afford. By 11, Harry can duel adult wizards. By 13, he can win. Voldemort returns through Quirrell and immediately starts hearing rumors about a boy and his father moving through Europe destroying his old hiding places. Horcruxes start vanishing one by one before Voldemort even fully has his body back. He sends Death Eaters after them, and the Death Eaters don't come. The final confrontation happens at Spinner's End because Snape has a sense of symmetry. Harry kills Voldemort at 14 in the same hallway where his mother died with his father standing behind him. He never sets foot in Hogwarts, and he never meets the kids who would have been his closest friends in another life. The wizarding world wakes up one morning to find the war over. Nobody can quite explain who finished it. Snape and Harry are gone again by lunch. What if Snape never learned Occlumency? Snape returns to Voldemort at the end of the first year already a double agent for Dumbledore with one critical gap in his preparation. Occlumency was the skill he kept meaning to master and never quite did. Brilliant at potions, brilliant at dueling, fluent in dark magic most adults wouldn't recognize on a page and somehow the one thing that was supposed to keep him alive got pushed to next month every month for 15 years. Voldemort returns through Quirrell during Harry's first year and calls his Death Eaters back to the graveyard a few years later. Snape arrives, kneels and Voldemort looks at him for approximately 4 seconds before reading every secret behind his eyes like text on a page. Voldemort reads the meetings with Dumbledore, the Order's locations, the plans for Harry, all of it in one calm sweep. The execution happens in front of every Death Eater present slowly over the course of an hour as a lesson about what happens to people who think they're cleverer than the Dark Lord. Word reaches Hogwarts by morning through Death Eaters who deliberately let it leak because Voldemort wants Dumbledore to know exactly what he's lost. Dumbledore loses the architecture of his entire plan along with his spy. Snape was supposed to deliver the truth about the Horcrux in Harry's head when the time came, supposed to slip Harry the memory in a pensive, supposed to be the bridge between what Dumbledore knew and what Harry needed. With him gone, Harry walks toward the final battle carrying a piece of Voldemort's soul nobody has told him about. Harry dies in the Forbidden Forest believing the curse will save the school. The Horcrux inside him survives. Voldemort wins the war without ever quite understanding why it was so easy. What if Dumbledore never trusted Snape? Snape arrives at the top of the Hogwarts grounds in late 1980 soaking wet, half mad with grief, ready to trade everything he is for the chance to save Lily Evans. Dumbledore looks at him for a long moment. The mark on his arm, the prophecy he just delivered, the years of friendship with people who are now openly Death Eaters, and decides this man cannot be trusted with the life of a child. He turns him away at the gates. Snape leaves the grounds and goes back to Voldemort that same night rejected by the only person he thought might still see something worth saving and returns to the Death Eaters with the kind of commitment that only comes from having tried the other path and been refused. Voldemort welcomes him back, promotes him, and gains the most useful follower he's ever had. A brilliant potions master with a personal grudge against Albus Dumbledore. The Potters die on schedule. Voldemort falls the same night. But the Order of the Phoenix has no source inside the inner circle, no warning system, and the surviving Death Eaters scatter into the dark with their plans intact. When Voldemort returns through Quirrell, Snape is teaching Defense at Hogwarts under deep cover for the Dark Lord, slipped into the post by a quiet word from Lucius Malfoy, whose Ministry connections turn out to run deeper than the Order ever assumed. Instead of fighting Quirrell for the stone, Snape quietly clears his path, and Voldemort gets his body back ahead of schedule. Then Snape makes the move he's been planning for 15 years. The night Voldemort raises a goblet of the Elixir of Life, Snape is the one who hands it to him, and the cup contains everything except eternal life. Lesson learned, never let your potions master pour your celebratory drinks. Severus Snape takes command of the inner circle, claims the empty throne, and becomes the new Dark Lord with significantly better organizational skills and an actual nose. Every version of Snape, hero, villain, Dark Lord, always ends up back at Hogwarts. But Hogwarts isn't the only school out there. So, that's your next lesson. All 11 Wizarding Schools in Harry Potter explained. Click now before McGonagall catches you eyeing the competition.


=== The Childhood of EVERY Wizard in Harry Potter EXPLAINED in Detail ===
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=c1s60l4SttM

Every wizard in Harry Potter showed up to Hogwarts already broken. So, I tracked down what happened to every single one of them as kids. Some of these are heartbreaking and some straight-up disturbing. Delores Umbridge. Delores Umbridge grew up in a household that was falling apart before she could even pick a side, which for the record she absolutely did. Her father Orford was a wizard with a dead-end job mopping floors in the Department of Magical Maintenance. Her mother Ellen was a Muggle. Her younger brother was a Squib. Delores looked at this family portrait and decided the only logical move was to pretend half of it never existed. By 15, the family split clean down the middle. Delores stayed with her father and Ellen disappeared back into the Muggle world with her son. Delores never spoke to either of them again. Fun fact, she later pressured her own father into early retirement, too, slipping him a small monthly allowance to stay out of sight. Whenever co-workers asked if she was related to that Umbridge who used to mop the floors here, she'd smile sweetly and explain that her deceased father had been a distinguished member of the Wizengamot. He was, at that point, very much alive. By 17, she was already described as judgmental, prejudiced, and sadistic. So, basically fully formed Delores. The wizarding world never stood a chance. Hagrid. Hagrid was enormous from day one, which makes sense given that his mother, Fridwulfa, was an actual giantess. She left the family when Hagrid was about three, apparently deciding that a half-human toddler was too small for her standards, literally. Or maybe she was just afraid she would accidentally sit on him. Fridwulfa went back to the giant colonies and eventually had another son, Hagrid's half-brother Grawp, who was considered a runt by giant standards and still stood 16 ft tall. Hagrid's father, whose name the wizarding world never bothered to record, was a tiny wizard with a massive heart who raised his son completely alone. He died during Hagrid's second year at Hogwarts, which meant that by 13 Hagrid had lost his father to death and his mother to indifference long before that. The kid who would eventually care for every dangerous creature on the planet started out the most dangerous looking kid on the playground himself. Gentle, oversized, and with nobody around to explain why the world felt three sizes too small. Albus Dumbledore. Albus Dumbledore spent his earliest years in Mould-on-the-Wold, a quiet village where the biggest excitement was supposed to be the garden gnomes. That changed fast. After his younger sister Ariana was attacked by Muggle boys who saw her doing magic, her powers turned unstable, dangerous to herself and everyone around her. Their father Percival retaliated, ended up in Azkaban, and the family name became unspeakable overnight. His mother Kendra moved the whole family to Godric's Hollow and locked the doors, figuratively and literally. Albus, the eldest of three, grew up in a house built on secrecy where his sister had to stay invisible. His father's name became a closed subject, and his younger brother Aberforth coped by getting angry, while Albus coped by getting brilliant. He read his way through every book in the house, mastered subjects years above his level, and was corresponding with the greatest magical minds of the era before he'd even started shaving. By 11, Albus was already the kind of kid who made adults uncomfortable, too smart, too composed, and carrying a household full of secrets behind a perfectly polite smile. Ron Weasley. Ron Weasley was the sixth of seven children, and by the time he showed up the Weasley family had already burned through most of its resources, patience, and cupboard space. His parents Arthur and Molly raised the entire operation at the Burrow, a house held together with magic, love, and the wizarding equivalent of duct tape, where absolutely nothing was new and everything had a previous owner. Ron's robes were hand-me-downs, his books were hand-me-downs, even his pet rat Scabbers was inherited from his older brother Percy, a deal that would eventually turn out spectacularly worse than anyone imagined. Bill and Charlie were too old and too far away to matter day-to-day. Percy, unfortunately, was right there and insufferable. Twins Fred and George treated Ron as a full-time test subject for every prank, hex, and terrible idea they ever cooked up. Basically, Ron arrived at Hogwarts already knowing two things with absolute certainty. He was surrounded by people who seemed better at everything, and he was permanently sick of corned beef sandwiches. Gross. Sirius Black. Sirius Black grew up at 12 Grimmauld Place, a gloomy London townhouse stuffed with cursed heirlooms, mounted house-elf heads, and a family tapestry that took up an entire wall. His parents, Orion and Walburga, raised Sirius and his younger brother Regulus in a house that ran on pure-blood obsession, dark artifacts, and Walburga's preferred parenting method, screaming. Family gatherings were worse. Sirius grew up sharing Sunday dinners with cousins like Bellatrix and Narcissa, meaning he was surrounded by future Death Eaters before he could ride a broomstick. His cousin Andromeda was the only relative who didn't make him want to leave the room, and she eventually got blasted off the family tapestry for marrying a Muggle-born, which told young Sirius everything he needed to know about what this family did to people who disagreed. Sirius hated all of it. He was loud, restless, and fundamentally allergic to everything Grimmauld Place stood for. He boarded the Hogwarts Express already itching to be anywhere else, and became the first Black in recorded history to be sorted into Gryffindor. Walburga's portrait is still screaming about it decades later, so imagine the live reaction. Severus Snape. Severus Snape grew up on Spinner's End, a grim little street near a river and a factory chimney, and you can see it in him. His father, Tobias, was a Muggle with a temper. His mother, Eileen Prince, was a witch who seemed to have given up on magic and most other things long before Severus came along. The house was tense, neglected, and Snape spent most of his time escaping it. Here's the real gossip. The one bright spot in that whole childhood had nothing to do with his parents. It was Lily Evans, a Muggle-born girl doing accidental magic in the local playground. Snape had been watching her for a while, which sounds unsettling because it slightly was, but he was also a lonely, badly dressed kid who'd finally found someone like him. Lily had no idea what she was. Snape recognized the signs, explained everything, the magic, the world behind it, the school they'd both end up at. And just like that, the loneliest kid on Spinner's End had his first real friend. They spent the years before Hogwarts together, talking about the wizarding world, about Hogwarts houses, about everything waiting for them once they turned 11. For a kid whose home life was miserable, Lily Evans was the entire reason the future looked like something worth getting to. Remus Lupin. Remus Lupin was 4 years old when Fenrir Greyback climbed through his bedroom window and bit him. The attack was personal. Remus's father, Lyall, a Ministry wizard, had publicly insulted werewolves during a hearing, and Greyback took it as an invitation. His mother, Hope, a Muggle, had no framework for what her child had become. Lyall, despite being the supposed expert on dark creatures, had none either. The Lupins moved constantly after that, never staying anywhere long enough for neighbors to notice their son's monthly disappearances. Every full moon was a crisis managed in secret, and every new town came with an expiration date built in. Hope and Lyall worked through every healer, rumor, and back-alley cure they could find. None of it worked. By the time Dumbledore personally showed up to offer Remus a place at Hogwarts, complete with a plan involving a secret passage in a haunted shack, the family had nearly given up on the idea that their son could have anything resembling a normal life. He arrived at school already fluent in the one subject Hogwarts never taught: how to disappear. James Potter. James Potter had the kind of childhood that makes everyone else's look underfunded. His parents, Fleamont and Euphemia, had him late in life. They'd already given up on children entirely when James arrived, which meant he was raised as the only child of two elderly, wealthy, completely besotted parents who treated him like a small miracle. Fleamont had made the family fortune by inventing Sleekeazy's Hair Potion, a product so successful it quadrupled the Potters' already comfortable gold reserves. And the Potters spent generously on their only son. James never wanted for anything, which is exactly how you end up with an 11-year-old who treats Hogwarts like his personal playground. His father also passed down the invisibility cloak before school, which is either a beautiful family tradition or the worst possible gift for a boy with zero respect for boundaries. The Sorting Hat put him in Gryffindor, and by the end of his first week, he'd already found Sirius Black, started a lifelong friendship, and made at least three enemies. Fred and George Weasley. Fred and George Weasley were born on April 1st, April Fools' Day, and the Wizarding World has been dealing with the consequences ever since. Third and fourth of Arthur and Molly's seven children, they were wedged between rule-obsessed Percy and long-suffering Ron, which gave them both a permanent target and a willing test subject. The twins were pulling pranks before they could read, had already mapped every hidden corner of the Burrow by age seven, and once tried to get their younger brother Ron to make an unbreakable vow, which would have been funny right up until the part where it killed him. Their father, Arthur, walked in just in time and was reportedly furious. One of the rare moments in Weasley history where Arthur genuinely lost his temper. Classic Weasley chaos. Two kids who turned troublemaking into a vocation before their letters even arrived. Hermione Granger. Hermione Granger read the entire syllabus before the first day of school, and that was just primary school. She was the kid who corrected teachers mid-sentence, finished tests first, and spent every break alone with a book because the other kids had decided she was insufferable years ago. Her parents, both dentists, had zero connection to the magical world, which meant that when her Hogwarts letter arrived, the whole family was starting from scratch. Most Muggle-born kids would panic. Hermione went book shopping. She bought every title on the recommended list and extras, then read them all before September. By the time she boarded the Hogwarts Express, she could recite Hogwarts A History from memory and casually correct people on wand movement she'd only read about in theory. Her parents, meanwhile, were reportedly proud and mildly terrified, which feels like the correct response when your 11-year-old announces she's leaving for a castle to study spells. Minerva McGonagall. Minerva McGonagall's childhood came with a built-in lesson about secrets, courtesy of her own mother. Her father, Robert, was a Muggle, a Presbyterian minister in Scottish Highlands. Her mother, Isobel Ross, was a witch who fell in love, married him, and then spent years hiding her magic because she couldn't bring herself to tell her husband the truth. Quick note, Isobel only revealed herself after baby Minerva started doing things no minister's daughter should do, summoning toys, making objects rearrange themselves, the usual signs. Robert took the news about as well as you'd expect. The marriage held, but the warmth drained out of it. Minerva grew up in a house where magic was treated as a problem to be managed. Her mother's quiet sadness colored every room, and the cost of loving a Muggle shown up over breakfast. By the time her Hogwarts letter arrived, Minerva had already absorbed the principle that would define her entire career, never hide what you are, and never let anyone else feel they have to. Tom Riddle. Tom Riddle was born in London orphanage on New Year's Eve 1926, and things went downhill from there. His mother, Merope Gaunt, gave birth, named him, and died within the hour, leaving behind a baby and absolutely nothing else. His father was a wealthy Muggle from Little Hangleton who'd been kept under a love potion by Merope and walked away the moment it wore off. Translation, Tom started life rejected by every branch of his own family tree. The orphanage staff figured out early that something was wrong. Young Tom could move objects without touching them, hurt other children when provoked, command animals, and had a particular habit of collecting stolen trophies from kids he'd frightened, hidden in his wardrobe like souvenirs. He once terrified two orphans in a seaside cave during a day trip, and the matron spoke about him with visible unease to anyone who asked. By the time Dumbledore showed up with his Hogwarts letter, Tom was 11 years old and already the scariest person in the building. Neville Longbottom. Neville Longbottom's story usually starts with Bellatrix Lestrange, but before that, there was actually something worth losing. He was born to two of the most respected Aurors in the Ministry, Frank and Alice Longbottom. And for a brief shining window, everything was exactly as it should have been. A healthy baby, a loving family, two parents who'd already proven they could survive the worst the wizarding world had to offer. Then the worst came for them anyway. Shortly after Voldemort's first fall, Bellatrix Lestrange, her husband Rodolphus, his brother Rabastan, and Barty Crouch Jr. tortured Frank and Alice into permanent insanity. Neville was a toddler. He was raised by his paternal grandmother Augusta, a formidable witch with a vulture top hat and zero tolerance for weakness, which was tricky because Neville's childhood showed almost no signs of magic at all. The family was quietly terrified he might be a Squib. He visited his parents at St. Mungo's regularly, though neither of them could recognize him. Neville arrived at Hogwarts with his father's old wand, a toad named Trevor that kept escaping, and more courage than he'd ever given himself credit for. Draco Malfoy. Draco was raised at Malfoy Manor by his father Lucius, a former Death Eater with political connections, old money and the bearing of a man who'd never once carried his own bags. And his mother, Narcissa née Black, which meant Draco inherited pureblood ideology from both sides with no competing influences. The household ran on status, appearances, and a very clear ranking system of who mattered. Lucius taught Draco to see the world as a hierarchy and to make sure everyone knew the Malfoys were at the top of it. Narcissa, meanwhile, just wanted her son safe, which produced a kid caught between two very different kinds of love, one that demanded performance and one that demanded nothing. Draco learned to sneer, to rank people by bloodline, to parrot his father's opinions so early it all looked natural. But the cruelty was always borrowed, never really his. By the time Draco boarded the Hogwarts Express, he was already name-dropping his father to strangers, sneering at secondhand robes, and sorting people into categories by bloodline. 11 years old and already a fully operational Malfoy. Expensive and entitled and deeply convinced that the world owed him priority seating. Luna Lovegood. Luna Lovegood grew up in a house near Ottery St. Catchpole that looked like it could topple over at any moment, and honestly, it probably could. Her father, Xenophilius, editor of The Quibbler, raised her surrounded by dirigible plum bushes, butterbeer cork wind chimes, and a magazine dedicated to creatures and conspiracies that mainstream wizards politely pretended were nonsense. Her mother, Pandora, was a brilliant experimental witch who let Luna help in the kitchen, encouraged every impossible question, and made the Lovegood household feel like the one place where being strange was a compliment. Then, when Luna was nine, Pandora pushed a spell too far and something went catastrophically wrong. Luna saw the whole thing. Xenophilius raised Luna alone after that in the same peculiar house with the same impossible conversations, except now grief lived quietly underneath all the wonder. Luna arrived at Hogwarts, gentle, strange, painfully sincere, and entirely immune to peer pressure. Partly because an Affilias raised her to question everything, and partly because she'd already survived the worst thing a child can survive. Other kids called her Looney. She never seemed to mind, which somehow made it sadder. Ginny Weasley, the only daughter and youngest of Arthur and Molly's seven children. Ginny grew up at the Burrow, outnumbered, underestimated, and completely unfazed by both. With Bill, Charlie, Percy, Fred, George, and Ron already filling every room in the house, she learned early that the only way to get heard was to be louder, faster, or more stubborn than all of them. Her brothers taught her to fly in secret. Molly would have lost her mind, and Ginny had been sneaking into the broom shed since she was six, borrowing brooms that technically belonged to people who didn't know she could ride. She also grew up hearing stories about Harry Potter, the boy who survived Voldemort, and developed a mortifying, fully debilitating hero crush long before she'd ever met him. Well, spoiler, she eventually married him, making her the only person in Hogwarts whose childhood fantasy held up to reality. Ginny arrived at Hogwarts already capable of a solid bat-bogey hex, and barely able to string a sentence together around Harry. The hex held up better. Harry Potter, the future husband of Ginny Weasley, spent the first 10 years of his life in a cupboard under the stairs at number four Privet Drive, wearing his cousin Dudley's oversized hand-me-downs and believing his parents had died in a car crash. His aunt Petunia, his mother Lily's older sister, and her husband Vernon raised Harry the way you'd raise an inconvenient houseguest you couldn't legally return. Their actual son Dudley got two bedrooms, mountains of birthday presents, way too much food, and full permission to use Harry as target practice. Harry got chores, leftovers, and a thorough education in what it feels like to be unwanted. Strange things kept happening around him. Hair that grew back overnight, a teacher's wig turning blue, somehow landing on a school rooftop, but nobody ever explained any of it. And asking questions was actively discouraged. Meanwhile, the entire visiting world knew everything about him. The name, the scar, the parents who died for him. The Dursleys spent weeks destroying letters, boarding up the mail slot, and eventually fleeing to a shack on the rock in the sea. All to stop an 11-year-old from finding out that he was the most famous wizard of his generation. It didn't work. Now, you know what shaped every witch and wizard before Hogwarts. But, what happened to them after the Deathly Hallows? That's your next lesson here at Stickman Hogwarts. Click now before Umbridge finds a reason to give you all detention for loitering.


=== All 10 MAJOR Events You Missed Before Harry Potter EXPLAINED in Detail ===
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=DS_M9lQYsFw

The witch hunts. By the time Harry arrived to the wizarding world, Muggles and wizards are living in completely separate worlds, but it wasn't always like that. Let's go back to the Middle Ages. Magic was everywhere. Witches healed the sick. Wizards predicted the harvest, and nobody particularly hid what they were doing. But the moment things went wrong, a plague, an unexplained death, a child who didn't survive the winter, people needed someone to blame, and the neighbors who could do things nobody else could were an obvious target. Magic went from useful to suspicious almost overnight. Before long, a black cat or a whispered rumor was enough to start an accusation, and accusations had a way of ending at the stake. The thinking being that only flames could purify a soul corrupted by dark magic. And so, the hunt began around 1450. They did catch real witches from time to time, which sounds terrifying until you learn about the flame-freezing charm. Any competent witch could cast it and sit in the fire like it was a warm bath. Wendelin the Weird enjoyed the sensation so much that she let herself be caught 47 times in different disguises. 47. Centuries later, Harry Potter would study these witch burnings in History of Magic. Because Hogwarts had a remarkable talent for turning public executions into homework. The people who actually died were mostly Muggles accused by their own neighbors over a dead cow or a bad winter. Real witches watched from a safe distance and reached an obvious conclusion. It was time to disappear. International Statute of Secrecy. So, witches and wizards watched Muggles light bonfires across Europe and came to a groundbreaking realization. Maybe living next door to people who want you dead is not a long-term plan. It took years of negotiations between wizarding governments across the world to agree on one thing. Hiding was the only option left. In 1689, the International Statute of Wizarding Secrecy became law. It was a binding agreement signed by magical governments across the world, making it a crime for any witch or wizard to allow a Muggle to learn of their existence. In practical terms, no more magic in public, no more magical communities on Muggle maps, and no exceptions. The entire magical world vanished from Muggle sight almost overnight. Hogwarts became invisible, Diagon Alley hid behind a pub wall, entire villages folded off the map. Every witch and wizard became personally responsible for keeping magic hidden. Accidental exposure meant Ministry hearings, memory modification squads, and a lot of paperwork. The Ministry spent decades relocating dragons and convincing Muggles that certain lake monsters were just weather patterns. Platform 9 and 3/4, the Leaky Cauldron, the Quidditch World Cup, all of it exists because of this one law. But while wizards were busy hiding from Muggles, they missed a growing problem on their own side of the wall, the Goblin Rebellions. Goblins and wizards have never gotten along. This is not an exaggeration. Starting in the 17th century and continuing well into the 18th, goblins launched a series of full-scale rebellions against wizarding authority. The core issues were simple. Goblins wanted the same rights as wizards: wand use, political representation, basic legal protections. Wizards said, "No." Goblins asked again. Wizards said, "No." louder. Goblins picked up weapons. Professor Binns turned it into the most boring lecture series in Hogwarts history, which is actually an achievement given the competition. The rebellions had specific names and varying levels of chaos. The 1612 uprising centered around Hogsmeade. Local inns turned into wizard defense headquarters. The 1752 rebellion, led partly by Urg the Unclean, got bad enough that two Ministers for Magic resigned, Albert Boot and Basil Flack, and somehow also destroyed the wizarding milling industry with werewolves involved for good measure. The broader 18th century saw constant uprisings over wand rights and goblin ownership laws. The last major one came in 1890 led by Ranrok who tried to weaponize ancient magic. But the rebellions left scars that never healed. Goblin culture developed an entirely different concept of ownership. To a goblin, the maker of an object is its true owner and selling it is closer to lending. Wizards never understood this, never tried to, and then acted surprised every time a goblin brought it up. The tension at Gringotts, the suspicion, the barely concealed hostility Bill Weasley spent years navigating, all of it traces back to these wars. Basically, the wizarding world fought an entire species over equal rights. One wrote the textbook and then got offended when goblins didn't say thank you. The Triwizard Tournament. In 1294, the three largest wizarding schools in Europe, Hogwarts, Beauxbatons, and Durmstrang, looked at each other and thought, "What if we made teenagers fight things?" The Triwizard Tournament was born and it was exactly as responsible as it sounds. The concept was straightforward. One champion from each school, three deadly tasks, eternal glory for the winner. The tasks involved dragons, underwater hostage situations, and enchanted mazes full of creatures that could kill you in creatively different ways. Judges scored, adults supervised the whole thing, and presumably kept a straight face while a 17-year-old wrestled something with fangs. Students died repeatedly. Over the centuries, the death toll climbed high enough that the tournament was eventually discontinued and stayed dead for roughly 200 years. Then in 1994, the Ministry of Magic brought it back. New safety measures, an age restriction, an impartial magical judge to select champions. Completely foolproof. A 14-year-old got entered anyway, one champion died, and Voldemort used the whole event to come back from the dead. Resounding success. The tournament was canceled again after that, presumably for good this time. Though, with the wizarding world's track record, who knows? The death of Ariana Dumbledore. This moment is important because what happened in Godric's Hollow that summer would haunt Dumbledore for over a century and cost him his life. So, Dumbledore was 18 years old, freshly graduated, brilliant beyond anything Hogwarts had seen in a generation, and completely stuck. His mother, Kendra, had just died, leaving him responsible for his younger sister, Ariana, a girl whose magic had been shattered years earlier when three Muggle boys attacked her for doing magic in the garden. She was six. The attack broke something inside her. Her magic turned inward, unstable, uncontrollable, dangerous. Kendra had kept her hidden from the world, and now that job fell to Albus. He resented it. He was supposed to travel, to achieve things, to become the greatest wizard of his age. Instead, he was stuck at Godric's Hollow changing bed sheets and watching his future shrink. Then, Gellert Grindelwald showed up next door, and everything got worse. Grindelwald was charming, ambitious, and had ideas about wizards ruling over Muggles for the greater good. Dumbledore fell for the vision and fell for Grindelwald himself. The two of them spent a summer planning a revolution while Ariana sat in the next room. Aberforth, Albus's younger brother, eventually confronted them both. A three-way duel broke out. A stray curse hit Ariana, and she died right there in the room. Nobody ever found out whose wand it came from. Dumbledore carried that guilt for over a century. It shaped every major decision he ever made. His refusal to take power and his obsession with protecting students. The old man Harry trusted with his life was built on this one afternoon in Godric's Hollow. Grindelwald's rise and fall. After the disaster in Godric's Hollow, Grindelwald disappeared from Britain and did what ambitious dark wizards do. He went continental. Over the next few decades, he built an army, accumulated followers across Europe, and started a campaign to end the Statute of Secrecy and establish wizard dominance over Muggles. His slogan was for the greater good, which he had workshopped during that one summer with Dumbledore. Grindelwald was terrifyingly good at what he did. He was charismatic, strategic, and unlike Voldemort, he had a nose, meaning he could actually make people believe they were doing the right thing. His rise coincided with the 1930s and 40s, which means the Muggle and wizarding worlds were both falling apart at roughly the same time. And through all of it, Dumbledore did nothing. The most powerful wizard alive sat at Hogwarts teaching Transfiguration while Europe burned because he was terrified of one thing, finding out whose curse killed Ariana. Facing Grindelwald meant facing that answer. Fantastic Beasts fans will point out, correctly, that there was also a blood pact involved. A literal vow they made as starry-eyed teenagers that stopped either of them from attacking the other. The books don't mention it, so make of that what you will. The point stands either way. Dumbledore had reasons not to show up, and he leaned into all of them. He finally did it in 1945. The duel is legendary, reportedly the greatest wizarding duel ever fought. Dumbledore won, took the Elder Wand, and locked Grindelwald in his own prison, Nurmengard, under the sign he had built himself, for the greater good. Staring at those words for the rest of his life. Funny how things work. Voldemort's rise and the First Wizarding War. Grindelwald was barely locked up when the next one showed up. Tom Riddle graduated from Hogwarts in 1945. Handsome, brilliant, head boy, and already a murderer. He had killed his own father and grandparents before finishing school. Casually, before the ink on his diploma was dry, Riddle vanished for about a decade after graduation, traveled, experimented with the darkest magic in existence, and kept splitting his soul into Horcruxes, a hobby he had picked up while still at school, because apparently some people start early. By the time he resurfaced as Lord Voldemort, he was barely recognizable. The charm was gone, the looks were gone, and the nose was on its way out. He built his following slowly and carefully. Before they were the Death Eaters, they called themselves the Knights of Walpurgis, named after April 30th, the traditional witches' night, basically the dark calendar's answer to Halloween. They started as a circle of old Hogwarts classmates from wealthy pureblood families, people who already believed they were better than everyone else, and just needed someone to say it louder. Voldemort said it louder. He promised a world where pureblood wizards ruled, Muggle-borns were eliminated, and anyone who disagreed disappeared. And for a while, it worked. The First Wizarding War lasted roughly 11 years, and it was brutal. People vanished without a trace. Entire families were wiped off the map. The Ministry was infiltrated so deeply that nobody knew who to trust. Muggle-borns were being picked off, and the government was too scared or too compromised to stop it. Voldemort wanted fear even more than power, and fear is contagious. By the late 1970s, Wizarding Britain was falling apart. The Ministry was losing. The Death Eaters were everywhere, and one man decided the answer wasn't politics or policy, but a secret group of volunteers willing to die for something better. The original Order of the Phoenix. Dumbledore looked at the Ministry failing, looked at the Death Eaters winning, and did what he always did, built something himself. The Order of the Phoenix was a secret resistance group operating completely outside the government, which tells you exactly how much faith Dumbledore had in official channels. He recruited personally, handpicking witches and wizards he trusted, Aurors, civilians, even people the Ministry would never have considered useful. They ran surveillance on known Death Eaters, protected Muggle-born families, guarded key targets, and fought in engagements the Daily Prophet never reported. The Ministry had resources, had numbers, had an entire bureaucratic apparatus behind it, and was still losing the war. The Order operated on trust in an era where trust could get you killed. Voldemort had spies everywhere, and the paranoia was suffocating. You couldn't be sure the person sitting next to you at a meeting wasn't passing information to the other side. Dumbledore held the whole thing together through sheer force of conviction. Without him, the Order falls apart, the resistance disappears, and the Ministry just keeps hoping the problem will go away on its own. It cost them enormously. Entire families were wiped out. Molly Weasley lost both her brothers. Frank and Alice Longbottom were tortured into insanity after the war ended. The Order held the line for years, but none of them knew that the turning point had already happened. The prophecy. Sybill Trelawney walked into the Hog's Head for a job interview, and in the middle of being politely rejected by Dumbledore, delivered the most important prediction in wizarding history. She didn't even remember it afterward. The universe has a sense of humor. A child born at the end of July to parents who had defied Voldemort three times would have the power to destroy him. Two boys fit the description, Harry Potter and Neville Longbottom. Voldemort heard about it, picked Harry, and in doing so, made the prophecy real. The most feared dark wizard of all time created his own worst enemy because he heard a rumor and panicked. And the reason he heard it at all, Severus Snape, young, ambitious, desperate to climb the Death Eater ranks, eavesdropping on the interview from behind a door. He caught the first half of the prophecy and ran straight to Voldemort with it, thinking it would earn him favor. It earned him something else entirely. Voldemort chose the Potters, which meant Lily Potter, which meant the only person Snape had ever loved was now a target because of him. A single overheard conversation in a pub and everything moved toward Halloween 1981. How Voldemort met Nagini. We all know the story. Voldemort walked into the Potters' house, James and Lily were gone in minutes and then lost to a one-year-old. Classic Voldy. The wizarding world erupted into celebration, fireworks, parties, relief that had been building for over a decade. Everyone assumed it was over, but Voldemort had spent years making himself impossible to fully destroy, splitting his soul into Horcruxes and hiding the pieces in objects scattered across the world. So, he was still around, but formless, noseless, barely conscious, something between alive and gone. And that's when Nagini enters the story. After that night, he drifted east into the forests of Albania and that's where Nagini was. She appears as a young woman, a Maledictus, someone carrying a blood curse that would eventually trap her permanently in the form of a snake. By the time Voldemort found her in those forests, the transformation was complete. Whether he found her or she found him is unclear, but Nagini stayed. She became his closest companion through those years in hiding and later, once he had clawed back enough power, he turned her into a Horcrux, a vessel for a piece of his soul. He kept her closer than any living thing he had ever trusted. The Dark Lord who terrorized an entire country spent 13 years as a ghost in a forest and came back with a snake that used to be a woman. Voldemort's version of a comeback story. Sirius framed. So, the war was over and Voldemort was gone. The wizarding world was celebrating and while everyone was popping champagne corks and shooting sparks into the sky, Sirius Black's life was ending in a completely different way. Peter Pettigrew, the Potters' Secret Keeper, the man who had actually handed them to Voldemort, knew he was finished the moment the Dark Lord fell. Sirius knew it, too, and went after him. He cornered Pettigrew on a Muggle street in broad daylight and that's where Pettigrew did the most cunning thing he had ever done in his pathetic rat life. He screamed that Sirius had betrayed the Potters, blew up the street, killed 12 Muggles in the explosion, cut off his own finger, transformed into a rat, and vanished into a sewer, all in about 6 seconds. Sirius was standing in the middle of the wreckage surrounded by dead Muggles laughing. The absurdity of it had broken something in him. The person everyone thought was too stupid to be dangerous had just outplayed him completely. The Ministry didn't investigate. Sirius Black went straight to Azkaban without a trial, and the Wizarding world moved on, satisfied that justice had been served. 12 years surrounded by Dementors for a crime committed by a rat hiding in a child's bedroom. Pettigrew spent those 12 years as Scabbers, the Weasley family pet, sleeping in Ron's pocket, eating scraps off the table, waiting. The real traitor was living in the same dormitory as Harry Potter, and nobody, not Dumbledore, not the Ministry, not the Marauder's Map collecting dust in Filch's office, caught it. Now, let's talk about what happened to every wizard after the Deathly Hallows explained. That's your next lesson here at Stickman Hogwarts. Watch now before Pettigrew crawls back into someone else's pocket.


=== All 21 NEW Wizards and Witches You Missed After The Deathly Hallows EXPLAINED in Detail ===
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=z_WULuS3qs0

Albus Severus Potter. Albus Severus Potter was born in 2006, the second son of Harry Potter, the savior of the wizarding world, and Ginny Weasley, his mother. At age four, he nearly drowned after falling off a boat and was saved by his father, Harry, which for a man who died and came back to life was just another day, nothing special. Named after two Hogwarts headmasters, Albus Dumbledore, his father's mentor and the greatest headmaster the school ever had, and Severus Snape, the man his father Harry spent six years hating before realizing he'd been protecting him the whole time. Deeply moving. Also, kind of a lot to put on a birth certificate. Then Hogwarts happened. Sorted into Slytherin, the first Potter in living memory, alongside his best friend Scorpius Malfoy, son of his father's old school rival Draco Malfoy. And unlike every Potter before him, Albus couldn't fly. His broomstick ignored him on the very first lesson, which earned him the nickname Slytherin Squib from his classmates. Charming lot. And if you've somehow forgiven the Cursed Child for existing, you know that his relationship with his father, Harry, was genuinely painful. Albus felt crushed under the weight of expectations he never asked for and a legacy he couldn't live up to. He wasn't the chosen one, the kid who came after one and couldn't get a broom off the ground. The arc, honestly. Scorpius Malfoy. Born in 2006, he's the son of Draco Malfoy and Astoria Greengrass. Astoria raised her son to respect Muggles, which put her at constant odds with his grandparents, Lucius and Narcissa Malfoy, who very much did not share that opinion. Imagine growing up with that dinner table conversation. Then his mother, Astoria, died from a hereditary blood curse. He and his father, Draco, grieved together. Who knew the Malfoy men were capable of something other than cruelty? In 2017, he arrived at Hogwarts and was sorted into Slytherin, where, as you already know, his best friend ended up being Albus Severus Potter. They They on the Hogwarts Express, sharing a compartment despite protests from Rose Granger-Weasley, his future crush and academic rival. Because of course his best friend was a Potter and his crush was a Granger-Weasley. The universe has a sense of humor. Unlike his father Draco at the same age, Scorpius turned out to be a genuinely kind, bookish, slightly awkward kid. Brilliant academically, Draco apparently instructed him to beat Rose on every test, which is the most Draco parenting advice imaginable. Then the rumors started. Half the wizarding world convinced themselves Scorpius was secretly Voldemort's son, conceived via Time-Turner because Astoria supposedly couldn't have children naturally. No evidence, but it didn't matter. The whispers followed him through every corridor at Hogwarts. Being a Malfoy was complicated enough without that. Rose Granger-Weasley. Rose Emeline Granger-Weasley was born in 2006, the first child of Ron and Hermione Weasley, two-thirds of the golden trio that defeated Voldemort. In 2017, she arrived at Hogwarts and was sorted into Gryffindor, exactly like both her parents. No surprises there. Academically, she's her mother Hermione's daughter through and through. Brilliant, driven, and apparently already stressed out about her O.W.L. exam schedule before she'd finished unpacking her trunk. Ron, her father, sent her off to school with one specific instruction, beat Scorpius Malfoy on every test. Because apparently academic rivalry is a Weasley love language. She's also a gifted Quidditch player, made Gryffindor Chaser in her second year, with McGonagall herself describing her flying as outstanding. So yes, Hermione's brains, presumably Ron's athletic genes, finally showing up when needed. As you already know, she wanted nothing to do with Scorpius Malfoy initially, deeply prejudiced against him because of his family name and, you know, the Voldemort stuff. Cold, sharp, and entirely convinced she was right. Classic. Fun fact, Rose and Scorpius are actually fourth cousins, both descendants of Phineas Nigellus Black, their shared ancestor. She rejected a family member, technically, James Sirius Potter. James Sirius Potter was born on the 26th of March, 2004, the firstborn of Harry and Ginny Potter, named after two legends, his parental grandfather, James Potter I, and his father's godfather, Sirius Black III. Big shoes to fill. Sorted into Gryffindor in 2015, obviously, he spent his Hogwarts years collecting achievements like they were chocolate frog cards. Quidditch captain, perfect. Head boy, all N.E.W.T.s passed. His father, Harry, noted that everything comes easily to James, which is a perfectly lovely thing to say about your eldest son, and an absolutely devastating thing for his younger brother, Albus, to overhear.
