After Hogwarts, he went professional, playing Chaser for Puddlemere United. In 2027, he married Grace Ollivander, granddaughter of legendary wandmaker, Garrick Ollivander. They had four children, sons John and Filmont, called Monty. Cute. And daughters Euphemia and Jude. Lily Luna Potter. Lily Luna Potter was born in 2008, the youngest child of Harry and Ginny Potter, her parents, and the only daughter, named after two women her father, Harry, never stopped missing. Her parental grandmother, Lily Evans, and Luna Lovegood, her parents' close friend and one of the most reliably strange people in the wizarding world. A beautiful name, a lot of history behind it. She arrived at Hogwarts in 2019 and was sorted into Gryffindor, continuing a family tradition so consistent it was basically a formality at this point. Like most Potters, she took to Quidditch naturally, playing Chaser for Gryffindor, a skill she owed largely to her older brother, James Sirius, who taught her to fly, which made them unusually close, even by Potter standards. In adulthood, he'd regularly choose her as Quidditch partner, which is either very sweet or very competitive, probably both. She went professional after Hogwarts, though eventually stepped away from the sport. Lily also desperately wanted Teddy Lupin, her father Harry's godson, to officially join the family by marrying her cousin Victoire Weasley. She got her wish. Teddy Lupin, the boy who lost everything and still turned out fine. Edward Remus Teddy Lupin was born in April 1998, the only child of Remus Lupin and Nymphadora Tonks. Both his parents were killed at the Battle of Hogwarts. His mother by her own aunt Bellatrix Lestrange, his father by Death Eater Antonin Dolohov. Teddy was weeks old. He was raised by his maternal grandmother Andromeda Tonks. She took her grandson in without hesitation, raised him alone, and gave him the kind of stable loving home that nothing in her bloodline suggested she'd ever build. His godfather Harry Potter stopped by for dinner roughly four times a week because Harry wasn't going to let another kid grow up feeling like no one wanted him. Between Andromeda's resilience and Harry's stubbornness, Teddy never stood a chance at turning out badly. Teddy inherited his mother's Metamorphmagus abilities. He could change his appearance at will, and his hair apparently went from black to ginger within the first hour of being born. His signature color in later years was turquoise. He did not, however, inherit his father Remus's lycanthropy, despite what Rita Skeeter loved to imply. He was sorted into Hufflepuff at Hogwarts, same house as his mother, and became Head Boy in his seventh year, the only known Hufflepuff Head Boy in canon. By 2017, he was dating Victoire Weasley, daughter of Bill and Fleur. His cousin James Sirius Potter caught them snogging on platform 9 and 3/4 and immediately told everyone because of course he did. He's James Sirius Potter. Lorcan Scamander. Lorcan Scamander, one of the twin sons born to Luna Lovegood and her husband Rolf Scamander, grandson of the legendary magizoologist Newt Scamander. His name means little fierce one in Irish, which for a kid raised by Luna Lovegood is either wildly accurate or deeply ironic. Luna married Rolf considerably later than Harry and Ginny or Ron and Hermione according to Rowling, though definitely before 2014 because at that point the twins already existed. Through his mother Luna, Lorcan is also a descendant of the Ollivander family. His maternal grandmother Pandora was born an Ollivander. So his bloodline runs from the greatest wandmaker dynasty in Britain to the greatest magizoologist in history. No pressure. The story goes Lorcan was sorted into Hufflepuff and played Beater for his house team. Whether he inherited his mother's dreamy calm or his great-grandfather Newt's talent for chasing dangerous creatures into confined spaces remains undocumented. Hugo Granger Weasley. Hugo Granger Weasley was born around 2008, the second child and only son of Ron and Hermione Weasley. He inherited his mother Hermione's bushy hair, which in a sea of Weasley redheads made him stand out immediately at every family gathering. His name was likely chosen by Hermione, possibly after French novelist Victor Hugo, whose work she loved, which means Ron's contribution to the naming process was almost certainly "Sounds good to me." Growing up, Hugo's closest companion was his cousin Lily Luna Potter. The two were the same age and apparently inseparable. Here's the strange part. Hugo is completely absent from The Cursed Child. His sister Rose is there, his cousin Lily is there. Hugo, who should have been starting Hogwarts at the same time, simply didn't appear. Hugo got to grow up quietly, the most normal kid in the most abnormal family in Wizarding Britain. Delphini. Voldemort had a daughter. Yeah, Cursed Child fans, this is your moment. Delphini was born in March 1998 at Malfoy Manor, the secret child of Lord Voldemort and Bellatrix Lestrange, his most devoted follower. Both parents died weeks later at the Battle of Hogwarts, leaving her an orphan before she could crawl. She was raised by Euphemia Rowle, a former Death Eater who took her in not out of love, but because she was paid to. Delphini never attended Hogwarts, never received a proper magical education, and grew up knowing almost nothing about her parents, which didn't stop her from becoming terrifyingly powerful. She could cast unforgivable curses, speak Parseltongue like her father, and duel adult Harry Potter and Hermione Granger simultaneously without flinching. She infiltrated the Wizarding World by posing as the niece of Amos Diggory, manipulated Albus Potter and Scorpius Malfoy into stealing a Time Turner, and attempted to rewrite history so her father would survive. She failed. Harry, Draco, Hermione, Ron, and Ginny stopped her. In the end, she begged them to kill her rather than face a life without the father she never knew. Sent to Azkaban instead, the daughter of the Dark Lord ending up in the same prison that once held her mother. Make of that what you will. Victoire Weasley, born the 2nd of May, 2000, the eldest child of Bill Weasley and Fleur Delacour. Her birthday falls on the anniversary of the Battle of Hogwarts, the day the Second Wizarding War ended, which is why her parents named her Victoire, French for victory. Her mother Fleur was never going to be subtle about it. Her mother Fleur is part Veela, a descendant of magical creatures whose beauty is literally supernatural, the kind that makes grown wizards walk into walls and forget their own names. Victoire inherited that bloodline and was described as blond and beautiful, which given the genetic lottery she won is about as surprising as a Weasley being sorted into Gryffindor. At the 2014 Quidditch World Cup, Rita Skeeter dedicated an entire section of her article to Victoire and Teddy Lupin, finding dark corners together instead of watching the match. Her father Bill eventually swapped seats with her and pointed her at the pitch. She sulked about it. By 2017, she and Teddy were still together, and Lily Luna Potter, her cousin, was already campaigning for a wedding, so Teddy would officially become part of the family. Fred Weasley the Second. Fred Weasley the second was born in 2008, the eldest child of George Weasley and Angelina Johnson. Named after his late uncle Fred, George's twin brother who died laughing during the Battle of Hogwarts. Because George wasn't going to let that name disappear. In 2019, he arrived at Hogwarts and was sorted into Gryffindor, same house as both his parents. He took up Quidditch and played Beater, the exact same position his father George and uncle Fred held on the Gryffindor team back in the late 1990s. Oliver Wood once called the original twins unbeatable. Fred the second apparently took that as a personal challenge. There's something about a kid growing up above Weasleys' Wizard Wheezes. Fred the second inherited the name, the house, and the position on the pitch. Whether he also inherited the talent for chaos, nobody's confirmed that yet. Given the gene pool, the answer is obvious. Roxanne Weasley. Roxanne Weasley was born in the mid-2000s, the younger child and only daughter of George Weasley and Angelina Johnson, and the sister of Fred Weasley the second. Her name comes from Persian and means dawn, which is either a coincidence or Angelina's way of saying the grieving chapter was over. Given her parents, one a Gryffindor Quidditch star, the other a professional troublemaker, the odds of Roxanne being boring are statistically impossible. Her mother Angelina played Chaser for Gryffindor during the original series. Her father George played Beater. Between them, Roxanne grew up with more Quidditch DNA than most professional players are born with. Rumor has it she ended up on the Gryffindor team because with that gene pool, staying off a broomstick would have been the real achievement. Lysander Scamander. Lysander Scamander, twin brother of Lorcan and the other half of Luna Lovegood's contribution to the next generation. His name comes from Greek and means liberator of mankind. No pressure for a boy who presumably spent his childhood chasing Nifflers through somebody's garden. Lysander and Lorcan didn't grow up in a normal household. Both their parents, Luna and her husband Rolf Scamander, grandson of Newt, were working magizoologists who spent years traveling the globe together discovering and classifying species nobody had ever documented before. So, these two boys were raised somewhere between field expeditions, magical creature reserves, and a grandfather's house. At the 2014 Quidditch World Cup, Luna left the twins at home with Grandpa. Rita Skeeter being, well, Rita Skeeter used this as an opportunity to speculate that the twins were too disturbed to be seen in public because apparently being raised by Luna Lovegood and the grandson of Newt's commander means you're automatically unhinged. Rita has never met a private family she couldn't turn into a headline. Molly Weasley the Second. Molly Weasley the Second, the eldest daughter of Percy Weasley and his wife Audrey, born in 2000, named after her grandmother Molly Weasley, the woman who killed Bellatrix Lestrange in a single combat and held the entire Weasley family together through two wars, seven children, and an undetermined number of Howlers. Naming your daughter after that woman is either a tribute or a challenge. Knowing Percy, it was both. She arrived at Hogwarts in 2011 and word is she was sorted into Ravenclaw, breaking the Weasley family's centuries-long Gryffindor streak. Percy's daughter choosing brains over bravery is the most Percy outcome imaginable. The man once wrote a report on cauldron bottom thickness for fun. Ravenclaw was always in the bloodline. It just needed someone to finally admit it. Lucy Weasley. Lucy Weasley, the youngest daughter of Percy Weasley and Audrey, born in 2005. Her father is the Weasley who once chose a Ministry career over his own family and spent the rest of his life making up for it. Her mother Audrey remains one of the most mysterious spouses in the entire clan. Lucy arrived at Hogwarts in 2016 and was sorted into Gryffindor, continuing the family tradition her father nearly abandoned. Word is she eventually married into the surname McGonagall. Whether that means what you think it means, nobody he confirmed. But the idea of a Weasley becoming a McGonagall is the kind of crossover event that the Wizarding World didn't know it needed. Dominique Weasley. Dominique Weasley is the middle child of Bill Weasley and Fleur Delacour, wedged between the family's golden eldest Victoire and the baby of the group, Louis. French name, part Veela blood. Here's an interesting detail. The name Dominique is actually unisex in French. Canon never spells out whether Dominique is a witch or a wizard. Most fans read her as Bill and Fleur's second daughter. Though the text itself leaves it open, deliberately or accidentally, hard to say. Word is she may have inherited her mother Fleur's looks and her father Bill's taste for adventure. Where that combination took her remains a mystery. The least documented kid in the flashiest branch of the Weasley family. Louis Weasley. Louis Weasley, the youngest child of Bill Weasley and Fleur Delacour and their only son. Here's the thing about Louis. His grandmother on Fleur's side was a full Veela, a magical creature whose beauty and charm are practically weaponized. That bloodline has passed through Fleur, through Victoire and Dominique, and now through a boy. Canon has never really addressed what Veela ancestry looks like on a grandson. Whether Louis got the supernatural good looks, the fiery temper, or just a very confused mirror. Word has it it's all of the above. He reportedly settled somewhere along the Cornish coast after Hogwarts, not far from Shell Cottage, where he grew up and apparently inherited his father's talent for staying out of the spotlight. For a kid with Veela blood and a legendary surname, keeping a low profile like that takes a real commitment. The Hogwarts extras. Yann Fredericks, Craig Bowker Jr., Karl Jenkins, and Polly Chapman. In The Cursed Child, Albus Potter and Scorpius Malfoy didn't exactly have a smooth Hogwarts experience. These four classmates were the background cast making sure of it. Yann Fredericks hung out with Rose Granger-Weasley and was apparently good at flying, which given that Albus couldn't get a broom off the ground must have been delightful to witness. Karl Jenkins was the charming individual who coined the nickname Slytherin Squib for Albus and enjoyed spreading rumors about Scorpius being Voldemort's son. Outstanding contribution to school culture. Polly Chapman rounded out the trio by joining the gossip train and picking the popular side every single time. The social equivalent of always betting on the winning Quidditch team after the match is over. And then there was Craig Bowker Jr. Craig was a Slytherin, a stickler for rules, once described simply as a good boy. He didn't go down fighting. He was simply there when Delphini needed someone gone. A good kid in the wrong corridor at the wrong moment. That one stays with you. Three bullies and one genuinely decent person who didn't make it. Hogwarts hasn't changed that much, has it? Now, let's talk about all 10 richest marriages in Harry Potter explained. That's your next lesson here at Stickman Hogwarts. Watch now before Rita Skeeter starts asking questions about your love life.


=== All 11 Most TRAGIC Childhoods in Harry Potter EXPLAINED in Detail ===
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=RN2FVgxu7ic

Rubeus Hagrid. Everyone loves Hagrid. Not everyone knows how brutal his childhood actually was. Hagrid's mother was the giantess Frieda Woofa, and giants aren't exactly known for maternal instincts. She walked out when she was barely a toddler, went back to her colony, and never looked back. That left his wizard father, a small gentleman, raising a half-giant son alone. By all accounts, he adored Hagrid and did the job beautifully. Then he died when Hagrid was around 12, leaving an orphan in a community that viewed giants as monsters. He entered Hogwarts as a regular first-year student at age 11 and was sorted into Gryffindor house. It was supposed to be a fresh start, but it didn't last. In Hagrid's third year, students started getting attacked, and Tom Riddle needed someone to blame for opening the Chamber of Secrets. Hagrid was the easy target, friendless and famously fond of keeping dangerous creatures in his dorm. Riddle pinned it on him, and the staff believed it. At 13, Hagrid was expelled, had his wand snapped in front of him, and was forbidden from practicing magic for the rest of his life. Dumbledore intervened to keep him on as a gamekeeper. So, Hagrid spent his entire adolescence and adult life living in a hut on the grounds of the school that had publicly branded him a murderer, doing manual labor for the institution that expelled him. Fun. Luna Lovegood. Luna Lovegood had one thing going for her, her father, Xenophilius. He was eccentric, devoted, and actually present. Everything else was damage. The first hit came at nine. Her mother, Pandora, was an experimental witch who liked testing her own spells, and one afternoon, a spell went wrong in front of Luna. Pandora died in their home with her daughter watching the whole thing. Hogwarts made it worse. Her Ravenclaw housemates decided Luna was strange and punished her for it for years. They stole her belongings and hid them around the castle as an ongoing joke. Shoes, clothes, schoolbooks, anything that mattered to her, vanishing for weeks at a time, she'd spent nights searching the grounds barefoot. Nobody intervened. The nickname Loony Lovegood stuck so completely that it followed her everywhere. Then at 16, the Death Eaters grabbed her off the Hogwarts Express to pressure Xenophilius into silencing The Quibbler. Luna spent months locked in the cellar of Malfoy Manor alongside Ollivander, half-starved listening to Bellatrix torturing other prisoners in the rooms above. By the time Dobby got her out, she'd been a hostage longer than most adults could have survived. Remus Lupin. Remus Lupin was 4 years old when he was bitten by Fenrir Greyback, a werewolf who targeted children on purpose. The attack was targeted revenge. Greyback was punishing Remus's father, Lyall, a Ministry worker who had publicly insulted werewolves at a hearing. Lyall came home to find his son wounded and marked with a curse he'd carry for life. The transformations began immediately. Once a month, a small child was locked away alone and turned into something that didn't recognize him. Remus spent his childhood waking up the morning after full moons covered in claw marks and bite wounds he'd inflicted on himself, scars that never faded. The family's life collapsed around the secret. His parents moved constantly, lived in poverty, and cut off contact with most of their old friends because a discovered werewolf child meant either execution or Ministry containment cell. Dumbledore offered him Hogwarts anyway, planting the Whomping Willow over a tunnel to the Shrieking Shack where Remus could transform in private once a month. Locals decided the building was haunted from the sounds alone. His friends, James, Sirius, and Peter, eventually worked out the truth and became Animagi just to keep him company during transformations. That's what childhood looked like, monthly pain, a family on the run, and a kid slowly figuring out that the wizarding world would fear him the moment his secret got out. Dobby. Dobby was born into service at Malfoy Manor, and born into is literal here. When house-elf parents serve a family, their children automatically become that family's property. No gap year, no free childhood, which meant his earliest memories were probably of polishing silverware, not playing. His entire childhood was work and nothing but work. But it was exactly that harsh environment that shaped his one-of-a-kind character. As a house-elf, Dobby spent his entire life handling every household task for the Malfoy family. He kept an enormous estate in order, cleaning rooms, cooking meals, serving drinks, and carrying out any command, no matter how cruel. His uniform was a filthy old pillowcase because handing a house-elf real clothing would legally set him free, and the Malfoys weren't about to lose free labor. For any misbehavior, Dobby was magically bound to punish himself. He ironed his own hands, slammed his head into furniture, and shut his ears in the oven door. Despite all of this, Dobby somehow came out of Malfoy Manor knowing the difference between right and wrong, something the family he served never figured out. Most house-elves considered slavery their life's purpose. Dobby somehow disagreed. Voldemort's first fall, the night baby Harry somehow survived the killing curse, became Dobby's personal proof that the world could change. So, when he found out Lucius was sneaking Tom Riddle's diary into Hogwarts, he broke the one rule every house-elf lives by, loyalty to the master, and warned Harry himself. Later, he stood up to Bellatrix Lestrange to protect his friends, which for a creature in a pillowcase is the magical equivalent of fighting a tank with a tea towel. Classic Dobby. Draco Malfoy. Draco Malfoy had the manner, the money, and a mother who genuinely adored him. That's where the good news ends. Start with his father. Lucius treated parenting like Death Eater recruitment, with love handed out strictly on performance. The right opinions on Muggle-borns, the right sneer at the right people. Draco grew up terrified of disappointing him, which is why at Hogwarts he couldn't stop invoking Lucius' name in every argument. Then there's the isolation. Malfoy Manor operated more like a fortress than a house. Draco arrived at Hogwarts having never learned how relationships actually work, and it showed immediately. He tried to buy Harry's friendship in Madam Malkin's with a sneer at Ron. And when that failed, he spent six years surrounded by Crabbe and Goyle. The ideology did active damage. Taught from the nursery that house-elves were property and Muggle-borns were filth, Draco learned to suppress empathy as a survival skill, running the exact program his father installed. The real damage landed at 16. Voldemort moved into Malfoy Manor and the Dark Lord personally assigned Draco to murder Dumbledore under the threat of family execution. He spent a year watching Bellatrix torture prisoners in his dining room. Then he failed the assignment, the family got pardoned, and the Malfoy name became a national joke. Severus Snape. Severus Snape's childhood started in Spinner's End, a grim industrial corner of a Muggle town. His father, Tobias, was a Muggle, unemployed and violent. His mother, Eileen, was a witch who'd given up on her own magic somewhere along the way. Severus grew up inside their screaming matches. The poverty was constant. At 9 years old, he showed up for his first meeting with Lily Evans wearing his mother's old blouse and trousers that didn't fit because there was nothing else to wear. Other kids noticed. Petunia mocked him immediately, and the pattern followed him to Hogwarts. Then came James Potter and Sirius Black. What Severus suffered from them went well past schoolboy teasing, years of targeted, repeated humiliation, often in front of crowds. The worst of it happened after their OWL exam when James hung him upside down in the courtyard. Severus lashed out the only way he could, called Lily, the one person who had ever been kind to him, a Mudblood in public, and lost her friendship permanently in a single word. By his late teens, Severus had nothing waiting for him at home and nobody left at school. The Death Eaters were the first group that offered him a place and he took it. Sirius Black. Sirius Black was born into the house of Black, a pureblood family so devoted to blood purity it practically ran like a cult. Sirius rejected all of it and his own mother, Walburga, hated him for it openly, loudly, and for the rest of her life. Walburga screamed at him constantly, called him a blood traitor to his face, and made the family home a place he actively dreaded. His younger brother, Regulus, played the part she wanted, obedient, proud of the bloodline, and eventually a Death Eater at 16. Sirius was the disappointment, the Gryffindor, the one who came home from Hogwarts with the wrong friends and the wrong opinions. By his teens, he was waging a quiet war inside his own bedroom. He covered the walls with Gryffindor banners and posters of Muggle motorcycles and Muggle girls. Then stuck them up with a permanent sticking charm so Walburga could never take them down. Genius. Every time she walked past that room, she was reminded exactly what her son thought of the family values. He ran away at 16 and showed up on the Potters' doorstep, and James's parents took him in without asking questions. Tom Riddle. Tom Riddle entered the world on New Year's Eve in a Muggle orphanage. His mother staggered in, gave birth, named him, and died within the hour. His father, Tom Riddle Sr., a Muggle, had abandoned Merope before the pregnancy even showed. Tom never met a single blood relative. Wool's Orphanage in London took him in. He grew up there through the late 1930s in a city that would soon be at war. Mrs. Cole, the matron, told Dumbledore outright that there was something wrong with Tom. Calm, quiet, and capable of frightening the other kids without ever raising his voice, the magic showed up early and went straight to cruelty. Tom figured out he could hurt others with his mind, make objects move, and terrify other children into obedience long before anyone told him what he was. He took two kids from the orphanage to a seaside cave on a trip and did something to them there that neither would ever talk about again. He was around 10. Nobody ever taught him what love looked like because nobody in his life had any to give him. By the time Dumbledore showed up with a Hogwarts letter, Tom had already spent a decade learning that power was the only thing that made people treat him well. Neville Longbottom. Neville Longbottom was barely a year old when Bellatrix Lestrange and three other Death Eaters broke into his family home and tortured his parents, Frank and Alice, with the Cruciatus Curse until their minds gave out permanently. Frank and Alice survived, but the people they had been were gone. Neville grew up visiting them in the long-term ward at St. Mungo's, where his mother would press empty sweet wrappers into his hand without ever recognizing her own son. His grandmother, Augusta, raised him after that. She loved him in her own way, but her way involved measuring him against his father, a brilliant, brave Auror, and finding him lacking at every turn and letting him know it. Neville internalized it. By the time he got to Hogwarts, he genuinely believed he was a disappointment to the Longbottom name. The family had even doubted his magic. As a small child, Neville showed no signs of being a wizard, and his great-uncle, Algie, decided the solution was to literally drop him out of an upstairs window, hoping fear would force the magic to surface. And worked. Neville bounced down the garden. The adults celebrated. Nobody seemed concerned about what they had just done to a child. At Hogwarts, classmates openly called him nearly a Squib. He couldn't remember passwords, melted cauldrons, and jumped at loud noises. Given the childhood, that tracks. Merope Gaunt. If you want to understand Voldemort, start with his mother. Her name was Merope Gaunt, and her childhood was brutal. Her father, Marvolo, was a pureblood fanatic living in a filthy shack in Little Hangleton, obsessed with his Slytherin bloodline and furious that his daughter had turned out plain, quiet, and magically weak. He beat her regularly, at one point strangling her in front of a Ministry official who had come to arrest him, and barely pausing for the interruption. Her brother, Morfin, was worse in his own way. He watched the beatings, laughed through them, mocked Merope for being too afraid of their father to use her own wand, and joined in whenever it suited him. Between the two of them, Merope was the household's cook, cleaner, and punching bag, treated more like a servant than a daughter. All of it broke her, which is who live in constant terror sometimes lose access to their own power, and Marvolo called her a squib to her face for years. She never went to Hogwarts, had no friends, and never left the property long enough to know another life existed. Ariana Dumbledore. What happened to Ariana Dumbledore is the reason her brother Albus spent the rest of his very long life carrying guilt that never faded, and eventually died for. She was six years old when three Muggle boys caught her doing magic in the garden and decided to teach her a lesson. They roughed her up badly, and whatever happened in that garden shattered her, her magic, her mind, and any chance of normal life after that. Her father, Percival, tracked the boys down and went after them. The Ministry sent him to Azkaban for it, where he died without ever seeing his family again. Her mother, Kendra, moved the family to Godric's Hollow and hid Ariana from the world because Ariana's magic had become dangerous, unpredict dictable, explosive, and triggered by fear. A single bad outburst could level a room. One of those outbursts killed Kendra when Ariana was 14. That left her older brothers, Albus and Aberforth, as her only caretakers. Albus resented the responsibility. He met Gellert Grindelwald that summer, and the two of them began plotting a magical takeover of the Muggle world, while Ariana was left to Aberforth. At 14, she was killed by a stray spell during a duel between Albus, and Grindelwald. And to this day, nobody knows which of the three cast it. Leta Lestrange. And yes, that surname should sound familiar, but Leta came from another branch of the Lestrange name, long before Bellatrix Black married into the family. You might know her as Newt Scamander's first real friend, the same Newt who wrote the textbook Harry used at Hogwarts. Leta Lestrange's childhood was genuinely terrible, and it started before she was even born. Her father, Corvus Lestrange Sr., used the Imperius Curse on a Senegalese witch named Loréna, Leta's mother, to force her into a relationship. Leta was born from that. Her mother died shortly after, and Corvus treated his daughter with about as much warmth as you'd expect from a man who built a family through dark magic. As a small child, Leta was put on a ship to America with her baby half-brother, Corvus Jr. He wouldn't stop crying, and Leta, exhausted, alone, still a child herself, swapped him with another baby from a nearby cabin. Then the ship sank. The other family's baby survived, Corvus didn't. Leta spent the rest of her life knowing her brother drowned because of a choice she made before she was old enough to understand what she was doing. She grew up carrying the Lestrange name, which in the wizarding world was already a red flag long before Bellatrix made it famous. Other children avoided her. At Hogwarts, she was the outcast everyone whispered about. Dark family, strange girl, stay away. Newt Scamander was her only real friend, and she lost him, too. Leta's experiment with a Jarvey, a magical creature that's basically an aggressive talking ferret, went wrong. Newt took the blame, got expelled, and Leta was left alone again. Harry Potter. Harry Potter was just over a year old the night Voldemort came to Godric's Hollow. You probably know exactly how that night went. What matters for this list is that Harry walked out of it an orphan. Then came the Dursleys. His aunt Petunia and uncle Vernon took him in, resenting every second of it, and spent the next 10 years making sure Harry knew exactly how unwelcome he was. They put him in the cupboard under the stairs, a literal storage space between the suitcase, and that was his bedroom until he was 11. His room contained a cot, a spider or two, and the constant sound of Dudley stomping overhead. Treatment matched the accommodations. Harry was locked in the cupboard as punishment for things like Dudley's tantrums, went without meals when Dursleys were angry, and wore Dudley's hand-me-downs that hung off him because he was chronically underfed. His birthdays were ignored. His cousin's gang chased him around the schoolyard as a sport called Harry Hunting. He was told his parents died in a car crash. Nobody in the house said their names. Harry spent a decade believing he came from nothing because the one family he had left was determined to make sure he never found out otherwise. Most of the kids in this list grew up to change the wizarding world anyway. Find out exactly how in all 24 major events you missed after the Deathly Hallows explained. That's your next lesson here at Stickman Hogwarts. Click now before Voldemort's childhood trauma becomes everyone's problem again.


=== All 12 RICHEST Wizards and Witches in Harry Potter EXPLAINED in Detail ===
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=WFqiZf9NZJo

12th place, Gilderoy Lockhart, the man who forgot his own fortune. Gilderoy made roughly $8 million from books he didn't write, stories he didn't live, and adventures he never actually had. Just listen to his credentials. A shelf full of best-selling books, five-time winner of Witch Weekly's Most Charming Smile Award, Order of Merlin. His CV would look flawless, but every single line of it was stolen from someone else. Gilderoy's method was elegant. Find a witch or wizard who actually did something heroic, interview them, extract every detail, then hit them with a memory charm, and publish the whole thing under his own name. Years of best-sellers, speaking tours, cartloads of fan mail, and a Hogwarts teaching position. And then he tried to memory charm two 12-year-olds with a broken wand. The spell backfired so completely that Gilderoy Lockhart forgot his own name, his career, and arguably his entire personality. He's been in the permanent spell damage ward at St. Mungo's ever since, cheerfully signing autographs for nobody in particular. 11th place, George Weasley. Here's the journey from second-hand dress robes that smelled vaguely of Great-Aunt Tessie to $10 million. The Weasley family was famously aggressively broke. George's dad, Arthur, worked in the one Ministry department that paid almost nothing because he genuinely loved Muggle rubber ducks. But George and his twin brother, Fred, didn't plan to stay broke. And when they were ready to build something, they found the least likely investor in wizarding history, Harry Potter, who handed them his entire Triwizard Tournament prize, 1,000 Galleons, and told them to go build something. A 14-year-old venture capitalist. Weasleys' Wizard Wheezes opened in Diagon Alley and immediately became one of the most successful shops in wizarding Britain. Even during the war, when the entire street was shuttered and terrified, their shop stayed packed. Then Fred died in the Battle of Hogwarts, and George kept the shop open anyway. After the war, the business expanded. Peruvian Instant Darkness Powder, Shield Hats, Decoy Detonators, jokes with practical applications. A fortune built from 1,000 gifted Galleons and the stubbornness to keep going. Harry Potter, for the record, never asked for his cut. Worst investor in wizarding history, best friend, though. 10th place, Hepzibah Smith. Hepzibah Smith was worth roughly $50 million, which is impressive until you find out how she lost all of it in one afternoon over a cup of tea. But first, her family. Hepzibah was a direct descendant of Helga Hufflepuff, one of the four witches and wizards who founded Hogwarts over a thousand years ago. That's like finding out your great-great-great-grandmother built Harvard, except boring old Harvard doesn't have moving staircases and a giant squid in the lake. So, she had the money, she had the bloodline, and she had a hobby that was basically a disaster waiting to happen, collecting incredibly rare, incredibly powerful magical artifacts and keeping them in her house, not in a vault, in her house, where she showed them to guests over tea and biscuits. Her two greatest prizes were Hufflepuff's golden cup, a relic from her own ancestor, and Slytherin's locket, both among the most historically significant objects in the wizarding world. And one day, she invited a charming young shop assistant named Tom Riddle over to see them because he was so polite, so interested, and so eager to learn. He poisoned her, stole both artifacts, and framed her house-elf, Hokey, by modifying the poor elf's memory. The Ministry investigated for about 5 minutes, Hokey confessed, and everyone moved on because apparently blaming an elf was easier than asking a single follow-up question. Riddle walked out with two objects he would later turn into Horcruxes, containers for pieces of his own soul, designed to make him immortal. So, Hepzibah's prize collection got used to help the most dangerous dark wizard in history cheat death. At the end of the day, Hepzibah just couldn't resist a pretty face. The pretty face just happened to belong to the future Dark Lord. Worst taste in men in wizarding history. Ninth place, Harry Potter. Harry Potter had roughly $60 million in his vault, and the wildest part is he didn't even know about it for the first 11 years of his life. The Potter fortune started with a man named Linfred of Stitchcombe, a 12th-century wizard whose neighbors called him the Potterer, which is literally where the surname comes from. Linfred quietly invented remedies that would later become Pepperup Potion and Skelegrow, two of the most widely used potions in wizarding medicine. He didn't patent either of them, which is either incredibly generous or incredibly poor financial planning. His descendants did better. Harry's grandfather, Fleamont Potter, invented Sleekeazy's Hair Potion, a hair product, and he quadrupled the family gold. So, just to be clear, the Potter fortune came from a medieval herbalist and a bottle of conditioner. Make of that what you will. Fleamont's son, James, and his wife, Lily, inherited everything, lived modestly, and died young, which left Harry, age one, as the sole heir to a vault full of gold he wouldn't even know about for another 10 years, until Hagrid walked him into Gringotts on his birthday. Then Harry's godfather, Sirius Black, died and left him everything on top, the Black family vault, a townhouse in central London, and whatever else the noble and most ancient house of Black had been hoarding for centuries. $60 million total for a kid who spent the first 11 years of his life sleeping in a cupboard under the stairs and wearing his cousin's old clothes. Eighth place, Rita Skeeter. Rita Skeeter was worth roughly $80 million, and roughly 99.9% of it came from gossip, scandal, and other people's worst moments. Basically, the Kardashian business model, but with a quill. She was the Daily Prophet's most read journalist, a best-selling author, and an unregistered Animagus who turned into a beetle to spy on people. Which, as career strategies go, is either brilliantly committed or profoundly unhinged, possibly both. Her Quick-Quotes Quill did most of the creative work. Feed it a few boring facts and it produced dramatic, scandalous, borderline fictional articles that sold papers by the thousands. Harry Potter was an unstable attention seeker, Hermione was a heartbreaker toying with famous boys, Hagrid was a dangerous half-giant. None of it true, all of it profitable. Her real payday came after Dumbledore's death, when she published The Life and Lies of Albus Dumbledore, a biography so invasive, so sensationalist, and so spectacularly well-timed that it became the best-selling book in wizarding Britain almost overnight. She wrote it in 4 weeks because apparently grief is just another content opportunity. And then a 15-year-old Hermione Granger discovered the beetle trick, trapped her in a jar, and blackmailed her into the silence for an entire year. $80 million of media empire briefly neutralized by a teenager with a jam jar. Seventh place, Garrick Ollivander. Garrick Ollivander was worth roughly $100 million, and you'd never guess it by looking at him or anything about the way he lived. But here's the thing about Garrick. He was the latest owner of Ollivanders, a family business that's been running since 382 BC. To put that in perspective, the Roman Republic was still figuring out plumbing, the Parthenon had been standing for less than a century, and the Ollivanders were already selling wands. Every witch and wizard in Britain has walked into that narrow, dusty shop on Diagon Alley and handed over seven Galleons for a wand. Every single one. That's a monopoly wearing a dusty window display as a disguise. And Garrick inherited all of it, the business, the reputation, the knowledge, and the fortune that comes with two millennia of being the only option. He could have lived in a mansion, he could have had albino peacocks like Lucius Malfoy. Instead, he lived alone above the shop surrounded by thousands of wand boxes, and got genuinely more excited about a phoenix feather core than about anything money could buy. During the war, Voldemort kidnapped him and locked him in the Malfoys' basement for months, torturing him for information about wand magic. $100 million in family wealth, and it couldn't buy him out of a cellar. Sixth place, Sirius Black. The richest fugitive in wizarding history, with roughly $150 million to his name, and absolutely nowhere to spend it. Sirius Black inherited the entire fortune of the noble and most ancient house of Black, one of the oldest, wealthiest, and most deeply unpleasant pureblood families in Britain. The family home, 12 Grimmauld Place, sits in central London, which even by Muggle real estate standards is worth a small fortune. By wizarding standards, with its unplottable enchantments, hidden rooms, and extremely cursed silverware, it's worth considerably more. Sirius hated almost everything about his family, ran away at 16, got blasted off the family tapestry, and spent the next few years fighting in a war against the exact ideology his parents believed in. He never actually got cut off from the vault, though. The money stayed, the house stayed, the family heirlooms stayed, including a screaming portrait of his mother that nobody could remove from the wall. Then he got framed for 13 murders he didn't commit, and spent 12 years in Azkaban, the wizarding world's worst prison. He became the first person to ever escape the place, and celebrated his freedom by living in a cave and eating rats. And while on the run, hunted by every Auror in the country, he still found the time to anonymously order a Firebolt, the most expensive racing broom in the world, and send it to his godson, Harry, for Christmas. He got 2 years with Harry, 2 years of being the godfather he never got to be. Then his own cousin, Bellatrix, killed him in the Department of Mysteries, right in front of Harry. He left everything to Harry, every Galleon, every haunted heirloom. The money never meant anything to him. Remarkable, really. Fifth place, Narcissa Malfoy. Narcissa Malfoy was worth roughly $200 million. She was the youngest of three Black sisters, and the only one her parents never had to worry about. Bellatrix was fanatical, Andromeda eloped with a Muggle-born and got burned off the family tapestry, but Narcissa Narcissa was the perfect daughter. She knew how to read people, how to adjust, and how to walk away with everything she came for. Her parents adored her for it. She got everything. Then she married Lucius Malfoy. Real love by all accounts, but also instant access to roughly half the Malfoy fortune. One wedding and her net worth jumped from Black family heiress to one of the richest witches in Britain. And she kept playing. When Draco was given an impossible mission by Voldemort, Narcissa went to Snape and walked out of that room with an unbreakable vow. His life magically bound to protecting her son. Bellatrix pushed for it, sure, but Narcissa made it happen. When Lucius went to Azkaban after the Department of Mysteries disaster, she took control of the Malfoy estate without flinching. Managed the finances, maintained the manor, kept the family name functional while her husband sat in a cell, and her sister terrorized the countryside. And when Voldemort himself asked her whether Harry Potter was dead in the Forbidden Forest, she lied to his noseless face because all she needed to know was whether Draco was alive. That single moment of cold, calculated defiance brought the entire family enough goodwill to survive the post-war tribunals. Every sister lost something. Narcissa kept everything. Fourth place, Lucius Malfoy. Lucius Malfoy was worth roughly $500 million, and he spent a noticeable chunk of it on albino peacocks for the front lawn. That's the entire personality because he wanted everyone who walked up his extremely long driveway to his extremely large manor in Wiltshire to understand one thing immediately. This man has more money than sense, and he is perfectly comfortable with that. The Malfoy fortune was old, centuries of pure-blood wealth accumulated through land, influence, and a long tradition of being on whichever side was currently winning. Lucius perfected this strategy. He donated to St. Mungo's, funded Ministry campaigns, sat on the Hogwarts Board of Governors, and bribed enough officials to make his name synonymous with both generosity and corruption, depending on who you asked. He married Narcissa Black, connecting two of the wealthiest pure-blood families in Britain because apparently, even a fortune of $500 million can be improved by adding another fortune on top. And then Voldemort moved into his house, literally. The Dark Lord set up operations in Malfoy Manor, used Lucius's home as headquarters, humiliated him in front of his allies, took his wand, and turned the family patriarch into something between a servant and a hostage. Half a billion dollars in assets, and the man couldn't even sit comfortably at his own dining table. Turns out, money helps, just not always. At least he had his albino peacocks to look at. Third place, Bellatrix Lestrange. Bellatrix Lestrange started with roughly $300 million in her Gringotts vault, and put an actual dragon in front of it. An actual full-sized, half-blind Ukrainian Ironbelly chained beneath the bank for decades. What was inside? Mountains of gold, piles of goblin-made armor, enchanted treasures multiplying endlessly thanks to the Gemino Curse. Everything burning to the touch from the Flagrante Charm, and tucked among it all, Hufflepuff's golden cup, a Horcrux that Voldemort trusted her to protect. The vault was beautiful, lethal, and completely insane, which described its owner perfectly. Marrying into the Lestrange family doubled that to $600 million, the kind of financial upgrade most people would celebrate. Bellatrix probably didn't notice. Then she spent 14 years in Azkaban for torturing the Longbottoms into permanent insanity. She emerged from prison looking feral, fanatical, and not remotely interested in her financial portfolio. The money just sat there. $600 million in gold and artifacts guarded by a dragon growing through dark enchantments, untouched for over a decade. Bellatrix didn't care about Voldemort. The vault was a storage unit she happened to own, not a priority. And she would have traded every Galleon for 5 more minutes of serving the Dark Lord, which tracks perfectly for her. Second place, Voldemort. You thought Voldemort wouldn't make this list? Well, let's see the numbers. He got access to over $100 billion in under a decade, and didn't earn a single Knut of it. Tom Riddle grew up in a Muggle orphanage wearing second-hand clothes, eating institutional food, and owning nothing of value. He arrived at Hogwarts with an empty trunk and a chip on his shoulder the size of the Astronomy Tower. So, how did he manage to do this? Through intimidation and blackmail. The Lestrange vault, hundreds of millions in gold handed over by followers too devoted to question him. The Malfoy coffers opened willingly by Lucius, who treated Voldemort's cause like a networking opportunity until it was far too late to back out. The Ministry treasury itself, once he [snorts] took control. Death Eater's donations from every wealthy pure-blood family to afraid or too fanatical to say no. Confiscated assets from Muggle-borns, stripped of their wands and their rights. He absorbed wealth, every vault, every estate, every family fortune that came within reach got redirected, repurposed, and spent on an ideology built entirely around one man's refusal to die. And personally, he lived in other people's houses, wore robes, and showed zero interest in material comfort. The man who controlled $100 billion didn't own a home. You know what happens next. Everything was destroyed, every asset seized, every ally dead or imprisoned, every Galleon reclaimed. He died with nothing, which is exactly what he started with. First place, Nicolas Flamel. Nicolas Flamel was worth over $100 trillion, not billion, trillion. For context, Elon Musk has spent 30 years hoarding wealth, building rockets, electric cars, and a social media platform to get roughly $800 billion. Flamel just had a stone and time, lots of time. And before you ask, yes, there's a trick, and no, you can't repeat it. He created the Philosopher's Stone sometime in the 14th century, a single object capable of two things, producing the Elixir of Life, which made the drinker immortal, and turning any base metal into gold. Unlimited lifespan and unlimited money from one stone held by one man for over six centuries. That's literally the motherlode cheat code from The Sims, but for unlimited real money. Flamel and his wife Perenelle lived quietly, remarkably quietly, for two people who had solved both death and poverty before the printing press was invented. They apparently spent most of it on research, philanthropy, and helping fund the Beauxbatons Academy campus. He was an known associate of Albus Dumbledore. The two collaborated on alchemy, which given that one of them was already immortal and the other was arguably the most powerful wizard alive, was to be in a terrifying research partnership for everyone else involved. In 1992, Flamel agreed to let Dumbledore destroy the stone after Voldemort tried to steal it. He and Perenelle had enough elixir stored to settle their affairs, and then they died, voluntarily, after 600 years. The most casual exit in the history of money. Now, if you want to know what happened to all this money after the war, check out all 24 major events you missed after the Deathly Hallows explained. === All 15 TRAGIC Masters of The Elder Wand EXPLAINED in Detail ===
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=PExibbcsdnI

Antioch Peverell. Antioch Peverell came from an ancient pureblood wizarding family and was already a skilled and powerful wizard, but like every wizard drama story goes, he needed more. The Elder Wand enters the chat. Now, Dumbledore actually believed Antioch created it himself, but the legend tells it differently. Around 1214, Antioch bargained with Death for the ultimate weapon, a wand that could win any fight against any opponent forever. Death gave him exactly what he asked for, which is always a bad sign. So, once Antioch got the most powerful weapon of the wizarding world, he tested it immediately. He tracked down a wizard he had a grudge against, wiped the floor with him in a single duel, and spent the rest of the evening telling every stranger in the inn about his shiny new super weapon. Basically, waving a bag of gold in a room full of thieves and then falling asleep upstairs. By morning, Antioch was dead. Someone crept in during the night, cut his throat, and walked off with the wand. Total time as the most powerful wizard alive, roughly one drunk evening. His killer vanished into the night with the wand. Who it was, nobody knows for sure, but there's a theory, and it leads straight to the next name on this list, Emeric the Evil. Around 1420, Emeric the Evil had turned medieval Britain into his personal hunting ground. Emeric was a medieval dark wizard described as exceptionally aggressive, which given the competition on this list is saying something. First-year Hogwarts students study him in History of Magic, where they regularly confuse him with Uric the Oddball, a harmless eccentric who wore a jellyfish on his head. Emeric wielded the Elder Wand to terrorize the English countryside for years, amassing followers and cursing anything that looked at him sideways. Nobody knows exactly how Emeric got the Elder Wand. Some believe he was the one who killed Antioch Peverell, the wand's original owner, making him the first thief in a very long chain. Others say Antioch was already dead, and the wand passed through unnamed hands before reaching Emeric. The only thing every version agrees on is that somebody died for it. Around 1432, a wizard with the spectacularly absurd name of Egbert the Egregious challenged Emeric to a straight duel. One catastrophic exchange of curses later, years of unchecked cruelty ended in a single evening. The wand moved on without looking back. His killer took the wand, earned himself a ridiculous title, and then did the last thing anyone expected, Egbert the Egregious. So, around 1432, Egbert the Egregious had just become the wizard who ended Emeric's reign, and the magical world braced for whatever came next. Egbert the Egregious was a dark wizard of the Middle Ages. By all accounts, he had a terrible reputation, and honestly, his nickname says it directly. Egregious literally means outrageously bad. Among the Elder Wand's known masters, Egbert stands out as an exception, though not the only one. More on that later. Because he won the wand in a straight duel, face to face. What came next was almost nothing. Egbert's time with the wand left strangely little trace in the historical record, but Egbert might be the only owner in the wand's long bloody history who held the Elder Wand and found it boring. What he did accomplish accidentally was proof of concept. The wand could be taken through a fair duel. Every ambitious wizard on the continent took notes, and from this point forward, the Elder Wand attracted a very specific kind of hunter. Scholars, warlords, and power-obsessed maniacs who saw it as the ultimate shortcut. The next owner fell squarely into the first category, Gellert Grindelwald. By the late 1400s, the Elder Wand belonged to someone who, for once, opened a book before opening fire. Gellert was described as an unpleasant character, a medieval dark wizard who studied forbidden magic the way other people study languages. He collected curses, cataloged dark spells, and yet even he refused to write about Horcruxes, considering the ritual too dark even by his standards. He treated the wand like a research partner, calling it his most wicked and subtle friend in writing. The relationship produced Magic Most Evil, a catalog of the most dangerous spells and curses known at the time, and still one of the most restricted books in wizarding history. The Elder Wand fed him knowledge, and Gellert fed it obsession. For a while, the arrangement worked. Fun fact, Gellert is the only recorded owner who tried to document what the Elder Wand actually does, rather than just swinging it at enemies. His own son decided that waiting for a natural inheritance was overrated. Hereward locked Gellert in the cellar of his own home and left him there to die. The wand's most studious owner was murdered by the one person he presumably trusted, Hereward. So, around 1490, Hereward walked away from his father's house with the Elder Wand and a family legacy that could not have looked more cursed if it tried. The wand accepted him anyway, which says plenty about how little loyalty has ever meant to it. According to Dumbledore, he was genuinely mad. Whether the madness came before or after growing up in a house full of dark magic experiments, nobody knows. Either way, he decided he wanted the wand more than he wanted a father. So, after he got away with the wand, Hereward almost disappeared from the record. For someone holding the most powerful wand ever created, his historical footprint is almost insulting. One brutal inheritance, then silence. What happened to him is anyone's guess. The pattern suggests another wizard eventually killed him for the wand, but the record gives us no name, no date, and no clean version of the story. The Elder Wand vanished from the record again, drifting through decades of missing history and probably leaving a few bodies nobody bothered to document. When it resurfaced in the 1500s, the wand had found someone who treated dark magic less like a hobby and more like a business, Barnabas Deverill. Barnabas Deverill picked up the Elder Wand in the 1500s and immediately made it everyone else's problem. He used the wand to carve out a reputation as one of the most feared figures in Britain, gathering followers and crushing opposition. Barnabas was a dark wizard who had zero interest in keeping a low profile. He's the one who gave the wand the name Eldrun Wand, an old word for elderwood. The Elder Wand had several names over the centuries, but Barnabas was the first owner to actually name it publicly. The man wanted everyone to know what he was holding. Deverill built a network of dark loyalists, waged open campaigns against rival wizards, and generally made the English magical community a terrible place to live. Honestly, the man treated magical tyranny like a full-time profession, with structure, staff, and zero remorse. The Elder Wand served as both crown and executioner's axe during his reign. And for a while, Deverill seemed untouchable, but the wand's favored lesson has always been patience. Deverill was eventually hunted down and killed by a wizard named Loxias. By the early 1600s, the Elder Wand belonged to Loxias, a dark warlord who turned violence into a governing philosophy. He launched what survivors later called the Wars of Loxias, decades of open warfare across Europe, dueling and executing anyone who questioned his authority. His reputation grew so monstrous that rivals stopped using his name entirely, whispering the Wand Lord instead. Translation, this man scared people so badly they invented a title to avoid speaking to him directly. Centuries before Voldemort made name avoidance fashionable, Loxias was already running the playbook. His campaign fed on the Elder Wand's power, every curse amplified, every public execution turned into a spectacle. Under Loxias, the Elder Wand went from rumor to a recognized symbol of total dominance across the continent. But as always, someone showed up with a better strategy. Loxias was overthrown, and his reign collapsed in a storm of curses that reportedly cracked castle walls for miles. The problem is, the story of who actually defeated him split almost immediately, and what followed was the strangest chapter in the wand's history. Two names, one mystery, and zero answers, Arcus Livius. Sometime around 1670, the Elder Wand trail goes cold. After Loxias fell, plenty of wizards rushed to claim they were the one who killed him, because killing the most feared warlord in Europe meant becoming the Elder Wand's next master. That kind of bragging rights attracted a crowd, but out of all the names thrown around, only two stuck in the historical record, Arcus and Livius. Almost nothing is known about either of them, who they were, where they came from, and what they did with the wand. Dumbledore himself admitted he had no idea which one actually did it, and joked that it might have been Loxias's own mother. So, who really took down the Wand Lord? If you've got a theory, drop it in the comments below. For nearly 200 years after that, the Elder Wand disappeared from documented history entirely. When the trail picked up again in the late 1800s, the wand had landed somewhere nobody expected, and with an owner who had zero interest in pointing it at people, Mykew Gregorovitch. By the late 1800s, the Elder Wand had found its way to Mykew Gregorovitch, a master wandmaker based in Eastern Europe. For the first time in centuries, the wand's owner had no interest in conquest. Gregorovitch wanted to reverse-engineer it, to crack the secret of its power, and replicate it in his own designs. He spent years experimenting on the wand, studying its core, its wood, and its response to different spells. The research was meticulous. The security was not. Gregorovitch let it slip that he possessed a wand beyond compare, which in the wizarding world is an open invitation for someone to come take it. Near the end of the 19th century, that someone arrived. A young blond thief broke into Gregorovitch's workshop at night, silent, precise, and terrifyingly gifted. One stunning spell later, the intruder was gone, and so was the wand. Gregorovitch caught a glimpse of the thief's face as he leapt from the window, a face that would haunt him for the rest of his life, and eventually cost him his life. That thief was Gellert Grindelwald. By 1899, the Elder Wand was in the hands of a teenage Gellert Grindelwald. Over the next four decades, he'd prove exactly why that was a catastrophically bad thing for everyone else. The Elder Wand made him unbeatable in a duel, and word travels fast when nobody who fights you walks away. Grindelwald brought something no previous owner had, charisma, vision, and patience all at once. He preached unity between wizards and freedom from secrecy, wrapping authoritarian ideology in the language of liberation. His slogan, "For the greater good," convinced thousands that Muggle subjugation was somehow progressive. By the 1940s, Grindelwald had ignited the global wizarding war, reshaping magical politics across continents. He built a fortress called Nurmengard to lock up his enemies. Well, spoiler, he spent the rest of his life in it. In 1945, the one person Grindelwald couldn't manipulate finally confronted him, Albus Dumbledore, former best friend, intellectual equal, and the only wizard alive who understood Grindelwald completely, met him in what's still called the greatest duel of all time. When the smoke cleared, Grindelwald was defeated and locked inside the very fortress he'd built for others. He'd spend the next 53 years there until a noseless dark wizard showed up looking for answers about a certain wand. But hold on, rewind 19 years. Someone already defeated Grindelwald before Dumbledore did and it wasn't some legendary dark wizard hunter. It was Newt Scamander. In 1926, Grindelwald was hiding inside MACUSA disguised as the American Auror Percival Graves. After a confrontation in a New York subway station, Graves turned on MACUSA itself and Newt Scamander stepped in. He released the swooping evil to block Graves' spells, hit him with a binding spell that brought him to his knees, and Tina Goldstein summoned the dropped wand with Accio. Then Newt cast Revelio and Graves' face transformed into Grindelwald's. By every rule of wand allegiance, the Elder Wand's loyalty should have shifted to Newt that night. Grindelwald was defeated, subdued, disarmed, and dragged off in front of the entire MACUSA leadership. And as Harry Potter would prove decades later, you don't need to physically hold the Elder Wand to become its master. You just need to beat the wizard who owns it. That makes Newt Scamander the Elder Wand's forgotten master, owner for 19 years without ever knowing it. Grindelwald kept wielding the wand across Europe, but if its full loyalty belonged to a magizoologist in a blue coat, that might explain more than the history books admit. The real transfer came later when a very specific wizard finally stopped avoiding the fight, Albus Dumbledore. From 1945 to 1997, the Elder Wand belonged to Albus Dumbledore and for 52 years, the wand served wisdom instead of war. He defended Hogwarts, dueled dark wizards, and performed magic so advanced that even Voldemort avoided fighting him directly. Dumbledore managed what every previous owner had failed at. He wielded absolute power without letting it define him. The catch, Dumbledore understood exactly what he was holding. He knew the wand's history, every murdered owner, every stolen allegiance. And he spent decades planning to break the cycle. His strategy was elegant, die undefeated. If no one conquered him, the wand's power would die with him. He arranged his own death with Severus Snape, a mercy killing to spare Draco Malfoy from becoming a murderer. On paper, perfect. In practice, the plan had a single devastating flaw that Dumbledore either missed or accepted as a necessary risk. On the night of the Astronomy Tower, Dumbledore's plan failed before Snape even entered the scene. Someone else got there first, Draco Malfoy. In 1997, Draco Malfoy, pale and sick with fear, disarmed Dumbledore at the top of the Astronomy Tower. He couldn't bring himself to kill. The plan was never his. But Expelliarmus sealed a contract Draco never understood. The Elder Wand's loyalty transferred to him instantly. He never held it, never touched it, and spent the following months having no idea he was technically the most powerful wizard in Britain. The wand sat in Dumbledore's tomb and Draco went home to a house full of Death Eaters. None of them had any idea they were living with the Elder Wand's rightful owner. Draco's ownership lasted about 9 months until Harry Potter grabbed his wand during a rescue at Malfoy Manor and the Elder Wand quietly changed hands for the last time. Draco never found out. Meanwhile, someone far more dangerous was digging up Dumbledore's grave, Voldemort. In 1998, Lord Voldemort tracked the Elder Wand to its last known owner, Grindelwald, still locked in Nurmengard after 53 years. Now, what happened next depends on how you consumed your Harry Potter. If you read the books, Grindelwald kept his mouth shut. He knew Dumbledore had the wand but refused to say a word and Voldemort killed him on the spot. If you only watched the films, yeah, your version is a little more relaxed. Grindelwald just told him everything and Voldemort walked away like he got directions at a gas station. Either way, Voldemort got what he needed. He cracked open Dumbledore's tomb and pulled the Elder Wand from a dead man's hands. But the wand had other plans. Turns out stealing a wand from a corpse doesn't transfer allegiance, defeating its master does. And Voldemort had the wrong man on his list. He murdered Severus Snape in the Shrieking Shack convinced that killing Dumbledore's killer would complete the chain. But the wand's loyalty had already left Snape's orbit entirely. It belonged to a Malfoy boy and then to Harry Potter. Every spell Voldemort cast with the Elder Wand worked at a fraction of its power. In the Great Hall, when he raised it for the final killing curse against Harry, the wand recognized its actual master standing across from him. The curse rebounded and the Elder Wand killed the man holding it, ending both Voldemort and the bloodline of violence stretching back to 1214, Harry Potter. So, in 1998, Harry Potter stood in the wreckage of Hogwarts holding a wand that had outlived every person who'd ever carried it. He'd won it the same way he'd survived most things, accidentally, reluctantly, and without any idea it was happening at the time. When he'd grabbed Draco's wand at Malfoy Manor weeks earlier, the Elder Wand's allegiance had quietly shifted to him. There was no ceremony and Harry wasn't even fighting for the Elder Wand. After the war, Harry did something no previous owner had managed. He returned the Elder Wand to Dumbledore's tomb, ending the bloodline of masters permanently. Unless you only watched the films, in which case he just snapped it in half and yeeted it off a bridge like a candy wrapper. Either way, the curse ended. For the first time since 1214, the Elder Wand story closed without a murder. Centuries of theft and betrayal and it took a boy who never wanted power to finally let the wand rest. Honestly, that might be the most magical part. Now, let's talk about every legendary wizard and witch actually died. That's your next lesson here at Stickman Hogwarts. === All 3 Black Sister Origins in Harry Potter EXPLAINED in Detail ===
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=4Px7yGs4CB4

Born into darkness, the Black family has one of the strongest pureblood bloodlines in the entire Harry Potter series. And honestly, some of the most interesting characters to ever come out of that family are three sisters. Cygnus Black the Third and Druella Rosier, both purebloods, both deeply connected to Voldemort and his growing army of Death Eaters, married and had three daughters in the 1950s. Bellatrix, the oldest, Andromeda, the middle, Narcissa, the youngest. From the moment they were old enough to listen, all three were taught the same thing. Pureblood wizards were superior. Muggle-borns were beneath them. Blood was everything. The Black family name meant something, and keeping it that way wasn't a suggestion, it was simply the way things were in that house. All three went to Hogwarts and were sorted into Slytherin, one after the other, as if the hat barely needed a moment. The family would have accepted nothing less, and the hat apparently agreed. Now, at Hogwarts, you start to see the cracks. Bellatrix gravitated immediately toward students who were already on their way to becoming Death Eaters. Narcissa moved in similar circles, though she carried herself differently, more composed, more calculated, less likely to scare people in hallways. And then there was Andromeda, also Slytherin, same family, same upbringing, and somehow quietly she started pulling away from all of it. So, Hogwarts was over, and that's when everything got darker. They got married. When the time came to marry, all three sisters made choices that revealed everything you needed to know about who they were. Bellatrix married Rodolphus Lestrange, a fellow she had known since Hogwarts. Now, if you read every single page of the Harry Potter series looking for one moment of genuine warmth between them, good luck. You will not find it. Not one. She never mentioned him affectionately, never bore him a child, never once looked at him the way she looked at Voldemort. The marriage was obligation dressed up as tradition. A pureblood matching a pureblood because that was what the Black family did. Bellatrix fulfilled the requirement and immediately moved on. Her devotion belonged to a noseless man, and everyone who knew her understood that perfectly. Narcissa was different. She genuinely fell for Lucius Malfoy, another pureblood from a family just as wealthy and well-connected as the Blacks. There was real affection there, real loyalty, the kind that would eventually push her to do things that even surprised herself. Together they built an extraordinary life. Malfoy Manor, enormous wealth, a son named Draco born in 1980 whom Narcissa loved so fiercely, it would become the most defining thing about her entire existence. Lucius hardened Draco with pureblood ideology, preached to him about blood status, shaped him in the Black and Malfoy tradition. Narcissa simply adored him. Both things were true simultaneously, and neither canceled the other out. And then there was Andromeda. She fell in love with Ted Tonks, a Muggle-born. Now, in a family that burned people off the tapestry for lesser offenses, marrying a Muggle-born was not just a scandal, it was a declaration. Andromeda knew exactly what she was doing, and she did it anyway. The family responded immediately. Her face was burned off the Black family tree, right alongside Sirius Black's. Narcissa, when asked about her sister years later, was cutting and precise. They had not set eyes on Andromeda since she married Ted Tonks. That was that, done, gone. As far as the Blacks were concerned, she never existed. So, now you have three sisters living completely different lives. Andromeda and Ted built something quieter, less wealthy, less prestigious, but significantly warmer. They raised their daughter, Nymphadora, who hated being called that and made sure absolutely everyone knew it, preferring simply Tonks, in a household built on decency rather than status. Meanwhile, Narcissa raised Draco in extraordinary luxury. Expensive care packages, the best broomsticks money could buy, every comfort a child could possibly want. And Bellatrix was not raising anyone. She was following Voldemort with the kind of devotion that should probably have concerned people considerably earlier than it did. But then in 1981, Voldemort fell, and what happened next split these three sisters apart in ways nobody could have predicted. The Black sisters' breaking point. When Voldemort fell, the Death Eaters did what most people do when the thing they backed suddenly collapses. They scattered and started lying. Most of them claimed they had been acting under the Imperius Curse the whole time. Yeah, okay. That Voldemort had controlled them against their will. Lucius Malfoy said exactly that, kept a straight face, and walked free. Narcissa exhaled. Life for the Malfoys continued more or less as normal. Bellatrix did the complete opposite. She stood in front of the court and told them exactly where her loyalties were, proudly. She wasn't going to pretend she hadn't done what she did. She sat in the chain chair like it was a throne. And as they dragged her out toward Azkaban, she screamed at the judge, Barty Crouch, that the Dark Lord would rise again, that they alone had been faithful, and that when he returned, they would be rewarded beyond any of his other followers. Not exactly the speech of someone who had been acting against their will. Now, before all of this, before the capture, before the trial, Bellatrix and her group had done something the wizarding world would never forget. They went after Frank and Alice Longbottom, both Aurors, both members of the Order of the Phoenix. Bellatrix believed they had information on Voldemort's whereabouts, so they tortured them for hours. When Frank refused to talk, they went after Alice. When Alice refused, they kept going. The four of them, Bellatrix, Rodolphus, Rabastan, and Barty Crouch Jr., tortured the Longbottoms so severely that both were driven permanently out of their minds. They spent the rest of their lives in St. Mungo's Hospital unable to recognize their own son. That son was Neville Longbottom, the same boy who would one day pull a sword out of a hat at the Battle of Hogwarts. While Bellatrix rotted in Azkaban, Andromeda was raising Tonks in a world that had nothing to do with any of this. Tonks went to Hogwarts, was sorted into Hufflepuff, which given who her aunts were is honestly hilarious. And by 1994 had qualified as an Auror after years of grueling training. Andromeda was proud. She was also quietly terrified because having a daughter who hunts dark wizards for a living is not exactly a relaxing situation. Then Voldemort came back. Peter Pettigrew cooked him back into existence using a ritual that somehow managed to restore his entire body, his powers, and his ego, but completely forgot the nose. 14 years of waiting, and the best they could do was a face that looks like it lost an argument with a wall. Anyway, he was back. He called his Death Eaters to him and made a specific point of acknowledging the Lestranges. They had gone to Azkaban rather than deny him, and he intended to honor that with a mass breakout that sent the Ministry into full panic mode. Bellatrix walked back into the world 14 years older, gaunt, skull-like, and burning with the exact same energy she had walked in with. The war was starting again, and this time it was going to be worse, much worse. The war begins again. Now, here's where things start moving very fast. Voldemort needed a prophecy, one that had to do with him and Harry Potter, and he sent Bellatrix and Lucius to the Department of Mysteries to get it. What followed was a disaster, depending on which side you were on. Bellatrix met her niece Tonks for the first time and immediately tried to kill her. They dueled. Bellatrix won, leaving Tonks badly injured. Before that, Tonks managed to stun Lucius, which led directly to his capture. And then in the middle of all of it, Bellatrix killed her own cousin, Sirius Black. Harry chased her afterward and tried to use the Cruciatus Curse on her, the torture curse, out of pure rage. She didn't even flinch. She probably wanted it. She just laughed at him and told him he needed to really mean it, to actually enjoy causing pain before it would work properly, which tells you everything you need to know about Bellatrix Lestrange. Lucius being caught was a turning point for the entire Malfoy family. The name that had stayed clean for years was now front page news. He went to Azkaban, Narcissa was left to manage the fallout alone. And Voldemort, still furious at Lucius for failing the mission and getting himself caught, decided to punish the whole family in the most calculated way possible. He gave their 16-year-old son the task of killing Albus Dumbledore, the greatest wizard of their age. If Draco failed, the consequences would fall on all of them. Narcissa went to Severus Snape and begged him to help. She showed up at his door in tears, convinced that Voldemort was sending a 16-year-old on a mission designed to get him killed, and she wasn't wrong to think so. Snape agreed to help, which should have been the end of it, except Bellatrix was standing right there and pushed for him to make an Unbreakable Vow with Narcissa, the kind of vow that kills you if you break it, just to make sure he meant it. Snape agreed to that, too, and Bellatrix took that as proof of loyalty. It should probably have raised more questions than it answered, but nobody in that room was asking the right ones. Bellatrix spent that year training Draco Occlumency, defensive magic, how to hold himself together under pressure. Draco spent it repairing a Vanishing Cabinet that would allow Death Eaters to enter Hogwarts. When the moment finally came, he got Dumbledore cornered and couldn't go through with it. Snape fulfilled the Unbreakable Vow and killed Dumbledore him- self. As they fled that night, Bellatrix set Hagrid's hut on fire on the way out, because she simply could not leave without doing something completely unnecessary on the way out the door. But the worst was still coming, and the Malfoys were about to find out what it actually meant to have Voldemort living in their house. The Dark Lord came to dinner. After Dumbledore's death, Voldemort's rise was total, and the Malfoys discovered very quickly that having the Dark Lord as a house guest was considerably less glamorous than it sounds. Malfoy Manor became Voldemort's base of operations. Lucius, freed from Azkaban, came home to find his house occupied, his wand taken by Voldemort personally, and his family publicly humiliated at Death Eater meetings where Voldemort casually referenced Andromeda's marriage to a Muggle-born and the family's embarrassing connections to blood traitors. The man who had spent decades wielding the Malfoy name like a weapon was now being mocked with it in his own dining room. Narcissa sat through every meeting with perfect composure and said absolutely nothing. She was very good at that. To give you some context on where Andromeda was during all of this, things were considerably harder on her side. With the Muggle-Born Registration Commission now hunting Muggle-Borns across Britain, Ted was forced to go on the run. Andromeda, protected by her pure-blood status, stayed behind. Alone in the house they had built together, unable to help him, waiting for news. The one piece of brightness in all of that darkness was that Tonks was pregnant. A baby boy was coming. Andromeda was going to be a grandmother. Now, back at Malfoy Manor, Harry, Ron, and Hermione were captured by snatchers and brought directly to Voldemort's dining room. Bellatrix saw one of them carrying a sword she believed was locked in her Gringotts vault and completely lost her marbles. She tortured Hermione demanding to know how they had gotten it. She argued with Narcissa over how to handle Draco.
