The manor, once one of the most prestigious addresses in wizarding Britain, had become something between a prison and a war room, and nobody inside it was remotely comfortable. Then Dobby, the Malfoys' former house-elf, the same they had treated as property for years, showed up and got everyone out. As they disappeared, Bellatrix threw a knife into the chaos and it found Dobby on the way out. He died in Harry's arms on a beach shortly after, which is one of the most quietly devastating moments in the entire series. Voldemort was furious that Harry had slipped through their hands again, and the Malfoys, whose home it was, whose elf it had been, bore the full weight of that fury. And then the news came that Ted Tonks had been killed while on the run. Andromeda had lost her husband. She was now facing the rest of the war entirely alone with a daughter fighting in it and a grandchild who had just arrived in the world. She held onto Teddy with everything she had left, but the war was not finished with any of them yet. And what was coming next would define each sister permanently, the Battle of Hogwarts. When Voldemort marched on Hogwarts, all three sisters ended up in the same place for the last time. Narcissa had already made her position clear. Before the battle properly started, she gave Draco her wand. His had been taken by Harry at the manor leaving herself completely wandless in the middle of a war. She wasn't there for Voldemort, she was there for her son and she needed him to be able to defend himself. When Voldemort cast the killing curse at Harry in the Forbidden Forest and Harry fell, it was Narcissa who stepped forward to check if he was dead. She leaned down, checked his pulse, and whispered to him, "Is Draco alive?" Harry nodded, and Narcissa stood up and told Voldemort that Harry Potter was dead. The most important lie of the entire war, told quietly in the middle of the Forbidden Forest by a woman who had spent years on the wrong side of everything for no reason other than her son was still breathing somewhere inside that castle, and she needed to get to him. That one decision changed the outcome of the war. Meanwhile, Bellatrix was still fighting. She was the last Death Eater standing beside Voldemort, which honestly tracks perfectly. She cast the killing curse at Ginny Weasley and came close enough that it enraged Ginny's mother, Molly. Now, Molly Weasley is not someone you want to enrage, as it turns out. What followed was brief. Molly killed Bellatrix with a curse straight to the chest. Voldemort screamed when it happened, a man who had spent his entire life claiming he felt nothing screaming at the death of one person. Draw your own conclusions. Earlier in the battle, Bellatrix had killed Tonks. Remus Lupin was also killed. Andromeda lost her daughter and her son-in-law on the same night, less than a year after losing her husband. She was left with an infant grandson named Teddy, barely a few months old already without his parents. Harry Potter, who had lost his own parents at 1 year old and had grown up knowing exactly what that felt like, stepped forward as Teddy's godfather. It was his way of honoring Remus and Tonks. After the war, the Malfoys were forgiven. They had stopped fighting. Narcissa's lie hadn't helped it end, and the court took note. Lucius and Narcissa walked through that battle looking for their son, not for Voldemort. That mattered. Narcissa eventually became a grandmother to Scorpius Malfoy when Draco married Astoria Greengrass. Andromeda raised Teddy the way she believed her daughter would have wanted. Narcissa says you're ready for the next lesson here at Stickman Hogwarts. All 24 major events you missed after the Deathly Hallows. Click now before she makes you take the Unbreakable Vow.


=== What Happened to EVERY Couple After The Deathly Hallows EXPLAINED in Detail ===
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=P8Z5zPP4CKI

Draco Malfoy and Astoria Greengrass. Of all the post-war love stories in the wizarding world, nobody saw Draco Malfoy's coming. The boy who spent six years making Harry Potter's life miserable, who wore the Dark Mark on his arm at 16, who nearly destroyed everything, ended up building something genuinely good and then lost half of it in the most heartbreaking way. After the war, most people expected the entire Malfoy family to end up in Azkaban and honestly, they probably deserved it. But Narcissa had already saved the entire wizarding world with one whispered lie in the Forbidden Forest and Lucius provided the Ministry with detailed information on surviving Death Eaters in exchange for his freedom. Extremely on brand for a Malfoy. So, the Malfoys walked away, kept the manor, kept the fortune and tried to figure out what came next. Draco Malfoy married Astoria Greengrass, younger sister of Daphne Greengrass, a pureblood witch from a respectable wizarding family who had grown up surrounded by the same blood supremacy culture as Draco and had rejected it completely. She brought that rejection directly into Malfoy Manor, which caused considerable tension with Lucius and Narcissa, particularly Lucius who considered Astoria's attitude toward blood purity a personal insult. Together, they had one son named Scorpius and Draco made a deliberate and conscious decision to raise him differently. Kindness, respect for Muggle-borns, none of the ideology that had defined the Malfoy name for generations. It was a quiet but complete repudiation of everything his father had taught him, done not through grand speeches but through how he raised his child every single day. Then Astoria died young from a hereditary blood curse that had weakened her health throughout her life, leaving Draco to raise Scorpius alone in Malfoy Manor. The house that had once been Voldemort's headquarters, filled with dark artifacts and pureblood portraits, became the place where a single father raised a kind, thoughtful boy who grew up to be nothing like the Malfoy legacy suggested he should be. Not a dramatic redemption, slower and messier than that, but real. Harry Potter and Ginny Weasley. The films did Ginny Weasley dirty and that is simply a fact. In the books, she was never the quiet, supportive girlfriend waiting in the background. She was sharp, talented, and had completely gotten on with her own life by the time Harry finally noticed her. And yes, they did break up. Harry ended things at Dumbledore's funeral specifically because Voldemort would have used her against him and he was not willing to put her in that position. Ginny understood. She waited anyway. By around 2002, they were married making Harry a permanent member of the loudest, most chaotic family in wizarding Britain, which she had basically been an honorary member of since he was 11. They had three children. James Sirius, the eldest, who inherited his grandfather's talent for mischief and made it his personal mission to cause maximum chaos at Hogwarts, named after Harry's father and godfather. Then Albus Severus, quiet and thoughtful, who became best friends with Scorpius Malfoy and was sorted into Slytherin, which required Harry to have a very honest conversation with himself about house prejudice, named after two headmasters, one of whom Harry spent six years convinced he hated. And Lily Luna, the youngest, named after his mother and the friend who taught him that being different was never something to apologize for. Harry Potter, the boy who grew up in a cupboard with no family and no birthday cake, named every single one of his children after someone he had lost. If that doesn't get you, then nothing in the series will. Financially, the Potters were never struggling. Harry inherited his parents' Gringotts vault and the entire Black family fortune from Sirius. Ginny played professional Quidditch for the Holyhead Harpies, which in the wizarding world carries the same financial weight as the NBA here, before pivoting to journalism. The cupboard-to-castle pipeline, remarkable. George and Angelina Johnson. George Weasley lost Fred at the Battle of Hogwarts and then kept going anyway, which is either the bravest anyone did in the aftermath of that war or the most heartbreaking. And the answer is probably both simultaneously. He married Angelina Johnson, a former Gryffindor Quidditch player and chaser who had previously dated Fred, which sounds complicated until you understand that grief and love rarely follow the rules people set out for them. And George and Angelina had both loved the same person and lost him in the same moment. They had two children together, a daughter named Roxanne and a son named Fred, because George was never going to let that name disappear quietly into history when he had the power to carry it forward. Fred the Second grew up with his uncle's name and his father's humor, which means the legacy continued in the most direct way George could manage. Weasleys' Wizard Wheezes thrived after the war, expanding across the wizarding world and becoming one of the most successful businesses in Diagon Alley, to the eternal frustration of every teacher who kept finding joke products in their classrooms. George carried Fred forward not just in name, but in every product, every joke, and every time someone laughed because of something he made. It cost him something every single day. He did it anyway. Neville and Hannah Abbott. Not every post-war love story involved reforming governments or catching dark wizards. Some of them just involved a pub, a greenhouse, and two people who had earned a quiet life. After the war, Neville briefly joined the Auror Office, realized his passion had always been Herbology, and returned to Hogwarts as a professor under the woman who had once watched him melt 17 cauldrons in her classroom. McGonagall hired him anyway. That says everything about both of them. He married Hannah Abbott, a fellow Dumbledore's Army member and former Hufflepuff, which the internet had not predicted because the films had spent considerable effort suggesting he would end up with Luna. He did not. Hannah eventually became the landlady of the Leaky Cauldron and later took over as Hogwarts matron when Madam Pomfrey retired in 2014, which meant that at some point Neville Longbottom and his wife were both working at Hogwarts simultaneously while also running a pub. The logistics of that schedule alone deserve some kind of recognition. Neville was also named godfather to Albus Severus Potter, Harry's middle son, which means the boy who was once too frightened to stand up to his own friends ended up as a godfather, a professor, and the head of Gryffindor house. The Sorting Hat placed him in Gryffindor on day one. It absolutely always knew. Their life was quiet, grounded, and completely without drama. Luna, however, took a considerably different approach to settling down. Luna and Rolf Scamander. Luna Lovegood spent her Hogwarts years being dismissed as strange, dreamy, and slightly disconnected from reality by people who would later discover she was one of the most perceptive and genuinely original thinkers in the entire school. After the war, she became a magizoologist, traveled the world documenting magical creatures, and yes, she spent a meaningful amount of time searching for the Crumple-Horned Snorkack, a creature the rest of the wizarding world remained firmly convinced did not exist. She never found it. She found everything else, though, discovering and classifying entirely new magical species that expanded wizarding knowledge in ways nobody else had thought to try. She married Rolf Scamander, grandson of Newt Scamander, the man who literally wrote the book on magical creatures. Luna Lovegood, the girl everyone quietly laughed at in the corridors, married into the most famous magizoologist family in wizarding history. The universe definitely has a sense of humor. They had twin sons named Lorcan and Lysander and raised them in the environment where being unconventional was not just accepted, but actively encouraged, which honestly, given their parents, was never really in question. Luna waited until later in life to start her family, meaning her sons were younger than the children of Harry, Ron, and Hermione, which only added to the feeling that Luna had always been operating on her own schedule, completely unbothered by what anyone else was doing. Now, Ron and Hermione's story is just as solid, but considerably funnier. Ron and Hermione. Ron and Hermione might be the most debated couple in Harry Potter history. Half the fandom wanted Hermione with Harry. A surprisingly vocal portion wanted her with Draco, which is a whole separate conversation. Rowling herself admitted she sometimes wondered if she made the right call. But from the moment Ron looked at Hermione at the Yule Ball like someone had physically knocked the air out of him, it was always going to be them. Seven years of being completely obvious to everyone except each other. They just needed a war to finally do something about it. The wedding was exactly what you would expect. Hermione arrived prepared and composed. Ron got so drunk before the ceremony that his memory was mostly hazy. And when asked if he would take Hermione as his wife, reportedly responded with, "I think I do." Romantic. They had two children, Rose and Hugo Granger-Weasley, both sorted into Gryffindor. Hermione made sure the children carried her last name alongside Ron's. She had spent years fighting for equality. She was not leaving her name out of her own family. Rose inherited Hermione's academic discipline, which Ron took enormous pride in pointing out at every opportunity. Hugo remained slightly less documented, which honestly might make him the most well-adjusted person in the entire family. Both parents took active roles in raising them, though their approach differed considerably. Ron constantly highlighted how Rose's intelligence clearly came from Hermione. Hermione focused on their education with the same terrifying dedication she brought to everything else. The children were presumably exhausted and also very well prepared for life. Shortly after the war, Hermione had restored her parents' memories in Australia, meaning Rose and Hugo grew up with devoted Muggle grandparents, which Ron found equal parts confusing and genuinely wonderful. Ron joined the Auror Office briefly before leaving to help George run Weasleys' Wizard Wheezes, which was honestly the right call for everyone involved. Hermione predictably went on to become Minister for Magic. Now, here's the detail that makes this relationship feel completely real. At some point after the war, Ron decided he wanted to learn to drive a Muggle car, a completely reasonable ambition. Except for Ron being, well, Ron, he used a Confundus Charm to pass the driving test rather than actually learning the material. Hermione, the woman who had dismantled pureblood discrimination laws and reformed the entire Ministry, was reportedly furious about that specific thing. Not the dark wizard hunting, not the Horcrux camping trip, the driving license. Their relationship is one story. Ron's proudest moment, according to Rowling, was getting a chocolate frog card. Hermione is still processing that information. Percy and Audrey. Percy Weasley's redemption arc can be summarized in one sentence. He chose the Ministry over his family for years, watched the Ministry collapse under a Dark Lord, showed up at the Battle of Hogwarts, cracked a joke, and his entire family forgave him on the spot. Turns out one well-timed joke did what years of apology letters probably could not. After the war, Percy rose to head of the Department of Magical Transportation under Kingsley Shacklebolt, which is exactly the steady, respectable, slightly unglamorous career Percy was always going to end up in, regardless of what happened around him. He married a witch named Audrey, about whom Rowling has revealed almost nothing publicly, which means Audrey either possessed the patience of an absolute saint or genuinely enjoyed listening to Ministry Transportation Regulations discussed at dinner. Possibly both. They had two daughters named Molly and Lucy, and Percy rebuilt his family relationships quietly and without grand gestures, simply by showing up consistently, which for Percy Weasley was genuinely the harder thing to do. The mystery of Audrey is honestly one of the more entertaining loose ends in post-war wizarding history. She married into the most chaotic family in Britain, raised two daughters, and managed to avoid being documented in any meaningful way. Impressive work for someone married to Percy Weasley. Bill and Fleur. Bill and Fleur meanwhile had built the most stable and thoroughly documented home in the entire Weasley family, which given the competition was a considerable achievement. Bill Weasley survived a werewolf attack, married a part Veela French witch over his mother's initial reservations, fought in a war, and came out the other side looking completely unruffled, which suggests Bill Weasley is simply built differently from most people and has always known it. After the war he and Fleur settled permanently at Shell Cottage and raised three children named Victoire, Dominique, and Louis, combining Weasley stubbornness with Veela elegance in a household that was probably spectacular and chaotic in equal measure. Bill continued working as a curse-breaker for Gringotts, occasionally traveling abroad but always returning home. While Fleur was decorated by the French Ministry of Magic for her wartime bravery and chose family life over any further public recognition. People had spent years underestimating Fleur Delacour because she was beautiful and French and therefore assumed to be decorative, conveniently forgetting that she had competed against dragons and merpeople in the Triwizard Tournament at 15. Post-war she simply chose where her life was going and nobody stopped her. Their eldest daughter Victoire became the first member of the next generation to make Wizarding World headlines, though not for anything she did professionally. That particular story belongs to the boy who grew up surrounded by both the Potter and Weasley families and somehow managed to make falling in love a public event witnessed by the most enthusiastic 11-year-old at the station. Remus and Tonks. Remus Lupin and Nymphadora Tonks, who hated being called Nymphadora and made absolutely sure everyone knew it, had one of the most quietly moving love stories in the entire series and one of its most devastating endings. Remus spent years convinced he was too old, too poor, and too dangerous to be with anyone, a belief so deeply embedded that even after Tonks made her feelings completely clear, he spent the better part of a year talking himself out of accepting them. Tonks, who was funny and warm and entirely underrated by Remus's extensive list of reasons it would not work, simply waited him out. She was right, he eventually came around. They married, had a son named Teddy, and both died at the Battle of Hogwarts within what is believed to have been a short time of each other, leaving behind a baby boy who never got to know either of them. Teddy was raised by his grandmother, Andromeda Tonks, who had already lost her husband Ted during the war and now raised her grandson alone with Harry as godfather, showing up throughout his childhood to make sure Teddy always knew who his parents were and how completely they had loved him. Remus and Tonks did not get the post-war story they deserved, but what they left behind was a boy who grew up loved, surrounded by families his parents had fought for, carrying their names forward into a world they helped save. Some stories end too early, that does not make them any less worth telling. Teddy and Victoire. Teddy Lupin was orphaned at birth, inherited his mother Tonks's Metamorphmagus abilities, meaning he could change his appearance entirely at will, and grew up so thoroughly embedded in the Potter and Weasley families that what happened next was honestly inevitable to anyone except apparently Teddy and Victoire themselves. Harry took his godfather responsibilities seriously from the beginning, showing up consistently throughout Teddy's childhood in a way that ensured he never felt the particular loneliness of growing up without parents, even if the absence was always there underneath everything. By his Hogwarts years, Teddy had developed a romantic interest in Victoire Weasley, Bill and Fleur's eldest daughter, and was reportedly changing his hair color to match her favorite shade, which is either the most romantic or most chaotic use of Metamorphmagus abilities depending on your perspective. The relationship became public knowledge not through any announcement or quiet family conversation, but because James Sirius Potter, 11 years old and constitutionally incapable of minding his own business, spotted them kissing at platform 9 and 3/4 and immediately reported this information to absolutely everyone within hearing distance. James Potter inherited chaos from both sides of his family tree and deployed it without hesitation or apology. Teddy and Victoire's relationship linked the Lupin and Weasley families in the next generation in a way that Remus and Tonks, had they lived, would have found either completely wonderful or mildly overwhelming. Probably both. Hagrid and Madame Maxime. The films really pushed the romance between Rubeus Hagrid and Olympe Maxime. And honestly, it was easy to see why. Two half-giants, both warm and larger than life in every sense, thrown together during the Triwizard Tournament with an obvious mutual attraction and what appeared to be genuine chemistry. The films leaned into it and audiences went along with it completely. The truth after the Deathly Hallows is considerably less cinematic. Rowling confirmed that the relationship between Hagrid and Madame Maxime did not last. Nobody knows exactly why and Hagrid never really talked about it, which is on brand for a man who expresses everything through feelings and very little through words. Hagrid never married. His great loves were always Hogwarts, the creatures under his care and the people he called family, even when they kept nearly getting themselves killed in ways that gave him genuine grief. He was still in his hut on the edge of the Forbidden Forest in 2017, approaching 90 years old, still keeping creatures that had absolutely no business being near a school, and still inviting Harry's son, Albus Potter, to tea, exactly the way he had done for Harry three decades earlier. Madame Maxime continued leading Beauxbatons with dignity and strength into the post-war era. They were not each other's ending, but for a brief moment during one very eventful school year, they were exactly what the other needed. And in the wizarding world, sometimes that is enough. And speaking of endings, here are all 24 major events you missed after the Deathly Hallows explained. That's your next lesson here at Stickman Hogwarts. Click now before Ron says, "I think I do." again.


=== How the RICHEST Families in Harry Potter Got Rich EXPLAINED in Detail ===
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=9tO55XBU0nc

The Potters. Remember the scene where Harry sees his enormous pile of gold for the very first time? We were all shocked, including him. So, where did all that money come from? It started in the 12th century with Linfred of Stinchcombe, a quiet wizard living in Cotswold village who brewed medicinal potions and sold them to local Muggles. And in case you didn't know, Linfred is the very first Potter on the family tree. The Muggles assumed he was a slightly eccentric herbalist with surprisingly effective remedies. His neighbors affectionately nicknamed him the Potterer, and his descendants eventually polished that nickname into a proper surname. Several of Linfred's homemade concoctions became the foundation for staples still sitting in every Hogwarts hospital wing today, including early versions of Skele-Gro and Pepper-Up Potion. Translation, the entire Potter dynasty is funded by medieval cold and flu remedies that worked way too well. The gold from those early sales got reinvested so well that by the time Linfred died, he was leaving each of his children a small fortune. The seed money for every Potter vault that followed. Then Fleamont Potter showed up, Harry's grandfather, and invented Sleekeazy's Hair Sleeking Potion, the same product Hermione would later use to tame her hair for the 1994 Yule Ball, and watched it grow into a full wizarding beauty empire. He quadrupled the existing family gold in a single lifetime, sold the company at a tidy profit when he retired, and left James a vault that comfortably covered 7 years of Hogwarts, a lifetime supply of Quidditch gear, and the kind of inheritance that lets an orphan 11-year-old walk into Gringotts and never once flinch at a price tag. The Ollivanders. The Ollivanders have been making wands since 382 BC, which makes the family business older than the Roman invasion of Britain, older than most European countries, and older than Hogwarts itself by roughly 13 centuries. For nearly 2,400 years, if a British witch or wizard needed a wand, they walked into an Ollivander shop. Other wand makers existed on the continent, but in Britain, the Ollivander name owned the market so completely that competition barely registered. Generations of wizards, every Hogwarts first-year for centuries, all funneled through one dusty shop run by one family. The craftsmanship earned the dominance. Ollivanders specialized in three wand cores: unicorn hair, dragon heartstring, and phoenix feather, and turned the wand chooses the wizard philosophy into a brand identity. And here's what the Ollivanders actually did right. They kept the bloodline deliberately small. While the Blacks were producing enough cousins to fill a tapestry the size of a wall, the Ollivanders ran the workshop on a strict one heir per generation policy. The craft passed from father to son, the shop passed from father to son, and the vault passed from father to son. Undivided, undiluted, intact. They married carefully, the kind of strategic pureblood matches that kept the Ollivander name welcome at every important vault in Britain, and then quietly handed the entire wand-making operation to a single son each generation. The best monopoly in the wizarding world is the one nobody else figured out how to copy. The Malfoys. The Malfoy fortune is the wizarding world's masterclass in being on the right side of every regime change starting in 1066. Armand Malfoy crossed the English Channel with William the Conqueror. Most wizards of that era hid in forests and pretended Muggle politics didn't matter. Armand did the opposite. He attached himself directly to the new Norman court, performed the kind of magical services nobody put in writing, and walked away with a vast estate in Wiltshire as his reward. The land became Malfoy Manor, and it has been in the family ever since. For the next 10 centuries, the Malfoys made themselves useful to whoever currently held power. Ministry officials, royals, dark lords, school governors. Donations flowed in every direction at once. Whoever won the next civil war always found a Malfoy thank-you owl in their morning post. Lucius refined the formula in the modern era. Public face, charitable patron of St. Mungo's, school governor at Hogwarts, model citizen of magical Britain, private inventory, cursed objects, dark artifacts, and a cane that hides a wand because even his accessories are flexing. After the first wizarding war, the Malfoys walked free from Azkaban on the official position that they were all under the Imperius Curse. Okay. After the second wizarding war, same defense, same result. Vaults intact, manor intact, social standing somehow still intact. Even after backing a noseless tyrant twice, no asset seizures, and no prison time. The Malfoys built the only fortune in Britain that has survived every regime change, every war, and every embarrassing photograph by being too useful to prosecute. The Scamanders. So, Newt was a magizoologist by training, which in the early 20th century was barely a recognized profession. He spent years traveling the world cataloging, studying, and rescuing magical creatures, often at significant personal risk. The result was Fantastic Beasts and Where to Find Them, published in 1927 and adopted as a required textbook at Hogwarts within years of release. It stayed on the syllabus for nearly a century. That detail is the entire fortune. A required textbook in the British wizarding curriculum prints money on autopilot. Every Hogwarts student buys a copy every year for decades. Royalties from a mandatory school text inside a closed magical economy work out to passive income stream most authors would commit minor crimes to access. Newt also held a senior position at the Ministry of Magic in the Beast division, contributed to international magical legislation around creature welfare, and was awarded the Order of Merlin Second Class for his work. Government salary, recognition pay, lecture circuits, expert consultations, all stacked on top of the textbook royalties. His marriage to Tina Goldstein, a former Macusa Auror, added another professional income to the household. By the time the next Scamander generation arrives, the family is comfortably established as old academic money, the wizarding equivalent of a Cambridge dynasty with a publishing empire attached. Their grandson Rolf marries Luna Lovegood, merging two strange and reasonably wealthy magical lines into one household that probably has the weirdest dinner conversations in Britain. The Scamanders are the wealthiest example of study what you love in magical history. The Weasleys. The Weasleys' fortune starts when Harry Potter gives Fred and George the Triwizard Tournament prize money. The twins take the thousand straight to Diagon Alley and open Weasleys' Wizard Wheezes. Within months, the shop is outselling almost everything around it. Skiving Snackboxes, Daydream Charms, Decoy Detonators, Extendable Ears, Peruvian Instant Darkness Powder, and the WonderWitch love potion line all flying off the shelves at once. Diagon Alley had not seen a business move that fast in decades. The Ministry had already commissioned 500 Shield Hats and Cloaks for its staff before the final battle was even over. After the war, the demand only grew. A wizarding world that had spent a year banned from laughing had some catching up to do. Then comes the Battle of Hogwarts, and Fred doesn't walk out of it. Weasleys' nearly closes for good. Ron eventually walks away from his Auror career and steps in as co-owner alongside George, helping rebuild the entire operation into a proper retail empire. A Hogsmeade branch and an expanding product catalog that keeps printing galleons for decades. And if we look closely, the Weasleys have always been rich. The catch, old money rich doesn't go very far when you're splitting it between nine people. The Burrow has been in the family for generations, fully owned, sitting on its own quietly prosperous plot of land near Ottery Saint Catchpole. The Weasleys themselves are Sacred Twenty-Eight aristocracy, same official registry as the Malfoys, the Blacks, and the Lestranges. Molly was born a Prewett, another ancient pureblood line. After her brothers died, the rest of the Prewett inheritance came with her into the marriage, adding a second old vault to the Weasley household. So, stack the old Weasley land, the Prewett vault, and George's empire together, and by the time the next generation reaches Hogwarts, the family vault looks nothing like the one Ron grew up with. The Weasley name finally stops being a punchline about counting Knuts. The Abbotts. The Abbotts are one of those Sacred Twenty-Eight families that spend most of their history staying carefully out of the headlines. The story goes that the original Abbott money came from the standard Sacred Twenty-Eight playbook, ancestral land, a quiet vault at Gringotts, and the kind of generational stability that pureblood Britain treats as the baseline for old families. Then Hannah Abbott rewrote the family's financial future by taking control of the most strategically located building in magical Britain. The Leaky Cauldron is the only facing entrance to Diagon Alley from Muggle London. Hogwarts students on summer shopping trips, Ministry visitors arriving from out of town, foreign tourists, weekend shoppers, the occasional confused Muggle who somehow ended up at the right brick wall, and every single Muggle-born first-year receiving their introduction to the wizarding world. All of them passed through the Leaky Cauldron at some point. The pub holds an unbreakable monopoly on the single most important piece of magical infrastructure in the country, the doorway between two worlds. Hannah went to work at the Leaky Cauldron after Hogwarts and eventually became its landlady, the title traditionally held by the person who owns or runs a British pub. Her marriage to Neville Longbottom, heir to the entire Longbottom estate, which had survived the war with its old pureblood fortune intact, gave the household a second source of stability on top of the pub income. Between Neville's family vault and Hannah's daily takings from the busiest crossing point in magical Britain, the post-war Abbott-Longbottom household became one of the quieter financial success stories of the next generation. The Gaunts. The Gaunts are direct descendants of Salazar Slytherin, one of the four founders of Hogwarts, and Slytherin's personal estate is the seed. He left behind land, magical artifacts, and a private library that the family carried forward through every generation. The most famous surviving piece is Slytherin's locket, solid gold set with emeralds shaped into a serpentine S. On the open magical antiques market, an authenticated founder era artifact like that would have been valued at thousands of Galleons. The second heirloom was Marvolo Gaunt's ring, a heavy gold band set with a black stone carved with the Peverell coat of arms. The Gaunts wore the ring as proof of pure-blood pedigree from two of the oldest names in magical Britain, completely unaware that the black stone set into it was the Resurrection Stone itself. The land was the quiet engine. For centuries, the Gaunts held property near Little Hangleton, including a substantial estate that produced rental income from the surrounding magical and Muggle tenants. Then there's the social capital. Being a verified descendant of Salazar Slytherin in the wizarding world is the closest thing to magical royalty. For most of the family's history, the Gaunt name unlocked invitations, marriage offers, political deference, and quiet financial favors from the other Sacred 28 households who wanted Slytherin blood somewhere in their family tree. For the better part of a thousand years, the Gaunts coasted on this stack. Then came the inbreeding. Generations of marrying cousins hollowed the family out genetically and financially, and by the time Marvolo Gaunt was born, the estate had been sold off. The manor was a collapsing shack, and the only thing left was the locket on his daughter's neck and the ring on his own finger. The Burkes. Caractacus Burke founded Borgin and Burkes in Knockturn Alley as a partnership with the Borgin family, and the business model was brilliant in its simplicity. Cursed necklaces, hands of glory, opal jewelry that had killed multiple owners, vanishing cabinets with one-way connections to other vanishing cabinets, and the kind of inherited heirlooms that respectable dealers in Diagon Alley refused to touch on principle. The financial logic was brutal. Dark artifacts have terrible reputations and microscopic supply, which means margins are enormous. Caractacus famously bought Slytherin's locket from a desperate pregnant Merope Gaunt, aka Voldemort's mom, for 10 Galleons, an artifact later valued at thousands. That single transaction tells you everything about how the original Burke fortune was assembled. Buy desperate, sell discreet, pocket the difference, and never ask where the merchandise came from. The family climbed the magical social ladder in the practical way. Herbert Burke married Belvina Black, daughter of one of the oldest pure-blood houses in Britain, and the marriage handed the Burkes exactly the legitimacy their gold could not buy on its own. The Lestrange's. The original Lestrange fortune was built the same way the Malfoy fortune was built in the same century by the same kind of opportunism. The Lestrange's arrived in Britain from France during the Norman Conquest and immediately attached themselves to the new ruling court. Norman wizards who made themselves useful to William the Conqueror walked away with land grants carved out of confiscated Anglo-Saxon estates, and the Lestrange's walked away with enough property to never need a paying job again. That land became the foundation of every Galleon the family would ever own. From there, the Lestrange's grew the fortune through one specific strategy, marriage stacking. For centuries, they intermarried with other elite pure-blood houses, especially the Blacks and the Rosiers, and every wedding brought a fresh dowry and a fresh merger of vaults into a single consolidated bloodline. Romance was a secondary consideration. The Lestrange's never had to build a business, invent a product, or sell anything to grow their gold. Inheritance did the work. The fortune compounded purely through dowries, marriage contracts, and the centuries-long pure-blood rule that gold only marries gold. The Flamel's. Nicholas Flamel was born in France in 1330, trained as a scribe and bookseller in medieval Paris, and spent the first century of his career as a respectable but unremarkable member of the magical community. Then sometime in the late 1300s, he became the only wizard in recorded history to successfully complete the Philosopher's Stone, and the Flamel fortune started compounding immediately. The stone produced two outputs that broke the rules of every other fortune on this list. The first was unlimited gold on demand. Drop in any base material, iron, lead, copper, walk away with the same weight in pure Galleon grade gold. There was no upper limit, no diminishing returns, no scarcity to manage. The second output was the elixir of life brewed directly from the stone, which kept Nicholas and his wife Perenelle alive and healthy for the next 600 years. The math is what makes the Flamel's uncatchable. Every other rich wizarding family on this list grew through interest, monopoly, or inheritance, all of which take generations to compound. Nicholas and Perenelle had 665 years of personal compounding time, 665 years of producing gold whenever they wanted, investing it however they wanted, and never once dying and splitting the inheritance among quarreling heirs. By the modern era, the Flamel household had been quietly running the most efficient gold operation in magical history for longer than most countries had existed. When Voldemort came for the stone in 1992, Nicholas and Perenelle made the call to destroy it. Dumbledore's exact words were that the Flamel's had enough elixir left to set their affairs in order, and then they would die. After 665 years of unlimited gold, they walked away from the operation by choice, the only family on this list whose fortune ended on their own terms. So, the gold's been counted. Now for everything else. All 24 major events you missed after the Deathly Hallows explained. That's your next lesson here at Stickman Hogwarts. Click now before McGonagall takes points for being late.


=== All 18 MAJOR Spells Used in The Deathly Hallows EXPLAINED in Detail ===
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=Ry2oznDehKY

Piertotum Locomotor. Piertotum Locomotor is the moment every Potterhead sat up and realized Hogwarts had been hiding a fully operational medieval security system this entire time. In Harry Potter and the Deathly Hallows, the battle is about to begin. Dumbledore is gone. Voldemort is outside. The vibe's apocalyptic. Then Professor Minerva McGonagall steps forward, points her hand at the statues lining the castle walls, and calmly says, Piertotum Locomotor! Suddenly, every suit of armor, every stone knight, every gargoyle with centuries of silent judgment springs to life. Like Hogwarts just activated defense mode. Stone feet step down from pedestals that have held them for centuries. The corridors echo with the heavy thuds of marching armor as ranks of enchanted guardians begin to move, making their way toward the battlements to defend the castle they have silently watched over for generations. Then, with the quiet satisfaction of someone finally checking off an item from a centuries-long to-do list, she adds, "I've always wanted to use that spell." By the end of that scene, the one thing is clear. Dumbledore may have been the most powerful wizard of his time, but Minerva McGonagall stands right beside that legacy. She's the woman who turns a school into an impenetrable fortress without breaking a sweat. Fun fact, Piertotum Locomotor is the same kind of animation magic used to bring the life-size wizard chess set to life in Harry Potter and the Philosopher's Stone. And if you read the books, you'll know Albus Dumbledore later uses similar magic to animate the statues of the fountain of magical brethren to protect Harry during his duel with Lord Voldemort at the Ministry. Fire spells. Not all magical fire is created equal. Deathly Hallows gives us three different flavors, each progressively worse for anyone standing nearby. First, Incendio and related fire-conjuring charms simply produce flames. Less catastrophic, but still a very clear signal that the conversation is over. Then there is Confringo, the blasting curse. Hermione Granger uses it during the Tottenham Court Road cafe duel to send explosive bursts of fire at attackers. Finally, Fiendfyre. This is a cursed, semi-sentient magical fire that devours everything in its path. In the Room of Requirement, Vincent Crabbe unleashes it while chasing Harry Potter for the diadem. The result is a gigantic inferno shaped like serpents and beasts that quickly proves impossible to control. The weird part about Fiendfyre is that even the wizard who casts it can't control it, and can even be killed by it. Sorry, Crabbe. Expecto Patronum. Expecto Patronum is the ultimate get away from Dementor spell. It conjures a Patronus, a magical guardian that not only blocks Dementors, but also chases them off entirely. In Deathly Hallows, members of the Order of the Phoenix use it repeatedly during battles and ambushes to protect themselves and others, turning fear into literal silver light. Harry famously cast his stag Patronus while Hermione Granger, Ron Weasley, and other Order members summon theirs to defend the group against Dementors and minor dark creatures during skirmishes. One fun fact about Expecto Patronum, Patronuses are deeply personal. They reflect the caster's inner character. Trying to force one without happy memories usually fails. It's also the ultimate get lost, creepy soul sucker spell, turning despair into dazzling corporeal silver light. In essence, it's hope made magic, and arguably the most cheerful way to fight fear in wizarding battles. Salvio Hexia. Salvio Hexia is basically wizarding bug spray for hexes, a magical perimeter that politely tells hostile spells to take a hike. The incantation creates a protective enchantment around an area that deflects or weakens incoming hexes, making it perfect for setting up a temporary safe zone when enemies might be lurking nearby. In Deathly Hallows, Hermione repeatedly cast Salvio Hexia while camping with Harry and Ron during their Horcrux hunt. She layers it on with other defensive spells around their tent, so snatchers, Death Eaters, and random dark magic cannot easily ruin their night. Not the flashiest spell in the series, but when wizarding world is hunting you, magical bug spray suddenly becomes essential surviving gear. Obliviate. Have you ever wished you could just erase all those embarrassing moments that kept you up at 2:00 a.m.? Well, that's exactly what the Obliviate spell or Memory Charm is for. Point your wand, cast the charm, and poof, specific memories vanish from someone's mind like they never happened. In Deathly Hallows, this spell gets some heavy use. First, Hermione Granger uses it on two Death Eaters, Dolohov and Rowle, in a London cafe after an ambush, wiping their memories to keep the trio off their radar. But in Deathly Hallows part two comes the gut-punch moment when Hermione casts Obliviate on her own parents, erasing her existence from their minds to protect them from Voldemort. Being a superhero in the wizarding world is not exactly a chill career choice. Did you know Obliviate is the go-to spell for dealing with Muggle witnesses? Saw a wizard performing magic on a train? Even spotted a house-elf doing the laundry in the open? Obliviate. Saw someone cheating? Yeah, maybe let that memory stay and grab popcorn for the drama. Repello Inimicum. When the Battle of Hogwarts begins, subtle magic goes out the window, and the castle's defenses go into full lockdown. One of the key spells used is Repello Inimicum, literally meaning repel the enemy. So, if you're running from an ex, this is your spell go-to. In Deathly Hallows, Minerva McGonagall leads the effort to shield the castle, casting Repello Inimicum alongside Protego Maxima and Fianto Duri to reinforce Hogwarts' magical perimeter. Together, these spells strengthen the castle's defenses and create powerful barriers around the grounds. Unlike standard Protego spells, which mainly block incoming attacks, Repello Inimicum is designed specifically to push enemies away from a protected area. Arresto Momentum. Arresto Momentum is the wizarding world's emergency brake. The spell slows the motion of a falling object or person, reducing impact before gravity turns the situation into a hospital visit. In Harry Potter and the Deathly Hallows, the spell appears during the infiltration of Gringotts Wizarding Bank. As the trio escapes on the back of the dragon, they leap from a massive height into the lake below, and Harry casts Arresto Momentum to slow their fall before impact. Not the flashiest spell in the series, but when you are plummeting off a dragon, flashy is not the priority. Survival is. Real Potterheads might remember first seeing Arresto Momentum clearly in Harry Potter and the Prisoner of Azkaban when Albus Dumbledore silently cast it to save Harry Potter after Dementors knock him off his broom during a Quidditch match. Expelliarmus. The disarming charm that every fan, newbie, or veteran knows by heart. Point your wand, swish, yell Expelliarmus, and bam, your opponent's wand goes flying through the air like a confused owl. Harry first learned it during a practice duel with Draco in Chamber of Secrets. And from that moment on, it's basically his go-to in every duel. In Deathly Hallows part one, Harry's Expelliarmus moments reach legendary status. First, during the Battle of the Seven Potters. Picture this, Harry flying through the sky pursued by dark wizards willing to throw every lethal curse imaginable, and he disarms them midair. Epic, right? But deadly, too, because that spell let Snape identify the real Harry. Then the ultimate mic drop, part two. Harry uses it against Voldemort himself, the Dark Lord, the most powerful wizard of all time, and the boy who lived wins with a single Expelliarmus. Imagine decades of magical dominance undone by a spell you learn in second year. Make sense, right? And this is exactly the moment Voldemort probably wished he could just obliviate himself. High-key iconic. Homenum Revelio. Homenum Revelio is essentially a human radar spell. Cast the charm and you can instantly tell if another human is nearby. Instead of flashing bright colors or exploding in sparkles, this spell simply reveals the presence of people around you. The first time we see it in Deathly Hallows is when Hermione uses it after the trio arrives at 12 Grimmauld Place to check they aren't walking into a Death Eater party. Sometimes nothing happens because there's literally no one there. The spell is simple, tactical, and perfect for stealth checks. Think of it as magical people-spotting technology that is way better than yelling, "Is anyone here?" in a spooky old house. Fidelius Charm. The Fidelius Charm is one of the most dramatic secrecy spells in the wizarding world. It doesn't just hide a location or a fact, but locks the secret inside a person's soul. That person becomes the secret keeper, and nobody, not even the people being protected, can reveal the secret unless the keeper allows it. Think of it like wizarding witness protection on magical steroids. Remember when the golden trio escaped Malfoy Manor in Deathly Hallows and landed at Bill and Fleur's Shell Cottage? That cozy hideout wasn't just charming, it was under the Fidelius Charm with Bill Weasley as secret keeper, invisible to prying Death Eaters. It was basically a magical do not disturb zone, safe, secret, and Death Eater proof. Here's some Potterhead trivia. Fidelius is the same charm that hid James and Lily Potter's house during the First Wizarding War. Even when Voldemort, with his nose pressed to every window, well, when he still had one, in Godric's Hollow couldn't find them until the secret keeper, Peter Pettigrew, revealed the location to him. Sectum Sempra. Sectum Sempra is one of those curses that isn't technically classified as dark, but it really should be. Invented by Severus Snape while he was still an emo Hogwarts student, the incantation Sectum Sempra creates wounds as if its invisible sword were slashing its target. It follows the movement of your wand, causing deep gashes and severe bleeding that can be fatal if not treated immediately. In short, the spell is dangerous, unpredictable, and definitely not something you casually throw out at duels. Canonically, we see it in action during Deathly Hallows when Snape uses it in the Battle of the Seven Potters, accidentally cutting off George Weasley's ear. Fan theories suggest it might even have been the curse Voldemort used to slash Snape's throat at the very end. Here's a bit personal annotated copy of Advanced Potion Making book. During Half-Blood Prince, Harry discovers it, sees the note for enemies, and uses it on a completely unsuspecting Draco. Seriously, Harry, just because you can doesn't mean you should. Relashio. Relashio is the spell you use when you want someone or something to let go fast. It forces release and often produces fiery bursts, especially underwater, making it perfect for escape situations. In Deathly Hallows, the spell is used by the goblins during the Gringotts break-in. When Harry, Hermione, and Ron need to escape from the dragon after retrieving Hufflepuff's cup, goblins use Relashio to trigger underwater explosions, knocking guards and obstacles aside and giving the trio a chance to flee. Simple, effective, and a little dramatic. It's less about finesse and more about get me out of here before I become dragon food. Avada Kedavra. Avada Kedavra is the big red button of spells. Instant death with zero recovery. It's basically a magical you're fired from life. Say the words, green light flashes, target drops, game over. Well, unless you're Harry Potter, of course, and you have a loving mother ready to sacrifice her life for you. Or, you know, a giant rock in the way. In Deathly Hallows, Death Eaters and Lord Voldemort spam it all over the Battle of Hogwarts. Neville nearly takes one before deflecting it with guts and bad timing. Fred Weasley gets hit square in the chest. Rest in peace. Crucio. Crucio, the torture curse that does not mess around. It inflicts excruciating pain without leaving a scratch, turning your opponent into a screaming, writhing mess. In Deathly Hallows, Death Eaters use it during the Battle of the Seven Potters and the Battle of Hogwarts. Not to defeat enemies, but to make them regret every life choice that led them here. Unlike Avada Kedavra, victims can survive. But sometimes surviving feels worse than being dead. Canonically, Neville Longbottom's parents were tortured with Crucio and lost their sanity. Not so fun fact, in the book, J.K. Rowling describes the curse as pain beyond anything Harry had ever experienced. "His very bones were on fire. His head was surely splitting along his scar. He wanted it to end, to black out, to die." Ouch. Imperio. Imperio is the control curse. Say Imperio and the victim's brain becomes your remote control. It's less about pain and more about turning free will into a suggestion with a very ugly punchline. In Deathly Hallows, it's used on Pius Thicknesse at the Ministry's Death Eaters can walk through the front door like they have appointments. Imperio also hits goblins during the Gringotts break-in, so the dragon doesn't immediately vaporize everyone. One interesting fact about Imperio is that victims under Imperio can be utterly convinced they're doing something normal, like a Muggle trying to assemble IKEA furniture. Disillusionment Charm. The Disillusionment Charm is magical camouflage. Cast it on yourself or someone else and you become nearly invisible, blending seamlessly into your surroundings. Great for disappearing when someone says, "We need to talk." In Deathly Hallows, the trio uses it repeatedly during their Horcrux hunt, slipping past Death Eaters, Snatchers, and anyone else who might ruin their day. It's perfect for sneaky movement or escaping sticky situations without anyone noticing. Here's a warning. Disillusionment Charms don't just make you completely invisible. It's more like being a wizarding chameleon. Clothing wrinkles and movement can still give you away. In other words, it's excellent for stealth and basically Hogwarts-approved hide-and-seek magic with real-life or death stakes. Protego spells. Protego Totalum and Protego Horribilis are basically wizarding military-grade spells. They blanket entire areas, turning forests, tents, or even Hogwarts itself into nearly impenetrable magical fortresses. In Deathly Hallows, survival isn't always about flashy duels or dramatic wand clashes. Sometimes it's about sitting back and saying, "Not today, Death Eaters." while hidden behind an invisible, nearly indestructible shield that does all the heavy lifting. Remember the shimmering blue shield around Hogwarts during the battle, showing the spells holding back Death Eater attacks? Yeah, that was Protego Totalum, the spell that creates a broad defensive barrier. While Protego Horribilis reinforces it against dark magic. Harry, Hermione, and Ron also use the Protego spells on a smaller scale while camping across Britain during their Horcrux hunt, with Hermione layering protections around their tent to keep Death Eaters, Snatchers, and other unwelcome visitors at bay. Geminio Charm. The Geminio Charm, also called the Doubling Charm or Duplication Charm, is basically the wizarding version of a photocopier. Point your wand at an object, say Geminio, and suddenly it multiplies. Touch it again, more copies. Touch it one more time, you get it. In Deathly Hallows, this spell hits hard twice. First as a curse during the iconic Lestrange's Gringotts vault heist. The golden trio go in for Hufflepuff's cup, but everything they touch starts doubling. Every coin, every trinket, every cursed ornament explodes into copies until they're almost drowning in treasure. Panic level: expert. Second time Hermione uses it as a charm, not a curse, on the Slytherin locket in the Ministry of Magic. Cleaner application, fewer near-death experiences. Still ridiculously powerful, though. Not so fun fact for wizards planning on using Geminio as an instant money glitch, the duplicates are worthless. They tarnish, rot, or break faster than a chocolate frog in summer. Sorry, Weasleys. Merlin's beard! You survived this lesson, then you're ready for the next one. All 10 major events after the Deathly Hallows explained. Watch now before Snape starts screaming your name.


=== How Much Has Harry Potter Cost Hogwarts? ===
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=e4BGgZ0DF1A

The boy who broke everything. Snape was bored, so he calculated the exact amount of money Harry Potter cost Hogwarts. Every smashed corridor, every dead basilisk. And you'll want to watch until the end, because this final bill is absolutely insane. Harry arrives at Hogwarts, 11 years old, no friends, no clue what a galleon is, and within 2 months he's already running up a tab. Halloween, first year. A mountain troll gets into the girls' bathroom, and instead of getting a teacher, Harry jumps on its back and shoves his wand up its nose. The troll goes down, takes half the bathroom with it, smashed sinks, burst pipes, walls cracked by a creature that weighs over a ton. $50,000 for a fight that lasted 45 seconds. A few months later, he goes after the Philosopher's Stone. Three first-years versus the most heavily guarded object in wizarding Britain, and they destroyed every layer of protection on the way down. Devil's Snare burned, the enchanted chess set annihilated, potions lab wrecked, trapdoor collapsed. $500,000 in one night. Oh, and Quirrell? Harry touches him and the guy literally disintegrates. Dark magic residue cleanup, specialist teams, containment charms, $75,000 to scrub a dead professor off the floor. Year two opens with Harry and Ron stealing a flying car and crashing it into the Whomping Willow. Branch damage, long-term tree trauma. The car totaled somewhere in the Forbidden Forest. $150,000. And that's just because then the Chamber of Secrets opens. Pipes burst across the castle, bathrooms flood, students, a cat, and a ghost get petrified. Professor Sprout has to run a wartime mandrake agriculture program for 6 months, and Gilderoy Lockhart accidentally wipes his own memory and becomes a permanent resident at St. Mungo's. $1.2 million for a disaster caused by a diary. And at the bottom of it all, a 60-ft dead basilisk rotting in the school's plumbing. Fangs the size of swords, enough venom to contaminate the water supply for decades. Extraction costs $2.5 million. Except they never actually did it. We literally see the skeleton still lying there in Deathly Hallows when Ron and Hermione go back for a fang. Maybe Hogwarts pocketed the removal budget. Chapter total, roughly $4.5 million. Sounds like a lot, but Harry's just getting warmed up, and trust me, it's about to get so much worse. Harry Potter and the Auditor of Azkaban. Year three. The Ministry's solution to one escaped convict is to surround a school full of teenagers with dementors. Soul-sucking demons that cause permanent psychological damage just by floating past you. Great idea. Keep in mind Hogwarts has roughly a thousand students, and every single one of them spent nine straight months breathing the same air as creatures designed to harvest human happiness. Multiply that by an entire school year of therapy, trauma response, and St. Mungo's healers on permanent rotation, and the bill climbs fast. One of those dementors almost kisses Harry mid-Quidditch match. He falls 100 ft and his Nimbus 2000 sails straight into the Whomping Willow. $5 million. Nobody reimburses him for the broom. Meanwhile, Sirius Black keeps breaking into the castle like it's a hobby. The Fat Lady's portrait, an ancient enchanted painting that's been guarding Gryffindor Tower for centuries, slashed to ribbons. Three full castle lockdowns. Hundreds of paintings fleeing their frames in panic. Every wand on the building recast from scratch. $3 million. All for a guy who turned out to be innocent. Then there's Buckbeak, a hippogriff that scratched Draco Malfoy because he ignored every instruction and insulted it. Full Ministry trial, professional executioner dispatched to Hogwarts with a giant axe. $500,000 spent, and the hippogriff escapes anyway. Year four, the Triwizard Tournament. Dumbledore brings back a competition that was banned for two centuries because contestants kept dying. The Black Lake gets restructured into an underwater obstacle course. The Quidditch pitch is ripped apart and replaced with a giant hedge maze. The Great Hall gets reconfigured for two visiting schools and a Yule Ball. $8 million in infrastructure alone. And then Cedric Diggory touches the cup, gets transported to a graveyard, and Voldemort kills him on the spot. 17 years old, family compensation, Ministry investigation that goes nowhere because Fudge refuses to believe Voldemort's back, legal fees, and let's be honest, hush money. Add it all up, $12 million. Add it all up, 28.5 million for one school year. The tab is now sitting at 33 million, and we're only halfway through Harry's time at Hogwarts. Harry Potter and the Half-Blood Debt. Year five, Voldemort is back, and the Ministry's response is to send Dolores Umbridge to Hogwarts. Great idea. Her job, shut Harry up and turn the school into a government outpost. Hundreds of educational decrees engraved on bronze plaques. A surveillance network of enchanted cat plates in every corridor. Charmed doors tracking everyone. And a student inquisitorial squad. She turns a school into a police state and bills every penny to the Hogwarts budget. $3 million for decorative fascism. Then Fudge shows up to arrest Dumbledore. Dumbledore doesn't go quietly. Surprise, surprise. Fawkes grabs him, the office erupts, and he vanishes. Left behind, stunned aurors, chaos, and a headmaster's office that needed serious restoration. $1.5 million. Fred and George's farewell gift, a portable swamp in a corridor that Umbridge can't remove for weeks. Plus enchanted fireworks chasing her through the castle for days. $200,000 in cleanup. Worth every knut. Filch is not happy, but all of that is the warm-up. At the end of year five, Harry leads six students into the Department of Mysteries, and they demolish it. The Hall of Prophecy goes first. Over 100,000 glass orbs, the shelves fall like dominoes. $800 million. The Ministry's single most expensive loss in its entire history. The battle rips through three more departments. The Time-Turner room destroyed, making that technology extinct. The Death Chamber structurally damaged. The brain room trashed. $35 million. And after all of this, after Umbridge, after the Ministry lied about Voldemort all year, after a student died and nobody did anything, parents start pulling their kids out. Enrollment crashes. $10 million in lost tuition that doesn't come back. $850 million for year five alone. Tab's at $883 million, and we're not even at the war yet. The real destruction starts next chapter. Harry Potter and the Deathly Hallows. Year six, Draco Malfoy spends the entire year secretly repairing a vanishing cabinet in the Room of Requirement. And when he gets it working, a squad of Death Eaters walk straight into the school. The Astronomy Tower takes heavy damage in the battle that follows. Dark magic residue soaks into the castle walls so deep it takes curse-breakers months to clean. $15 million for a cabinet everybody forgot existed. Katie Bell hospitalized after a cursed necklace. Ron nearly killed by poison mead. Bill Weasley permanently scarred by Fenrir Greyback. Hogwarts has to bring in a full healer rotation from St. Mungo's. A school operating like a field hospital. $8 million. Then Dumbledore dies on top of the Astronomy Tower, and the moment he falls, the protective wards he personally maintained for decades collapse with him. Ward reconstruction alone, $50 million. Year seven, the war goes off campus, but the bill keeps climbing. The Burrow burned to the ground by Death Eaters. $1.2 million. The Seven Potters extraction. 60 coys drink Polyjuice Potion to look exactly like Harry. Everyone flies in different directions to confuse the Death Eaters. It doesn't work. Voldemort shows up personally. Broomsticks destroyed, suburban rooftops smashed. The Muggle power grid fried across half a country. $25 million. The Lovegood house leveled by an erumpent horn explosion. $500,000. Back at Hogwarts, the Carrows convert classrooms into torture chambers. Students practice the Cruciatus Curse on each other. Dark magic saturates the walls on a daily basis, and attendance collapses entirely. $50 million in institutional damage. And then Harry breaks into Gringotts. Three teenagers infiltrate the most secure bank in the wizarding world. Blast through a high-security vault and escape on a half-blind dragon that smashes straight up through the marble lobby and out the roof. Structural damage across multiple levels. The entire security system compromised, and public confidence in wizarding banking dead overnight. $1.5 billion. And Harry doesn't even apologize. Two more years, $1.65 billion in damages. Harry's running tab is now 2.53 billion, and he still has one night left at Hogwarts. The Battle of the Budget. May 2nd, 1998. And what happens in the next 12 hours makes everything before it look like a rounding error. The protective wards go first. Centuries-old defensive magic, some of it dating back to the founders. Layers upon layers reinforced by every headmaster since, shattered in minutes. You can't replicate a thousand years of accumulated enchantments by throwing money at it. But you have to try. $5 billion, and they'll never be what they were. The Astronomy Tower and the castle roof tear open under concentrated dark magic. $2 billion. McGonagall brings every suit of armor and every stone statue to life to defend the school. Hundreds of ancient rune-inscribed artifacts charge into battle and get crushed by giants, blasted apart by curses, shattered against courtyard walls. $8 billion. Then Crabbe casts Fiendfyre in the Room of Requirement. Centuries of hidden objects, lost relics, forgotten treasures, all gone. Including Ravenclaw's diadem. Conservatively, $10 billion. The room itself scarred so deep it never fully recovers. Total loss, $15 billion. dollars. blows up the bridge to stop a wave of snatchers. Major structural collapse. Months of magical engineering to rebuild. One billion dollars. The Great Hall, the heart of Hogwarts where every student was sorted, where every feast was held, becomes a battlefield. Walls crack, the enchanted ceiling collapses, chandeliers crash down. Three billion dollars. Outside the Quidditch pitch, rebuilt twice already thanks to Harry, is cratered beyond recognition. The greenhouses shatter, releasing decades of carefully cultivated magical plants into the wild. Mandrake populations wiped out entirely. The same mandrakes that saved the petrified students back in year two. Courtyards crushed by giant footprints, two billion dollars. And then there's the cost no galleon can cover. Over 50 people killed in a single night. Students, teachers, Order members. Funerals, medical treatment for survivors carrying curses that never fully heal. Family compensation, 2.5 billion dollars. Emergency stabilization alone, shoring up collapsing corridors, temporary ward scaffolding, and making the castle minimally safe to enter again, another 265 million. The battle ends. Voldemort falls. But Hogwarts is in ruins, closed for months. The Ministry is destabilized, half of its workforce dead or compromised. Gringotts still recovering. Diagon Alley shuttered. The wizarding economy is in free fall. Reconstruction, institutional rebuilding, economic recovery. An entire generation dealing with trauma that no spell can fix. 50 billion in ripple effects, conservative estimate. Chapter total roughly 88.8 billion dollars. Running total about 91.3 billion dollars. But if you thought we were done, wait, there's more. The debt who must not be named. Harry's bill started before he was born. And no, we're not letting the pre-Hogwarts year slide. So, Trelawney makes a prophecy, Snape leaks half of it, and suddenly there's a target on a baby who hasn't even learned to crawl yet. Bellatrix Lestrange vault alone bankrupted Voldemort with over 800 million. And the Malfoys, the Blacks, and every pureblood family that donated operating budget, 1.2 billion dollars. The Ministry counter spends 1.8 billion on orders and the original Order. Voldemort then shows up at Godric's Hollow and levels the place, 2.3 million in property damage. Sirius gets 12 years in Azkaban for nothing, 900,000 dollars in wrongful imprisonment. Damage before Harry even sets foot in Hogwarts, roughly three billion. So, here it is. The total cost of Harry Potter from a prophecy in a pub to a castle in flames, 94.3 billion dollars. Hogwarts may be destroyed, but luckily there are 10 other schools that might still be standing. === The Lives of ALL 21 Weasleys in Harry Potter EXPLAINED in Detail ===
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=en3qvtPXtRE

Matilda Weasley. When you think of the Weasleys, you think of a house bursting with children, hand-me-down robes, and chaos as a lifestyle. But, the earliest Weasley we can actually put a name to was nothing like that. Matilda Weasley served as Professor of Transfiguration and Deputy Headmistress at Hogwarts, working directly under Phineas Nigellus Black. Yes, the same Phineas Nigellus who held the title of least popular Headmaster in the school's history. Before Hogwarts, though, Matilda traveled the world as a curse-breaker for the Ministry of Magic. Ancient tombs, magical wards, the kind of work that gets you killed if you sneeze at the wrong moment. Turns out the Weasley tradition of walking into ridiculously dangerous situations and somehow walking back out started a full century before Bill took a similar job at Gringotts. She married a fellow curse-breaker named Paul, though the marriage didn't last. They separated and Matilda had no children. The family line continued through her brothers, but the appetite for danger, well, that stuck with the family name. And the next one to prove it was Gareth Weasley, Matilda's nephew, a fifth-year Gryffindor in the 1890s, and the first Weasley who saw school rules as entertainment. Gareth was obsessed with potion-making, specifically the kind that isn't in any textbook. He invented his own brews, tested them on family members, and drove his father mad in the process. His aunt Matilda spent half her time as Deputy Headmistress just keeping him from blowing up the Potions Classroom. Where Matilda gave the Weasley name its tradition of courage, Gareth gave it something equally important, the tradition of creative chaos. Fred and George would perfect that philosophy a century later, but Gareth got there first. Now, a few generations later, one Weasley made a choice that shook a very different family tree. His name was Septimus Weasley, born around the 1910s, Ron's grandfather, and the man who permanently attached the words blood traitor to the Weasley name. He married Cedrella Black, great-aunt to Sirius. She grew up inside one of the most obsessively pureblood households in wizarding Britain and then chose a Weasley. Her family responded the way they always did, burned her name off the tapestry. So, blood traitor meant they rejected the idea of pureblood supremacy, openly embracing their ties to Muggles, and never acting as if their bloodline made them better than anyone else. They had at least three sons, including Arthur, who inherited the attitude wholesale. Through this marriage, Black blood ended up running through a family that wore the label like a badge. Fun fact, Septimus is most likely the grandfather whose old wizard chess set ended up with Ron. The same set that won the most important chess game in Hogwarts history. Not bad for a hand-me-down. One of Septimus' sons took the family legacy in a very different direction. Billius Weasley, Ron's uncle, the family bachelor, and the Weasley who proved you don't need children to leave a legacy. He never married, never settled down, and had zero interest in either. What Billius had was personality. The man drank an entire bottle of fire whiskey, lifted his robes, and started pulling out bouquets of flowers. He also saw the Grim, the wizarding world's omen of death, and died within 24 hours. And the family turned the story into one of its darker legends. Billius gave the family two things. First, a name. Ron carries it as his middle name, Ronald Billius Weasley, right there on every howler and every Ministry document. And second, a reputation. The idea that a Weasley can be fearless, reckless, absolutely ridiculous, and still be someone worth naming your son after. But while Billius lived loud and fast, his brother built something that would last for generations. Arthur Weasley, head of the family of nine, Ministry employee on a salary that barely covered school supplies, and the most enthusiastic Muggle fan the wizarding world has ever seen. He took a job at the Misuse of Muggle Artifacts Office, the least prestigious department the Ministry had to offer, and loved every second of it. Where other pureblood families locked their doors to the Muggle world, Arthur left his wide open and installed a garage. Here's the thing about Arthur, he built the Burrow, the crooked, chaotic, magically held together house that became the emotional center of the entire Harry Potter story. Every Christmas, every summer, every moment Harry needed a family, the Burrow, Arthur and Molly made that. And then, seven children, seven Gryffindors, a curse breaker, a dragon tamer, a ministry official, two inventors, a war hero, and the very first Weasley daughter born in living memory. Arthur gave the wizarding world an entire generation of Weasleys, all raised on the same principle, people matter more than blood. But before the children, there was Molly, and she deserves her own chapter. Molly Weasley, née Prewett, born 30th of October, 1949. The woman who raised an entire household through love, discipline, and sheer force of will. She came from the Prewett family, pure blood, respected and included in the Sacred Twenty-Eight. Her brothers, Fabian and Gideon, both died fighting Death Eaters in the First Wizarding War. Molly carried that loss quietly for the rest of her life, and it shaped everything she did after. She met Arthur at Hogwarts, both Gryffindor, both trouble. One night, they snuck out after curfew together. Arthur got caught by the caretaker and caned hard enough to carry the marks for the rest of his life, and Molly slipped back into Gryffindor Tower at 4:00 in the morning completely undetected. Sounds about right for those two. Here's what Molly Weasley actually was. She raised seven children on a ministry salary that had no business stretching that far. And every single one of them came out brave, loyal, and slightly unhinged, which for a Weasley is the goal. The clock on the wall that tracked every family member's location, the Howlers that could reduce a teenager to ashes across three countries, pure Molly. And then there's the sweaters, every Christmas, hand-knitted, personalized, one for each child, and one for Harry. The moment Harry received a Weasley sweater, Molly had already folded him into the family. Most fans remember her for one line, "Not my daughter, you bitch." aimed at Bellatrix Lestrange seconds before ending her. But Molly had been fighting for her family long before that duel. Every meal, every sweater, every Howler, all of it was combat. Her eldest son inherited her stubbornness. Bill Weasley, the eldest of seven, and then he dropped all of it to crawl through cursed Egyptian tombs for Gringotts. A century earlier, his great-great-aunt Matilda had done the same work for the Ministry. The Weasley curse-breaking gene skipped a few generations and landed right back where it started. But here's the part that actually reshaped the family. Bill married Fleur Delacour, French, part Veela, glamorous, the exact opposite of everything the Burrow represented. Molly fought it. Fleur stayed anyway, especially after Greyback scarred Bill's face during the Battle of the Astronomy Tower. She made it clear she didn't care what he looked like. Wedding happened and Molly came around. With Fleur, Bill gave the Weasley bloodline something it had never had, an international branch. After seven centuries of pureblood stubbornness, the Weasleys finally went European. They had three children, Dominique, Louis, and Victoire, who would one day be caught kissing a Lupin on platform 9 and 3/4. Bill's younger brother wanted nothing to do with offices, banks, or England. He wanted dragons. Charlie Weasley, second eldest, stocky, covered in burns, and the only Weasley who was offered a career in professional Quidditch and said, "No." He was good enough to play for England. Oliver Wood said so himself. Charlie chose dragons instead, moved to Romania to study them at a reserve and never came back. Here's why that matters for the actual story. When Hagrid hatched an illegal Norwegian Ridgeback in his wooden hut, because of course he did, it was Charlie's friends who flew down and smuggled the dragon out. Without that Romanian connection, Norbert stays at Hogwarts, Hagrid gets arrested, and Harry's first year goes very differently. Charlie also brought reinforcements to the Battle of Hogwarts, foreign wizards recruited through his contacts abroad. He never married, never had children. Basically, the Weasley's family cool uncle who shows up smelling like a bonfire and has better stories than everyone else at the table combined. His younger brother took the opposite path entirely. Percy Weasley, third eldest, prefect, head boy, and the Weasley who ironed his socks. He climbed the ministry ladder faster than anyone expected. Junior assistant to the minister himself by his early 20s. Impressive until you realize the ministry promoted him specifically to spy on his own father. Percy either didn't see it or didn't want to. When Arthur tried to warn him, Percy chose the ministry. He sent back his Weasley sweater, called his father an embarrassment, and walked out. Molly couldn't talk about him without crying. For 2 years, his chair at the Burrow sat empty. Most fans remember what he did wrong. Here's what he did right. He came back, walked into the Battle of Hogwarts, apologized to his family in the middle of a war zone, and fought beside them. He was standing next to Fred when it happened. One moment they were laughing together, the next Fred was gone. After the war, he married Audrey, had two daughters, Molly and Lucy, and spent the rest of his career quietly making the ministry better from the inside. The prodigal Weasley finally home. The brother who made him laugh one last time deserves everything that comes next. Fred Weasley, one half of the most dangerous duo Hogwarts had seen since the Marauders, and the Weasley who proved that chaos can be a business model. Fred and George stole the Marauders Map from Filch's office in their first year. They were 11. The map had been confiscated decades earlier, sitting in a drawer waiting for someone reckless enough to take it. Two 11-year-old Weasleys qualified perfectly. They used it for years before handing it to Harry, who arguably needed it more. But, the real legacy was Weasleys' Wizard Wheezes. Fred and George built a joke shop in the middle of a war. Skiving Snackboxes, extendable ears, Peruvian Instant Darkness Powder. Half their inventory doubled as resistance tools. They funded it with Harry's Triwizard winnings and turned 93 Diagon Alley into the brightest shop front on the street while Death Eaters darkened everything around it.
