Fred died at the Battle of Hogwarts. The shop stayed open and someone had to run it alone. George Weasley, the twin who stayed, had already lost an ear during the Battle of the Seven Potters. Cursed off by Snape's Sectumsempra meant for a Death Eater. After the war, George reopened Weasleys' Wizard Wheezes and ran it himself. Every product on the shelf, every invention in the back room. Half of it still had Fred's handwriting on the prototypes. He kept going. He married Angelina Johnson who had gone to the Yule Ball with Fred. Some fans find that strange. Others think Fred would have found it perfect. They had two children. The first a son he named Fred. Basically, George took the worst thing that could happen to a twin and turned it into a life that honored everything they'd built together. The laughter stayed in the family, it just got a little quieter. And then there was Ron Weasley. Sixth of seven, hand-me-down everything, and the reason Harry Potter survived long enough to save the wizarding world. Every Weasley before him had something that made them stand out. Bill was the cool one. Charlie was the tough one. Percy was the smart one. Fred and George were the loud ones. Ron grew up at the bottom of that list wearing Charlie's old robes and carrying a broken wand he was too embarrassed to replace. His entire childhood was other people's leftovers. Ron sat down next to a skinny kid on the Hogwarts Express who had no friends, no family, and no idea how the wizarding world worked. Then Ron pulled out his corned beef sandwiches, complained about them, and that was enough. Harry bought the entire trolley and shared it all with the first person who'd ever just sat with him. That was the moment Harry Potter gained access to the Weasleys. Every Christmas at the Burrow. Every Molly sweater. Every Arthur question about rubber ducks. All of it traces back to two kids splitting sweets on a train. His strategic mind won the chess game that got Harry past Dumbledore's protections in their first year. On his grandfather's old set. He destroyed a Horcrux. He came back after walking away, which is harder than never leaving. After the war, he married Hermione Granger, the brightest witch of her age, the person who'd kept all three of them alive through most of it. The wedding was, by all accounts, eventful. Ron got so nervous, he spent most of the ceremony fairly drunk, answered, "I think I do." when asked if he'd take her as his wife, and remembered very little of it afterwards. Hermione married him anyway. Together, they had two children, Rose and Hugo Granger-Weasley. So, Ron gave Harry a family, then he built one of his own. But, Ron wasn't the last Weasley to leave a mark on Harry's story. Ginny Weasley, the seventh child, the first daughter in generations, and the Weasley who stared down Voldemort before she turned 12. In a family full of brothers who defined themselves by being loud, brave, or brilliant, Ginny spent her early years being overlooked. The quiet little sister. Then, her first year at Hogwarts happened. Tom Riddle's diary got inside her head and used her to open the Chamber of Secrets, and nearly drained her life to bring himself back. She was 11. Most grown wizards never face anything close to that. Ginny survived it and came out the other side absolutely furious. That fury became her signature. She was the best flyer in the family, yes, better than Charlie. And the Bat-Bogey Hex she threw was famous enough to earn her an invitation to the Slug Club. She joined Dumbledore's Army, fought at the Department of Mysteries, fought at the Battle of Hogwarts, and eventually went professional with the Holyhead Harpies. And then, she married Harry Potter. The Weasley and Potter lines merged, and three children followed. James Sirius, Albus Severus, and Lily Luna. Seven centuries of Weasley history, and it led straight to a Potter. But, Ginny wasn't the last Weasley to carry the family forward. The next chapter belongs to a name that was already gone and came back. Fred Weasley the Second. George's firstborn. He was born in 2008, a decade after the battle. George could have named his son anything, but he chose Fred, the name that meant everything and cost everything. Hogwarts, Gryffindor, Quidditch, all expected. What hit harder was the position. Fred the Second became a Beater, the same position Fred and George had played together side by side for years. The same role that had made them legendary on the pitch. George once had a twin beside him swinging at Bludgers. Now his son was doing it in his place. The Weasley's name kept growing, and Ron's eldest daughter would carry it into a combination the wizarding world had never seen. Rose Granger-Weasley, Ron and Hermione's eldest and the first person in history to carry both names at once. A pureblood family that spent generations being labeled blood traitors, and now the name itself has a Muggle-born surname fused right into it. Rose made the Gryffindor Quidditch team as a Chaser in her second year. Her broomstick obeyed on the first command, something Hermione's famously never did. The intelligence came from Granger, the Quidditch came from Weasley. The combination was exactly what both names deserved. She has a younger brother, Hugo. Together they represent something they Sacred 28 would have found unthinkable. A Weasley generation that carries Muggle-born heritage in its name and considers it a point of pride. The Weasley story started with a curse breaker who had no children. It ended with a family so large, so loud, and so stubbornly good that the wizarding world couldn't function without them. Okay, the Weasleys gave Harry a family, a home, and eventually a wife. But the Potters had their own story long before a skinny kid showed up at the Burrow. The lives of all 11 Potters in Harry Potter explained in detail. That's your next lesson here at Stickman Hogwarts. Click now before Molly sends you a Howler for skipping class.


=== All 24 Legendary Magical Creatures in Harry Potter EXPLAINED in Detail ===
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=xNKnd9uC_18

Number one, dragon. If there is one creature in the wizarding world that makes even experienced wizards reconsider their life choices, it is the dragon. These enormous winged reptiles are capable of flight and can unleash devastating streams of fire from their mouths and nostrils. The British Ministry of Magic classifies dragons as XXXXX, the highest danger rating reserved for creatures known to kill wizards and impossible to domesticate. Despite their ferocity, trained specialists known as dragon keepers study and manage them reserves. Dragons exist worldwide and appear in many distinct breeds, each with unique traits. Four famously appeared in the Triwizard Tournament, the Hungarian Horntail, considered one of the most aggressive, the Chinese Fireball, known for its distinctive red scales and mushroom-shaped flame, the Swedish Short-Snout, prized for its intense blue fire, and the Welsh Green, slightly less aggressive but still deadly. Beyond their fearsome reputation, their body parts possess powerful magical properties including heart strings used as wand cores. Number two, basilisk. The basilisk, often called the king of all snakes, is one of the most feared creatures in the wizarding world. The first known basilisk was created in ancient Greece by the dark wizard Herpo the Foul. Despite its terrifying reputation, the method of creating one is surprisingly simple. A chicken egg hatched beneath a toad. The result is a gigantic serpent capable of growing up to 50 feet in length and living for centuries. The basilisk's most dangerous weapon is its gaze. Direct eye contact causes instant death, while indirect contact results in petrification. It also possesses highly venomous fangs capable of destroying even powerful magical objects including horcruxes. Because of the extreme danger they pose, the breeding of basilisks was officially outlawed during the medieval era. Number three, hippogriff. Hippogriffs are among the proudest of magical creatures with the front half of a giant eagle and a hind of a horse. First recorded in the Middle Ages, they are believed to have originated in Europe and were later officially classified as magical beasts by the Ministry of magic. An adult Hippogriff can reach up to 3 m in height with a wingspan approaching 10 m, allowing it to fly long distances with incredible speed and strength. Despite their elegance, Hippogriffs are extremely sensitive to respect and etiquette. Anyone approaching must bow and wait for the creature to return the gesture before coming closer. Even if you happen to be Draco Malfoy. Their diet consists primarily of insects, birds, and small mammals. Number four, Thestral. Thestrals are wrongly considered bad omens in the wizarding world because they can only be seen by those who have witnessed death and fully accepted its reality. This rare breed of winged horse is native to British Isles and Ireland. Thestrals have skeletal bodies, reptilian facial features, and vast black wings resembling those of a bat. Adults are larger than ordinary horses with wingspans reaching up to 10 m, allowing them to fly with surprising speed and agility. Despite their eerie appearance, Thestrals are highly social, preferring to live in herds. According to Hagrid, they are dead clever. Carnivorous by nature, they often prey on birds and possess a keen sense of smell. Number five, Phoenix. While death marks the end for almost every magical and non-magical creature on Earth, the Phoenix treats it like a minor inconvenience. Native to India, China, and Egypt, this magnificent scarlet bird with red and gold plumage and a swan-sized body is famous for its ability to regenerate. When a Phoenix grows old, it bursts into flames in an event known as a burning day, only to rise again from the ashes as a newborn chick. Its tears possess powerful healing properties capable of cursing even basilisk venom, while its haunting song can inspire courage in the good and fear in the wicked. Loyal yet independent, Phoenixes remain among the rarest and most extraordinary magical creatures ever recorded. Fun fact, Phoenixes are immune to basilisk gaze. It neither kills nor petrifies them. Number six, Acromantula. Regular spiders are indeed scary, but the wizarding world has something far worse, the Acromantula, and Ron is not happy about it. This gigantic magical spider can grow larger than a cart horse with a leg span reaching up to 15 ft and eight gleaming black eyes. Unlike ordinary spiders, Acromantulas are intelligent and capable of human speech, though that rarely makes them friendlier. In fact, they possess a strong taste for human flesh. Native to the jungles of Borneo, they prefer dark forests where they build enormous dome-shaped webs and live in massive colonies led by their eldest members. Their fangs contain extremely valuable and highly toxic venom, making encounters with these creatures both deadly and dangerous. Number seven, Centaur. Meet the Centaurs, the ultimate companions of awkward social encounters, creatures who will stare at the stars longer than they'll speak to you. Centaurs have the torso, arms, and head of a human fused with a horse's powerful lower body. And though they possess human-level intelligence and speech, they insist on being classified as beasts by the Ministry of Magic. They dwell in herds deep within the forest, living by their own ancient code, and are skilled in archery, healing magic, astronomy, and divination. Proud, territorial, and fiercely independent, Centaurs generally avoid human contact, seeing themselves as stewards of nature rather than servants of wizardkind. Fun fact, Firenze, the creature who saved Harry during the first encounter with Voldemort in the Forbidden Forest and Philosopher's Stone, was a Centaur. He was later hired by Dumbledore to teach Divination at Hogwarts after Professor Trelawney was dismissed by Umbridge. Number eight, Werewolf. Werewolves are humans cursed to transform into savage wolf-like beasts under the light of every full moon, losing their human reason in the process. Their bite spreads the curse, meaning one unlucky wound can condemn another person to the same fate. Feared, discriminated against, and often forced into isolation by wizarding society, werewolves remain among the most misunderstood and dangerous beings in the magical world. One of the most well-known examples is Remus Lupin, James Potter's friend and Harry's Defense Against the Dark Arts teacher, who struggled with prejudice throughout his life and briefly taught at Hogwarts despite his condition. Canonically, no cure exists for lycanthropy and every full moon remains a night of peril for those afflicted. Number nine, Niffler. The Niffler is tiny, furry, utterly unstoppable, and a gold digger, literally. These black-furred burrowing creatures are obsessed with anything shiny, famously escaping Newt Scamander's pockets at the sight of every sparkling object in Fantastic Beasts. Native to Britain, Nifflers have long snouts and deceptively spacious belly pouches that can hold far more than seems possible. Gentle by nature and affectionate with trusted handlers, they are mischievous treasure hunters leaving a trail of glittering chaos wherever they go. In the magical world, no shiny coin or jewel is ever truly safe from a Niffler. Number 10, Occamy. These serpentine eagle-like creatures have two pairs of majestic wings and can grow or shrink to fit available space thanks to their uncanny ability to expand or contract their bodies. Native to the Far East, Occamies fiercely protect their nests, which are lined with pure gleaming silver eggs worth fortunes in wizarding markets. Coveted by witches and wizards alike, their eggshells are prized for enchantments, but getting close is another matter entirely. Intelligent, territorial, and lethal in defense, Occamies are among the most extraordinary and dangerous magical creatures ever documented. In short, try stealing an Occamy's egg and you'll get a silver-scaled surprise you won't forget. Number 11, Demiguise. Demiguises possess long silvery fur and enormous, expressive eyes that give them an innocent appearance, but their most remarkable ability is true invisibility. They can vanish from sight at will or when they sense danger. These gentle, primate-like magical beings are masters of subtlety and survival. Native to the Far East, they prefer quiet forests where they feed on plants and avoid confrontation. Their hair is especially prized by wand makers for its unique properties, making it one of the rarest and most coveted wand cores in the wizarding world. Intelligent and peaceful by nature, Demiguises would rather slip away unseen than fight, proving that sometimes the greatest power is simply disappearing at just the right moment. Number 12, Thunderbird. If there is one magical creature that literally rides the weather, it is the Thunderbird. These majestic birds hail from North America, have plumage that crackles with electrical energy. A Thunderbird can sense storms before they form and fly straight into tempests, channeling lightning and thunder through its wings as it glides with terrifying grace. Their calls can summon thunderclaps that echo across deserts and plains, and their feathers are prized for use in powerful weather-related charms. Independent, proud, and fiercely territorial, Thunderbirds are revered by some wizarding cultures as symbols of strength and freedom. In short, when a Thunderbird arrives, the storm becomes its opening act. Number 13, Graphorn. Graphorn is a magical creature that even makes mountain trolls look lame. These massive humpbacked beasts are covered in grayish-purple skin, tougher than a dragon's hide, and capable of repelling most spells, making them nearly impossible to take down by conventional magic. With two long golden horns and four thick four-thumbed feet, Graphorns are fiercely territorial and aggressive, especially when approached by would-be handlers. Their horns are highly prized as a potion ingredient, but collecting them is dangerous. Coveted, feared, and enormously strong, a Graphorn encounter is one that even a veteran magizoologist approach with caution. Magical fact, anger a Graphorn and its body starts emitting orange lights while its strength skyrockets. In other words, the moment it starts glowing is exactly when you start running. Number 14, Erumpent. Erumpent is a magical creature that turns stay back into an understatement. These massive rhinoceros-like beasts have thick black hides and a deadly reputation for explosive violence. The erumpent's horn contains a volatile fluid that detonates on contact, making even a grazing strike capable of blowing apart boulders and buildings alike. Native to Africa, they feed on vegetation but will charge anything they perceive as a threat with terrifying speed. Highly dangerous even to seasoned magizoologists, erumpents are studied with extreme caution. A word of warning for any wizard studying magical creatures, if an erumpent charges in your direction, your very first instinct should be to retreat. Number 15, swooping evil. Hatched from a glittering peculiar egg, the swooping evil is a large winged beast that looks like a cross between a dragon and a manta ray with a gaping tooth-lined maw hidden beneath its shimmering body. Native to remote regions, it can release a stunning powder that disorients and incapacitates anything nearby, making it deadly at close range. Its quills are pried for advanced spell work and its tough green hides can deflect some spells, adding to its fearsome reputation. As an encephalo phage, it feeds on human brains and its venom can temporarily drive victims insane. Diluted carefully, the same venom can erase memories, a technique Newt Scamander famously used to protect New York's no-maj population, dispersing it through rain via his Thunderbird, Frank. Number 16, nundu. Have you ever wondered if there was a creature that could make dragons look like oversized lizards? There is one and it is called nundu. This massive leopard-like beast prowls the swamps of East Africa. Its breath carrying a disease so potent it can wipe out entire villages in hours. Nearly impossible to subdue, the nundu uh moves with surprising stealth for its size and emits a low terrifying growl that can freeze even seasoned magizoologists in their tracks. Wizards regard it as one of the most dangerous creatures ever encountered, requiring dozens of expert witches and wizards to restrain. Number 17, Kappa. Kappas are reptilian amphibious creatures from Japan known for their sleek green scaled bodies and a water-filled cavity atop their head that gives them strength until it empties. Mischievous and dangerous, Kappas often attempt to drag unsuspecting humans into water or challenge them to sumo-like contests. Some legends even claim they feed on human blood showing no hesitation in strangling the unwary. Despite their menace, survival is possible. One simple trick is to offer a cucumber inscribed with a person's name, which appeases the Kappa and distracts it from attacking. Caution, respect, and cleverness are key when dealing with these unpredictable beings. Number 18, Boggart. Remember the magical creature from the Prisoner of Azkaban that turns your worst fear into its favorite pastime? It's called the Boggart. These elusive shape-shifting beings lurk in the dark cramped spaces and have a terrifying survival strategy. They become whatever you fear most. For one witch, it might appear as a giant spider. For another, it might take the form of the absolute nightmare of getting a full-on death glare from Snape. Boggarts feed on fear, growing stronger the more terrified you become. The only known counter spell is Riddikulus, which forces the Boggart into something humorous instead of horrifying, like turning Snape into an eccentric grandma. Number 19, Unicorn. If there is one creature in the wizarding world that embodies pure grace and ancient magic, it is the unicorn. These elegant horse-like beasts are cloaked in shimmering white fur and crowned with a single spiraling horn of immense power. Unicorns dwell in secluded forests where their presence alone preserves the purity of the land. Their blood is life-saving yet cursed when shed, and their hair and horn are prized for potent magical ingredients. Elusive by nature, unicorns avoid human contact and can sense the purity of a witch or wizard's heart. In short, to glimpse a unicorn is to witness magic in its most noble form. Number 20, Troll. Towering several meters tall with thick warty skin and massive limbs. Trolls rely on brute strength over subtlety. Notoriously dim-witted and aggressive, they often smash anything in their path, making them one of the most dangerous magical creatures a witch or wizard can encounter. Encounters are usually resolved with fire, powerful spells, a rapid retreat, or in the case of the golden trio, a well-timed Wingardium Leviosa on a troll's club and a little bit of wand whacking on the nose for good measure. From the infamous mountain troll in Philosopher's Stone to roaming specimens across Europe, trolls serve as a constant warning about underestimating sheer physical power. In short, if a troll charges your path, strategy and thinking won't save you. Speed and caution are your only allies. Number 21, Flubberworm. Flubberworms are the embodiment of magical boredom, turning zero excitement into an art form. These thick slug-like creatures measured up to a foot long and move at an almost imperceptible pace. They feed on vegetation and prefer damp shaded environments, making them common but unremarkable residents of Hogwarts greenhouses. Despite their dull nature, Flubberworms are surprisingly useful. Their mucus is a key ingredient in certain potions, and caring for them teaches patience to young witches and wizards. Fun fact, Flubberworms are edible and occasionally served at Hogwarts as fritters. Magical cuisine isn't always thrilling. Number 22, Blast-Ended Skrewt. These bizarre hybrids were bred by Hagrid by crossing manticores with fire crabs, resulting in a chitin plated monstrosity that looks like a burning spike-covered sausage with attitude. Skrewts can reach up to 10 ft long, shoot explosive jets of flame from their rear, and have temperaments that range from unpredictable to actively hostile. They're fast, territorial, and nearly impossible to train, making them a nightmare for Cure of Magical Creatures classes. In short, if a Skrewt chooses you as its target, running quickly becomes your only reasonable option. As Draco Malfoy once said, "Who wouldn't want pets that can burn, sting, and bite all at once?" Number 23, Kelpie. If there is one magical creature that turns a calm lake into a trap with teeth, it is the Kelpie. These shape-shifting water spirits lurk in rivers and lochs, usually appearing as beautiful horses at the water's edge until an unsuspecting witch or wizard climbs onto their back. The moment you touch them, a Kelpie's real nature reveals itself. Slimy skin, glowing eyes, and a deadly grip that drags victims underwater without hesitation. Kelpies feed on human flesh and are experts at disguise, making them one of the most dangerous aquatic beings in the wizarding world. In short, never trust a horse that smiles too nicely by the water's edge. Number 24, Chimera. The Chimera is a rare fire-breathing magical beast that combines three terrifying creatures into one. The head of a lion, the body of a goat growing from its back, and the tail of a dragon. Native to the mountains of ancient Greece, Chimeras are fiercely aggressive, unpredictable, and nearly impossible to reason with. Their lion head emits deafening roars, the goat's head bleats in eerie contradiction, and the dragon tail can scorch anything in its path. Wizards classify them as extremely dangerous and virtually untamable. In short, encountering a Chimera is catastrophically deadly. Speaking of catastrophes, your next lesson here at Stickman Hogwarts, All 10 Major Events After the Deathly Hallows Explained. Watch now before the Niffler sees how shiny you are.


=== All 10 RICHEST Marriages in Harry Potter EXPLAINED in Detail ===
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=OAxlIfc8Les

10th place, Ron Weasley and Hermione Granger. Yeah, I know your brain wants to scream, "No way, they're broke." But that's one of the biggest misconceptions in the fandom. This is the marriage that started in a tent and ended with a Gringotts vault. Ron Weasley spent 2 years as an Auror after the war, then left to co-run Weasleys' Wizard Wheezes with George. And this matters because the joke shop was already an enormous money spinner, the most popular storefront on Diagon Alley, actively expanding toward Hogsmeade, and selling products that occasionally set fire to things on purpose. Ron walked in as a co-owner with full profit sharing on a business that had zero competition and near unlimited demand from Hogwarts students alone. Now meet the other half of this household. After the Battle of Hogwarts, Hermione Granger started her Ministry career in the Department for the Regulation and Control of Magical Creatures, moved to the Department of Magical Law Enforcement, and eventually became Minister for Magic, the single highest political position in Wizarding Britain, with a salary to match. So, the combined picture, one half owns a share of the most successful retail operation in magical Britain. The other half literally runs the government. Together, that's entrepreneurial profit plus head of state salary, a combination most pure-blood dynasties ever managed to assemble. And let's be honest, Ron would get the Burrow, so that adds more to the marriage. Seven floors, a full garden full of gnomes, and a kitchen that produces the best food in the Wizarding world. Prime estate by any measure. Ninth place, Luna Lovegood and Rolf Scamander. When you think of Luna Lovegood getting married, Neville Longbottom is probably your first guess. But Luna married Rolf Scamander, grandson of Newt Scamander, the wizard who wrote Fantastic Beasts and Where to Find Them. That textbook has been required reading at Hogwarts since 1927. By the 1990s, it was in its 52nd edition with nearly 70 years of mandatory student purchases, reprints, and international editions generating royalty income on a scale most Wizarding businesses would And Rolf inherited all of it. And honestly, Luna never looked rich. Homemade jewelry, Spectrespecs made from magazines, and a general vibe that screamed eccentric louder than wealthy. But start counting the actual assets and the picture changes fast. The Lovegood estate is a fully owned multi-story magical modified tower sitting on private land. The family also owns the Quibbler, a fully independent Wizarding publication that somehow survived public ridicule, Ministry pressure, and boycotts without ever shutting down. That's either a loyal subscriber base large enough to sustain it or pre-existing family wealth quietly funding the operation. Probably both. And let's not forget, after the war, Luna became a celebrated magizoologist, traveling the world to discover and classify new magical creatures, although she eventually accepted that the Crumple-Horned Snorkack did not exist. Together, they spent their lives globetrotting and chasing creatures most wizards would run from. The Scamander wealth runs on autopilot. Textbook sales keep printing whether the heir is in a boardroom or knee-deep in a Niffler den somewhere in Patagonia. Eighth place, George Weasley and Angelina Johnson. By now, you already know that Weasleys' Wizard Wheezes is one of the most profitable storefronts on Diagon Alley. A 1,000 Galleon startup that turned into a retail empire with expansion plans for Hogsmeade. After Fred's death, the shop became both a business and a memorial, and George kept it thriving. He's co-owner, creative engine, and the face of a brand that prints money every September when a fresh class of Hogwarts students discover Skiving Snackboxes for the first time. Angelina Johnson, former Gryffindor Quidditch captain, Dumbledore's Army veteran, Battle of Hogwarts survivor, married George sometime after the war. And by all available evidence, never pursued a professional career. No Ministry post, no shop counter shifts. When your husband's business generates the kind of gold that Weasleys' Wizard Wheezes does, you get the rare luxury of doing exactly what you want with your time. Wild idea, I know. But in a world where most witches and wizards work until retirement, marrying into a self-sustaining joke empire is its own kind of financial magic. They had two children, a son named Fred after the uncle who started it all, and a daughter, Roxanne. The Weasley name used to mean stretched budgets and second-hand everything. In this household, it means the most successful brand on Diagon Alley, and a family that can finally afford not to count. Seventh place, Draco Malfoy and Astoria Greengrass. So, Draco Malfoy inherits the full Malfoy fortune, land seized during the Norman Conquest, centuries of Ministry influence, a mansion in Wiltshire loaded with dark artifacts, and vaults that survived two Wizarding Wars without a single Galleons confiscated. The Malfoys walked free every time because plausible deniability is the family's actual heirloom. His wife, Astoria Greengrass, brings the Greengrass estate, another ancient pure-blood fortune equally untouched by public scandal. Her family held a place on the Sacred 28 long before anyone turned that list into a political statement. Land, private vaults, and influence that predates the Ministry itself, all quietly maintained by a family whose entire financial strategy was three words: invest, inherit, wait. No careers necessary. Together, this marriage merges two of the largest inherited fortunes in magical Britain. Quick note, this is also the marriage Lucius and Narcissa disapproved of. Astoria refused to raise Scorpius with pure-blood supremacist values, which made family dinners at Malfoy Manor uncomfortably quiet. Sixth place, Orion Black and Walburga Black. Orion Black was the son of Arcturus Black III and Melania Macmillan, the direct male heir to one of the largest, oldest, and wealthiest pure-blood dynasties in magical Britain. Walburga Black was the daughter of Pollux Black and Irma Crabbe, also a Black by birth. This is the marriage where the House of Black married the House of Black and kept every last Knut in the family. So now, let's count the assets. The centerpiece is 12 Grimmauld Place, a massive ancestral townhouse in London, hidden from Muggle view and fortified by Orion with every security measure known to wizardkind. Concealment charms, unplottable status, wards powerful enough to still function decades after his death. This is prime London real estate that doesn't officially exist, which in the Wizarding world makes it priceless. Beyond the house, the Black family Gringotts vault old enough and deep enough to rival anything the Lestranges or Malfoys had stored underground. A collection of dark artifacts so dangerous they had to be sealed away permanently, hidden under the drawing-room floor, some of which Mundungus Fletcher later tried to sell on the black market, which tells you they had a street value even as stolen goods. Goblin-wrought silver, centuries of accumulated heirlooms, a golden ring with a Black family crest that made Creature physically weep when Sirius threw it away. And then there's the part that doesn't fit in a vault, political weight. The Blacks were Sacred 28 aristocracy with bloodline connections to the Malfoys, the Lestranges, the Rosiers, and the Crabbes. Neither Orion nor Walburga worked. Neither needed to. Here's the irony, this household ran entirely on inheritance, and the inheritance was so vast that it funded two sons through Hogwarts, absorbed a disowned heir giving up his claim, survived both parents' deaths, and still left enough behind for Sirius to pass Grimmauld Place and the remaining Black estate to Harry Potter years later. And speaking of Mr. Potter, fifth place, Harry Potter and Ginny Weasley. Harry Potter married Ginny Weasley, and when it comes to combined wealth, this couple might have the most lopsided starting point in Wizarding history. Harry inherits the entire Potter vault, a fortune built on Fleamont Potter's genius, who perfected Sleekeazy's Hair Potion, sold the company, and left behind enough gold to fill a Gringotts vault to the ceiling. Then Sirius Black dies and leaves Harry Grimmauld Place along with whatever remained of the Black estate. Two major pure-blood fortunes, one heir, zero siblings to split them with. Dumbledore left Harry a share of his estate. Snape had no one else, so Harry got that, too. Add his career, Harry became an Auror immediately after the war without any N.E.W.T.s, which is either inspirational or deeply unfair, depending on how you feel about standardized testing, and eventually rose to Head of the Department of Magical Law Enforcement. That's a senior government salary on top of a fortune he's barely touched. Now add Ginny. She played professionally for the Holyhead Harpies, one of the top Quidditch teams in Britain. Professional Quidditch players are the Wizarding world's equivalent of Premier League footballers, which means her earnings during those years were likely enormous. After retiring from the pitch, she moved to the Daily Prophet as Senior Quidditch Correspondent, trading a broom for a quill, but keeping the profile. So, the Potters aren't old money or new money, they're all the money, inherited, earned, gifted, and quietly compounding in a vault Harry probably forgets to check. Fourth place, Lucius Malfoy and Narcissa Black. Okay, Lucius Malfoy was independently wealthy and never held a formal job. His actual profession was influence. If he had Instagram, he'd be an influencer. Hair like that, manor like that, a million followers minimum. He donated enormous sums to the Minister for Magic, funded St. Mungo's Hospital, sat on the Hogwarts Board of Governors, and kept so many Ministry officials in his pocket that when he faced charges as a Death Eater after the First Wizarding War, the trial was practically a formality. The Imperius defense worked because Lucius had already paid for the verdict long before the courtroom doors opened. Narcissa Black brought the other half, the prestige, the bloodline, and the social armor of the House of Black, one of the oldest and most feared dynasties in Wizarding Britain. Her name alone opened doors that even Malfoy gold couldn't unlock. Of course, there were solid assets as well. Narcissa brought gold from two Sacred 28 bloodlines, the Blacks through father Cygnus, and the Rosiers through her mother Druella. Two ancient houses don't marry off a daughter empty-handed, which means inherited property and land came with the name. Together they turned Malfoy Manor into the political nerve center of pureblood Britain. Lucius handled the Ministry, Narcissa handled the social hierarchy. The household ran on wealth, intimidation, and a house-elf named Dobby, who was eventually freed by a sock, which remains one of the most expensive personal losses in wizarding history. And here's the part nobody talks about. After the second wizarding war, Lucius traded information on fellow Death Eaters to secure the family's freedom. The vault stayed, the manor stayed, the Malfoy name took a hit, but the fortune survived intact. Because in the Malfoy playbook, gold always outlasts reputation. Third place, Rodolphus Lestrange and Bellatrix Black. Rodolphus Lestrange was also Sacred 28 with generational wealth stored in Gringotts vaults so deep it required a dragon just to guard the entrance. The Lestrange vault contained mountains of gold, cursed treasure, and security enchantments. Voldemort trusted this vault so completely that he hid a Horcrux inside it, which says everything about the level of protection and paranoia that family money can buy. And outside Gringotts, the Lestranges held estates accumulated through centuries of pureblood intermarriage. Land and property merged from the Blacks, the Rosiers, and half a dozen families whose names didn't survive long enough to argue about it. Bellatrix Black brought the House of Black centuries of aristocratic prestige, inherited dark magic, and a fortune rivaling anything the Lestranges had stacked underground. Two Sacred 28 vaults merged on paper through a marriage that was purely transactional. The catch? Bellatrix married Rodolphus because it was expected of her, an obligation to the pureblood tradition, nothing more. She showed no affection toward him, never mentioned him in conversation, and never had children with him. Her devotion belonged entirely to the one with no nose. Rodolphus, for his part, fought loyally alongside her as a Death Eater, but was functionally invisible in his own marriage. Both spent 14 years in Azkaban for torturing the Longbottoms into permanent incapacitation. Both escaped and returned to Voldemort's side. Bellatrix died dueling Molly Weasley. Rodolphus survived and inherited a vault he'd barely touched in decades. So, on paper, this was the single wealthiest union in pureblood Britain. Second place, Fleamont Potter and Euphemia Potter. The Potter fortune already existed before Fleamont. His ancestor, Linfred and Stinchcombe, laid the groundwork centuries earlier by inventing remedies that evolved into potions still sold in the modern wizarding world, including Scourgify. His father, Henry Potter, sat on the Wizengamot. The family had gold, land, and political standing long before Fleamont ever picked up a cauldron. Then Fleamont graduated Hogwarts and created Sleekeazy's Hair Potion, marketed it with promise that two drops tame even the most bothersome hair. The potion exploded internationally, even making the front page of the Daily Prophet for winning over American wizards. Sales alone quadrupled the already considerable Potter fortune. And when Fleamont retired, he sold the entire company for what's described as a vast profit, turning a cosmetics brand into generational untouchable wealth. Euphemia's background is less documented, but the couple lived comfortably long enough that neither appears to have needed additional income. They had one child, James, born late in life after they'd given up hope of ever having children. They spoiled him accordingly, which tracks with a family that could afford to. The Potters also inherited the Cloak of Invisibility through the Peverell line, which isn't gold, but is literally priceless. Fleamont and Euphemia both died of dragon pox shortly after James's wedding, within days of each other. They never met Harry, but they left behind a vault full enough to fund three generations. But what if you could skip all of that and just manufacture gold directly? First place, Nicholas Flamel and Pernelle Flamel. Nicholas Flamel is the only known wizard in modern history to successfully create the Philosopher's Stone, a legendary alchemical object capable of transforming any base metal into pure gold. Actual unlimited gold production on demand for as long as the stone existed. And it existed for centuries. Nicholas was born in the 14th century. He and his wife, Pernelle, used the stone's second function, the Elixir of Life, to sustain themselves for over 600 years. 600 years of compounding wealth with zero overhead, zero competition, and zero mortality. The Flamels lived modestly and avoided political power entirely, which is the most terrifying part. They had the resources to buy Ministry, Hogwarts, and probably Gringotts itself, and chose not to. They funded Beauxbatons, their old school, and spent their later years in Devon enjoying opera and keeping an impossibly low profile for the richest couple on the planet. Then Voldemort came looking for the stone, and Nicholas made a decision that no one on this list would ever consider. He destroyed it, willingly gave up both immortality and infinite wealth, keeping only enough elixir for him and Pernelle to settle their affairs before dying. Canon never says where the Flamel fortune went, which means there's either a vault deep under Gringotts with six centuries of unclaimed gold gathering dust, or the greatest wizarding fortune in history simply vanished with two quiet old people from Devon. Either way, nobody else on this list even comes close. Now you know who married rich, but do you know who got rich first? All 12 richest wizarding families in Harry Potter explained in detail. That's your next lesson here at Stickman Hogwarts. Click now, the Flamel vault is still unclaimed.


=== All 24 MAJOR Events You Missed After The Deathly Hallows EXPLAINED in Detail ===
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=c3Qsj8FX6CM

Bye-bye, Dolores. Let's start with the best news first. Dolores Umbridge, the woman who made Voldemort look like he simply had a bad attitude, got exactly what she deserved. The pink cardigan-wearing, kitten-plate-collecting former High Inquisitor of Hogwarts was arrested, tried, and convicted for crimes against Muggle-borns. Her defense was essentially, "I was following orders." The Ministry said, "No. Hard no." Life sentence in Azkaban. Sad news is that by the time Umbridge arrived at Azkaban, the Dementors were gone. Every single one of them removed, which means Dolores Umbridge, of all people, was spared and got a quiet cell instead of the supernatural misery she had personally earned. The whole wizarding world felt that. It stings. And if you're wondering why Dementors were gone, let's just say someone took over the Ministry and actually started fixing things. We'll get to that later. For now, let's celebrate. Voldemort's stuck in limbo. After seven books, eight movies, and years of genuine childhood trauma, we finally saw Voldemort get what he deserved. But what happened to his soul? He didn't just die. Dying would have been too simple, too clean, and frankly too dignified for a man who spent decades terrorizing an entire civilization while looking like a haunted thumb. Because of the Horcruxes, the seven soul fragments he'd hidden around the world like the world's most unhinged scavenger hunt, Voldemort had mutilated his soul beyond any repair. When Harry finally defeated him, there was nothing left whole enough to pass on. Rowling confirmed that what remained of his soul was trapped in permanent limbo, unable to move forward, unable to come back. Formless, powerless, and stuck forever. For a man who dedicated his entire miserable existence to cheating death, that's not just defeat. That's the universe having the last laugh. Couldn't have happened to a nicer guy. The boy who got married. After surviving a killing curse twice, dying once, and defeating the most feared dark wizard of all time, Harry Potter did something absolutely nobody prepared for. He settled down. He married Ginny Weasley, which if you've been paying attention since Chamber of Secrets was absolutely inevitable. Together they had three children, James Sirius, named after his dad and godfather, because Harry apparently made a list. Albus Severus, named after two headmasters, one of whom made Harry's life extremely complicated on purpose. And Lily Luna, named after his mom and the girl who spent school searching for creatures nobody else believed in. Harry grew up in a cupboard under the stairs with no family, no birthday cake, and no one who cared. He ended up with three kids and a legendary wife. The relationship that took seven years. Look, nobody was surprised. These two had been arguing, bickering, and making eyes at each other since third year, while everyone around them screamed internally. Ron and Hermione got married after the war and had two children, Rose and Hugo. Rose presumably inherited Hermione's terrifying academic discipline and Ron's ability to eat an entire meal in 4 minutes. Hugo remains less documented, which honestly might mean he's the most normal Weasley in existence. Ron became godfather to Harry's eldest son, James, because apparently these three couldn't even have children without staying completely intertwined. The golden trio now with additional tiny golden trio members. How cute. The wizarding world never stood a chance. Neville's perfect ending. Neville Longbottom, the boy who forgot his wand, melted 17 cauldrons, and spent most of first year being terrorized by his own classmates, ended up as one of the most respected figures at Hogwarts. Yeah. Take a moment. After briefly joining the Auror Office following the war, because apparently decapitating a giant snake with a legendary sword qualifies you for most jobs, Neville followed his real passion and became Hogwarts Herbology professor, then head of Gryffindor house. The same house that once watched him get his legs jinxed by Draco Malfoy in a corridor. From forgotten kid to the person shaping the next generation of Gryffindors. The arc. The absolute arc. And before you say it, no, he didn't end up with Luna. The Deathly Hallows films really pushed that idea, and the internet ran with it. But he actually married Hannah Abbott, a fellow Dumbledore's Army member, and they lived above the Leaky Cauldron together. Herbology professor by day, pub landlord's husband by night. Honestly, perfect ending for Neville. From loony to legendary. Luna Lovegood spent years being dismissed as strange, dreamy, and slightly out of touch with reality. Then she became a magizoologist, traveled the world, discovered multiple entirely new magical species that nobody had ever classified. But there's more shocking news. She married Rolf Scamander, grandson of Newt Scamander, the man who wrote the book on magical creatures. They had twin sons, Lorcan and Lysander, and raised them in an environment where being unconventional wasn't just accepted, it was basically required. The girl everyone underestimated ended up expanding wizarding knowledge in ways that nobody else thought to try. Funny how that works. And about the Crumple-Horned Snorkack hunt? Luna never found them, but she still looked and had absolutely no regrets. Dementors got fired. Remember Dolores and how she missed her Dementor welcome party? Well, here's why. For centuries, the wizarding world's brilliant solution to prison security was hiring 10-ft tall, soul-draining depression creatures and hoping for the best. Completely airtight, except for the part where the Dementors happily switched sides the moment Voldemort promised them unlimited feeding rights. Minor flaw in the plan. After the war, Kingsley Shacklebolt, the new Minister for Magic, looked at the entire Dementor situation and said, "Absolutely not. Gone. All of them. Permanently." Azkaban got actual security upgrades instead. Trained Aurors, magical wards, enchanted constructs. A prison run by actual people making actual decisions rather than creatures that fed on human history. Wild idea. It only took the wizarding world roughly a thousand years to get there. The boy who got promoted. Harry skipped his seventh year at Hogwarts, joined the Auror Office with zero formal qualifications, and by age 26 was already running the entire department. He became the youngest person to ever hold that position in Ministry history. An institution historically dominated by wizards who spent decades carefully climbing ranks, attending meetings, filling out forms, and generally doing things the slow, respectable way. Harry did it before his brain had finished developing, which given some of his decisions across seven books, tracks perfectly. He also returned to Hogwarts occasionally to guest lecture Defense Against the Dark Arts. Voldemort spent 30 years trying to take over the wizarding world. Harry ran it by 26. Casual. Voldemort who? For years, saying Voldemort's name out loud in wizarding Britain was the social equivalent of walking into Azkaban, tapping a Dementor on the shoulder, and asking for a hug. Grown adults whispered, "You Know Who. He Who Must Not Be Named." because saying his actual name felt genuinely dangerous. Which during the second war, it literally was, because the Death Eaters put a tracking charm on it. After the war, though, something remarkable happened. People just started saying it. Voldemort. Out loud. Casually. In conversations. Without whispering or looking over their shoulders. His name lost its power the moment he lost his. The most feared dark wizard in a century reduced to just a name that people said freely over breakfast. Turns out the best way to defeat a legend is to make him ordinary. Hogwarts Remembrance Day. The Battle of Hogwarts took a lot of people we loved. Fred Weasley, Remus Lupin, Nymphadora Tonks, Colin Creevey, and 50 others who showed up to fight when they didn't have to. The wizarding world didn't just move on and pretend that didn't happen. An official memorial was established, and McGonagall oversaw annual commemorations at Hogwarts every year on the anniversary of the battle, because some things deserve to be remembered properly. Not just in private grief or quiet conversations, but officially, publicly, and permanently. For a world that spent years pretending Voldemort didn't exist, choosing to never forget what he cost was the most important thing they could have done. No mercy for Death Eaters. After the war, the Ministry did something it had spectacularly failed to do after Voldemort's first defeat. It actually held people accountable. No quiet pardons. No looking the other way because someone had a famous name or a large vault at Gringotts. Kingsley's reformed Ministry put Death Eaters on trial publicly, convicted them for their crimes, and sent them to Azkaban. The excuses were predictable. Imperius curse. Wrong place, wrong time. Just following orders. Some were believed, most weren't. For the first time in wizarding being powerful and well-connected wasn't enough to escape consequences. It wasn't a perfect system, but compared to what came before it, it was nothing short of revolutionary. Fred's name lived on. George Weasley lost his twin brother, the person who had been beside him every single day of his life, and then kept going anyway. He married Angelina Johnson. Yes, the same Angelina who had once dated Fred, which sounds complicated until you remember that grief and love don't follow neat rules. And together they had two children, a daughter named Roxanne and a son named Fred, because George wasn't going to let that name disappear. He kept the joke shop running, kept the laughter going, and kept showing up, which was the hardest thing he could have done, and also the most him thing imaginable. Fred the Second grew up with his uncle's name and his father's humor. The legacy continued. It had to. Kingsley fixed everything. Okay, so by now you know Kingsley Shacklebolt took over the Ministry of Magic and took away Dementors, held proper trials for Death Eater collaborators, and somehow managed to do all of that without a single unnecessary speech, a Ministry memo nobody read, or a portrait of himself in the lobby. He ran the entire Ministry with a level of basic competence and fairness so rare that building it practically qualified as a miracle of nature. He wasn't political. He didn't give speeches about his own greatness. He just did the job properly, which in wizarding Britain was apparently a staggering novelty. He eventually handed over the ministry that actually functioned to Hermione Granger, possibly his greatest achievement of all. Dudley changed. Nobody expected this. Not Harry, not Dudley, and definitely not anyone who watched Dudley spend 11 years making Harry's life miserable. But when the Dursleys went into hiding during the war, Dudley did something completely unprecedented. He offered Harry his hand and said he didn't think Harry was a waste of space. Incredible stuff from a man whose previous contributions to Harry's life included sitting on him. According to Rowling, they never became close, but they reached what she called Christmas card terms. Occasional visits, awkward silences, children playing together while the adults pretended 19 years of trauma didn't happen. Deeply British. Surprisingly touching. Dudley even made sure his own kids had no trace of his parents' prejudice. Growth is possible. Apparently, Hermione became Minister. Hermione Granger was called an insufferable know-it-all in first year by her peers, by a professor, by basically everyone who encountered her before they desperately needed her help. That same insufferable know-it-all went back to Hogwarts after the war, finished her seventh year, sat her NEWTs, joined the Ministry, reformed magical creature rights legislation, dismantled pure-blood discrimination laws, rose through magical law enforcement, and eventually became Minister for Magic, the most powerful position in wizarding Britain, held by a girl everyone rolled their eyes at in Charms class. Every single person who ever called her insufferable had to watch that happen. The witch who ran Hogwarts. Minerva McGonagall spent decades being the strictest, most dependable, most quietly terrifying witch at Hogwarts. Then Voldemort fell, and the wizarding world finally caught up with what her students had always known. She received the Order of Merlin first class, got her own chocolate frog card, and became Headmistress of Hogwarts. Her first decisions included allowing war-displaced students to return and finish their education, and overseeing the complete restoration of Hogwarts' protective enchantments. She ran the school with the same combination of compassion and zero nonsense that had defined her entire career. Some things do not change. McGonagall is one of them. But even she had inherited one problem so old, so stubborn, and so consistently disastrous that six teachers in six years hadn't been able to fix it. Until now. The DADA curse was broken. For over 50 years, the Defense Against the Dark Arts position at Hogwarts was cursed. Dumbledore refused to give it to Voldemort when he applied, and Voldemort, well, being Voldemort, cursed the job out of spite. No teacher lasted more than a year, not one. The position went through Quirrell, Lockhart, Lupin, fake Moody, Umbridge, and Snape in six years alone. A rotation of disasters so consistent it became a running joke among students. After Voldemort's defeat, the curse broke, just like that. Gone. McGonagall hired proper, capable professors who stayed for actual careers. Defense Against the Dark Arts became a normal subject taught by normal people who weren't secretly possessed, fraudulent government spies, or werewolves. Hogwarts students born after the war genuinely did not know how good they had it. Hermione won again. Hermione Granger had been trying to fix house-elf rights third year when literally nobody wanted to hear it. SPEW was mocked, ignored, and treated as an embarrassing hobby. Then she joined the Ministry, got actual power, and made it policy. For the first time in wizarding history, legislation was introduced specifically protecting the rights of house-elves and other non-human magical beings. It wasn't perfect overnight. Centuries of deeply embedded attitudes don't vanish because of one law, but it existed on paper. The girl who wouldn't get a single student to wear a badge ended up changing wizarding law. Never underestimate someone with a cause and enough patience to outlast everyone who laughed at them. Fawkes flew away. After Dumbledore died, Fawkes sang his lament over the castle, a sound described as simultaneously beautiful and heartbreaking, and then left Hogwarts entirely. Rowling confirmed he never bonded with another wizard. Not McGonagall, not Harry, not anyone. Just gone. Back to the wild, alone. Phoenixes are extraordinarily rare magical creatures capable of incredible loyalty, and apparently Fawkes decided that one wizard was enough. One relationship worth that level of devotion. Whether that speaks to something unique about Dumbledore or simply the depth of what they shared is up for debate. What isn't up for debate is that Hogwarts lost something irreplaceable the night Dumbledore fell, and Fawkes knew it better than anyone. Snape was finally honored. When a Hogwarts Headmaster dies, their portrait is traditionally hung in the Headmaster's office. Snape's wasn't, because his tenure had ended in betrayal, or so everyone believed at the time. The portrait was rejected, left out. But then Harry, the person Snape had spent 17 years protecting while being absolutely horrible to, intervened. He ensured Snape's portrait was installed and personally told the wizarding world what Snape had actually done. The bravery, the sacrifice, the love that had driven every decision. The portrait went up. The record was set straight by the one person who had every reason to stay quiet and chose not to. Teddy and Victoire. Teddy Lupin, orphan son of Remus Lupin and Nymphadora Tonks, raised by his grandmother Andromeda with Harry as godfather, grew up so thoroughly embedded in the Potter and Weasley families that the next step was basically inevitable. James Sirius Potter caught Teddy kissing Victoire Weasley, Bill and Fleur's eldest daughter, at Platform 9 and 3/4, and immediately ran to tell everyone about it. Because of course he did. He's James Sirius Potter. Teddy inherited his mother's Metamorphmagus abilities, meaning he could change his appearance at will. He was reportedly changing his hair color to match Victoire's favorite shade at the time. Remus and Tonks would have found that either adorable or deeply embarrassing, probably both. Petunia's hidden secret. Petunia Dursley spent the better part of three decades pretending magic didn't exist, treating her nephew like an inconvenience, and complaining about owls. Not exactly the warmest character in the series, which is why this detail hits differently. When Dumbledore left baby Harry on the Dursleys' steps, he wrapped him in a blanket. Petunia kept it for decades. Hidden among her belongings, never thrown out, never mentioned. After her death, Dudley found it while sorting through her things and returned it to Harry. In The Cursed Child, Harry admits he keeps it close and considers it a source of comfort. Petunia never said the words that mattered, never apologized, never softened publicly, but she kept the blanket. Make of that what you will. The oldest house-elf. Kreacher spent most of the series being bitter, resentful, and deeply unpleasant, which, given that he'd spent decades alone in a house full of dark magic memorabilia, is honestly understandable. Then Harry treated him with basic dignity, gave him Regulus Black's locket as a gesture of respect, and Kreacher transformed entirely. He led the Hogwarts house-elves into battle during the Battle of Hogwarts, charging at Death Eaters with kitchen knives and the energy of someone who had been waiting their entire life for a purpose. He survived, lived out his days working proudly in the Hogwarts kitchens, and died at the remarkable age of 666. The most metal retirement in wizarding history. Kreacher deserved every single year of it. The Golden Trio's Award. Chocolate frog cards are not handed out casually. Think of them as the wizarding equivalent of the Oscars, except the category is literally save the entire world, and the award jumps out of a box and tries to escape. These are reserved for the most legendary figures in wizarding history. Dumbledore, Merlin, founders of entire magical institutions. So, when Harry, Ron, and Hermione all received their own cards, that was the wizarding world's official way of saying, "Yes, you actually did that." Harry obviously got one. Hermione obviously got one. But Ron, Ron Weasley, the boy who spent seven years being overshadowed by his best friend and his own self-doubt, got one, too. And according to Rowling, that card was his proudest moment. Not the battles, not the Horcrux hunting, the card. Honestly, most relatable thing he ever did. Now, let's talk about all 10 marriages after the Deathly Hallows explained. That's your next lesson here at Stigman Hogwarts. Watch now before Kreacher says Master Regulus would be disappointed.


=== All 13 MAJOR Blacks in Harry Potter EXPLAINED in Detail ===
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=oM6stMxU_s8

Like Horace Black. When we talk about the Blacks, it's always serious, Azkaban, and a cursed tapestry. But before the big names, before the betrayals and the disownments, there was a man born in 1808 who started it all. Like Horace Black, the oldest name still visible on the Black family tapestry, sorted into Slytherin before anyone alive today could verify it, and married to a pureblood witch named Magenta Tripe. Why is Like Horace Black important? Well, besides the amount of gold in the family vault and the motto he established Toujours Pur. That's French for always pure. Like Horace also found a beautiful Muggle townhouse in London. He, quote, persuaded the owner to leave. Those quotation marks are doing heavy lifting, and claimed it as the family seat, number 12 Grimmauld Place. A pureblood dynasty headquartered in a home they took from the very people they despised. How ironic. That house would eventually hold a seven-century-old tapestry, a screaming portrait, a row of mounted house-elf heads, and one of Voldemort's Horcruxes. But the story was only the beginning. His great-grandson would take it somewhere Like Horace never imagined. Phineas Nigellus Black. He attended Hogwarts, sorted into Slytherin, came back as a professor, hated every second of it, and got promoted to headmaster. Connections, money, the family name, with the Blacks, anything else would be surprising. He became the least popular headmaster in the school's history. By his own great-great-grandson's accounting, he took the position for prestige, openly despised the students under his care, and once attempted to revise the admissions process to exclude Muggle-borns entirely. Very Slytherin. Luckily for Hermione Granger, the Ministry refused him. The fact that a sitting headmaster attempted it at all says enough about the kind of wizard he was. And beyond bloodline, Phineas gave the family something they'd never had before, a permanent seat inside Hogwarts. His portrait hung in the headmaster's office, the Blacks had a direct line into the school's inner workings long after he died. The portrait kept working for decades. It helped Dumbledore relay a message that saved Arthur Weasley's life during the Second Wizarding War. Later, it helped Snape deliver the sword of Gryffindor to Harry Potter. Phineas married Ursula Flint, a pureblood witch. Fun fact, of the 36 Blacks documented on the tapestry, 25 trace directly back to this couple. Their children were Phineas the Second, Arcturus the Second, Belvina, Cygnus the Second, and one more, the one who mattered most to the story that followed. His name was Sirius Black the Second, named after an uncle who died at eight years old. Sirius Black the Second became the first in the family to carry that name into adulthood. Born in 1877, he attended Hogwarts while his own father, Phineas Nigellus, was still headmaster. Whether that made his years there easier or more complicated probably depended on the day. He married Hesper Gamp, had three children, Arcturus, Like Horace, and Regulus, and lived to 75. By Black family standards, that counts as a quiet life. But this was the senior branch, the main bloodline, the one that inherited Grimmauld Place, the vault, and the family name. The branch that would eventually produce Regulus Black, the boy who stole a Horcrux, and of course Sirius Black the Third, godfather to Harry Potter. So yeah, the first generation of Black family chaos traces straight back to this Sirius. The second wave? Well, we'll get there. As for Phineas the Second, Arcturus the Second, and Belvina, there isn't much info. Maybe they married Muggles. That would explain the silence, but the quiet life ended with Sirius the Second. His eldest son wanted more. Arcturus Black the Third. Arcturus Black the Third was the man who solved a problem the family had carried for generations. The Blacks had the prestige, the manor, the motto. What they never had was a formal, recognized position inside the Wizarding government itself. So, Arcturus looked at his vault and said, "I'll buy my way up." He donated a significant amount of gold to the Ministry of Magic. In return, he received an Order of Merlin, first class, the highest honor in Wizarding Britain. Officially awarded for services to the Ministry. His grandson, Sirius, would later tell Harry the truth plainly, his grandfather bought it. Arcturus lived to 90, longer than any Black on record. But while his branch held the vault and the house, the other branch of the family was building something louder, and it started with Pollux Black. Pollux came from the second major branch of Phineas Nigellus's children. He married Irma Crabbe and became a father at 13 years old, a detail that says everything about how seriously this family took the preservation of the bloodline. By all accounts, Pollux was exactly what the Black family wanted to produce, sharp, proud, and unrelenting, fanatically devoted to the idea of pureblood as a personal conviction he carried with him into everything. They had three children. Each of them would leave a mark on the family story. And the eldest one, if the tapestry could talk, it would say her name with fear. Walburga Black. You probably know her from the screaming portrait in the hallway of Grimmauld Place. And if you don't know who she actually is, she's the one who decorates the family tapestry. And by decorates, we mean permanently removes anyone who breathes wrong, but we'll get to that. She married her second cousin, Orion Black, Arcturus the Third's son, which meant the two branches of Phineas Nigellus's bloodline merged back into one. Gross. Fun fact, Walburga's maiden name was Black, and after the wedding, it stayed Black. She transformed Grimmauld Place into a monument to pureblood ideology. The tapestry in the drawing room became the family's ultimate authority. Anyone who married wrong, believed wrong, or simply asked too many questions was burned off personally. So yeah, every name you see burned, Walburga Black's work. She supported Voldemort's vision and raised both her sons inside it. Regulus the Second shared her views completely, and Walburga made no secret of the fact that he was her favorite. Sirius the Third wanted nothing to do with any of it. She considered him a blood traitor, and when he left at 16, she burned his name off the tapestry the same day. But her brother's name, that burned cost her the most. Alphard Black. His crime was this, when his nephew Sirius ran away from Grimmauld Place at 16, Alphard gave him gold, a significant amount according to Sirius, enough to begin a life somewhere else. One act of generosity, Walburga burned him off the record. In the Black family, helping the wrong person earned you the same treatment as being the wrong person. But the money did not disappear. Sirius later used gold from vault 711 in Gringotts, almost certainly from Alphard's inheritance, to buy Harry a Firebolt while writing him from hiding. Gold that one Black quietly gave another eventually bought a broomstick for a Potter. But Alphard's younger brother took the family in a very different direction. Cygnus Black the Third. The youngest of Pollux's three children, Cygnus became a parent at 13, and with him the second branch of the Black family chaos officially began. He married Druella Rosier from another established pureblood house, and by all accounts was not pleased when their first child turned out to be a daughter. In a family where sons carried the name forward, daughters were a disappointment. He tried again. Another daughter, and again, a third. Eventually, Cygnus accepted what the family tree had given him and moved on. Seven centuries of Black men carrying the name forward, and it ended with Cygnus. What he could not have known was that those three daughters would go on to make more noise in Wizarding history than any Black man ever had. And the eldest, his firstborn, the daughter he had not wanted, became the most legendary Black of them all, Bellatrix Lestrange. If the House of Blacks spent seven centuries constructing a philosophy of pureblood supremacy, Bellatrix was the one who followed it to its absolute conclusion. She married Rodolphus Lestrange from another established pureblood family, but the marriage was incidental. Her devotion belonged to Voldemort entirely, and Bellatrix called it love. Voldemort called it having a reliable employee. She became his most dangerous follower, his most capable weapon, and the mother of his only child, Delphini. Yes, that's canon from the Cursed Child. Potterheads, take a breath. After Voldemort fell in 1981, most Death Eaters claimed that they were under the Imperius Curse. Bellatrix went to Azkaban proudly, after torturing Frank and Alice Longbottom into permanent insanity first. She picked up exactly where she left off the moment he returned. She killed her own cousin, Sirius Black, during the Battle of the Department of Mysteries. One curse, and he fell through the veil. A year later, she killed her niece, Nymphadora Tonks, during the Battle of Hogwarts. Where Walburga burned names off the tapestry, Bellatrix made sure they stayed off permanently. Family meant nothing when it got in the way of the cause. Bellatrix was the purest product of the Black family ever made, but her middle sister understood something Bellatrix never did, Andromeda Tonks. Cygnus's second daughter, of all the Blacks who broke from the family, Andromeda did it most completely, and paid the highest price for it. She married Ted Tonks, a Muggle-born wizard, and her aunt, Walburga, burned her name off the tapestry the same day. She and Ted built a life entirely outside the pureblood world, and raised their daughter, Nymphadora, far from everything Grimmauld Place stood for. When the war came, she and Ted sheltered Harry Potter and Hagrid in their home after the Seven Potters operation. Endured interrogation by Death Eaters, and gave nothing away. The war took Ted first, then Nymphadora, killed in the Battle of Hogwarts by Bellatrix, her own aunt. Andromeda was left with a newborn grandson, and raised him alone. Harry Potter became Teddy's godfather. The boy the Blacks had spent generations despising ended up tied permanently to the one branch of the family that had chosen him first. Her younger sister survived the war, too, but through a very different kind of loyalty, Narcissa Malfoy. Narcissa was the anomaly of the Black family, never quite accounted for. Where every Black before her had dark hair and darker eyes, she was blonde and blue-eyed. Where every Black child was named after a star or constellation, she was named after a flower. She married Lucius Malfoy. They first crossed paths at Hogwarts, both Slytherin, Lucius one year ahead. He courted her properly, gave her the Malfoy family brooch, which she kept for the rest of her life. Two of the wealthiest pureblood families in Britain running out of eligible matches, the union made strategic sense for everyone, but Narcissa fell in love with him. She defended Lucius against her own sister, stood by him when Voldemort humiliated him in his own home, and never once wavered. She was never a Death Eater, never took the Dark Mark. She shared the family's beliefs, but Narcissa's true loyalty was never to the ideology, it was to her family. And when Voldemort gave her 16-year-old son an impossible task, she proved it. She went behind the Dark Lord's back to Severus Snape and made him take an unbreakable vow to protect Draco. Bellatrix protested, Narcissa didn't care. When the moment came in the Forbidden Forest, Voldemort standing over Harry Potter's body waiting for confirmation, Narcissa knelt down, felt a heartbeat, and told the Dark Lord what he wanted to hear. Her only goal was to get inside Hogwarts and find Draco. That lie ended the war. After the battle, the Malfoys avoided Azkaban entirely. The Black family spent seven centuries trying to produce the perfect pureblood dynasty. Narcissa turned out to be the one who changed wizarding history for entirely personal reasons. This was one side of the family. The other side produced someone the wizarding world would never forget, Sirius Black III. Walburga's eldest son, the third Black to carry the name Sirius, and the only one who made it mean something entirely different. Every Black before him had walked into Hogwarts and straight into Slytherin. Sirius walked into Gryffindor. The tapestry was already warming up. He spent his school years becoming exactly what the Black family most feared producing, someone who genuinely didn't care about any of it. He built deep friendships across bloodlines, helped create the Marauder's Map, and partly to help his werewolf friend avoid getting caught during his full moons, and mastered illegal Animagus transformation at 15, becoming the black dog the family would never have chosen as their symbol. He left Grimmauld Place at 16 and never went back. What Sirius built outside the family turned out to be his real legacy. He became godfather to Harry Potter, and in that role he gave the Black name something it had never carried in seven centuries of history, genuine unconditional protection. When he died, everything passed to Harry, the vault, the house, the inheritance that Lycoris had built and seven generations of Blacks had carefully preserved, handed to a half-blood raised by Muggles. But his younger brother made every choice Sirius refused to make, Regulus Black II. Walburga's younger son, named after the brightest star in the constellation Leo. And in his early years, he lived up to everything that name meant to his family, Slytherin, Quidditch Seeker, Death Eater at 16 with his parents' full approval. Regulus was so proud of his allegiance that he decorated his bedroom with newspaper clippings about Voldemort. He was, by every measure the Black family had ever used, the perfect son. What changed him was Creature. When Voldemort used the Black family house-elf to test the defenses around one of his Horcruxes and left him to die in a cave, something shifted in Regulus permanently. He went back to that cave himself, drank the cursed potion from the basin, swapped the real locket for a fake, and ordered Creature to go home and destroy the original. The Inferi pulled him under. He was 18. Inside the fake locket, he left a note signed with three initials, R.A.B., Regulus Arcturus Black. It sat unread in Grimmauld Place for nearly 20 years, while his family mourned him as a loyal Death Eater and the wizarding world had no idea he had ever existed. He was the first person to successfully sabotage Voldemort's plan for immortality. He did it alone, in secret, with no expectation of recognition, and got none for two decades. The obedient son turned out to be the bravest Black of them all. So, the Blacks lost everything to a Potter. Turns out the Potters have secrets, too. The lives of all 11 Potters explained in detail. That's your next lesson here at Stickman Hogwarts. Click now before Creature hides the remote.


=== What If All These Harry Potter Scenarios Were TRUE? ===
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=t2KX_hQEbNA

What if Snape raised Harry? Picture this, Dumbledore shows up at Spinner's End with a one-year-old and says, "Congratulations, Severus. He's yours now." And somehow Snape actually takes him, which is bold. Dumbledore naturally allows it because if there's one thing he loves more than lemon drops, it's a suspiciously elaborate plan. The prophecy is still ticking along nicely. Harry's the chosen one, whether he grows up in a cupboard or a house full of simmering potions and emotional repression. So, they commit to the long game. Snape raises Harry, teaches him magic absurdly early, and keeps Voldemort completely in the dark. His Occlumency is flawless. Nothing leaks. Meanwhile, he's quietly raising the exact weapon Voldemort is trying to find. Subtle, very subtle. But here's what Snape also teaches him, Occlumency. Not at 15 after years of trauma, like a cannon. From childhood. By the time Harry walks into Hogwarts, he can read surface thoughts the way most first years read textbook covers. Casually, effortlessly, and without anyone noticing. So, year one, he sits in Defense Against the Dark Arts, glances at Quirrell, and immediately knows there's something very wrong with the back of that man's head. He doesn't panic. He tells Dumbledore, and the two of them handle it quietly before Voldemort even gets close to the Stone. Year two, Lucius slips the diary into Ginny's cauldron, and Harry pulls it out of her hands before she writes a single word in it because he caught the intention right out of Lucius's mind at Flourish and Blotts. Hands it straight to Dumbledore. Year three, he spots Peter Pettigrew hiding in Ron's pocket because a rat with human thoughts is not exactly subtle when you're a trained Legilimens. Sirius Black walks out of Azkaban a free man before Christmas. So, yeah, a mind reader with an invisibility cloak, Hogwarts never stood a chance. The hat drops him into Slytherin, obviously. Everything Snape built into this kid points one direction. Draco Malfoy, who fully expected to own that house, suddenly has to share it with the most famous wizard alive. And Draco doesn't handle competition well. So, maybe the hat decides to be funny and sends him somewhere dramatic, Gryffindor. The golden trio still forms just in green ties and with slightly more strategy than luck. When Voldemort finally returns, he's walking into a war he's already losing. Dumbledore has been guiding the Horcrux hunt for years, and Harry's feeding him intel from inside every room he enters. Snape runs the double agent game from the Death Eater side. The prophecy plays out the same way, just years earlier with fewer graves. Turns out Slytherin Harry might have been a better Harry Potter than the Gryffindor one ever was. But custody isn't the only thing that could have gone differently. What if Neville was the chosen one? So, Voldemort had two options, and he picked Harry. But flip that. He walks into the Longbottom house instead. In this case, Voldemort targets Frank and Alice. So, during the night of 1981, they're gone. Meanwhile, Neville lands with Augusta Longbottom, a woman who raises children the way drill sergeants run boot camps. Now, picture Harry's life in this timeline. Two loving parents, a vault full of gold, and absolutely no reason to ever develop empathy. By second year, he's strolling through Hogwarts corridors like he personally owns the castle, hexing kids who look at him funny, charming every professor except Snape. The Sorting Hat probably screamed Gryffindor before it touched his head. Brave, talented, and insufferable. Sound familiar? That's James at 15, except this version never gets humbled. And then there's Snape. In canon, his entire spy career runs on one thing, guilt over getting Lily killed. Here, Lily's very much alive, married to the man Snape despises, living the exact life he wanted. So, Snape has zero reason to flip. Dumbledore's most valuable double agent just doesn't exist. Voldemort's inner circle stays completely sealed. Rough. Meanwhile, Sirius never gets framed. Pettigrew gets caught, and Harry grows up with a godfather and two Marauders still standing.
