Starting at 18th place, Unfogging the Future, the book that proves divination is just anxiety with incense. This is a standard divination textbook meant to teach students how to interpret tea leaves, crystal balls, and vague doom without panicking. In theory, in practice, it convinces half the class they're about to die before lunch. The book is full of symbolic disasters, dark shapes, broken lines, and ominous shadows, which Professor Trrellani treats as academic discussion, and students treat as a personal threat. Hogwarts allows it because divination is considered mostly harmless, assuming you don't take it too seriously. The forbidden element isn't dark magic. It's psychological damage. This book doesn't curse you, it plants ideas. And once a teenager thinks fate is sealed, chaos usually follows. Hogwarts keeps it on the curriculum because not all danger is magical. Some of it is just badly interpreted homework. Moving into 17th place, Death Omens: What to do when you know the worst is coming. Comforting title, no notes. This book specializes in identifying signs of impending death, including grim sightings, falling objects, mysterious chills, and conveniently anything slightly unusual. It's meant as a reference guide, but it mostly functions as a panic accelerator. Harry encounters this book during divination lessons where it immediately convinces everyone that seeing a black dog means you're doomed. Hogwarts keeps it because understanding omens is part of magical education. They do not keep it because it's helpful. The forbidden edge here is subtle. The book treats death as inevitable. No counter measures, no reassurance, just this is happening. Prepare accordingly. Hogwarts allows it because banning books about death doesn't stop students from thinking about it. But maybe less detail would have been fine. At number 16, The Standard Book of Spells, grade seven. Yes, really. By seventh year, Hogwarts officially stops pretending. This book introduces advanced combat spells, powerful charms, and magic that can permanently injure if misused. Stunning, shielding, binding. This is no longer feather floating territory. The reason it lands on this list is context. By grade seven, students are legally adults, emotionally unstable, and living during a wizarding war. This book hands them real power and assumes responsibility will magically appear alongside it. The spells inside are regulated but effective. They're designed for real world threats, not exams. That makes this book quietly dangerous. Hogwarts keeps it mandatory because ignorance is worse. But the jump from textbooks to battlefield readiness is abrupt and suddenly homework fights back. Now at 15th place, Fantastic Beasts and Where to Find Them early editions. The version where the footnotes were more dangerous than the creatures. Early editions of Fantastic Beasts and Where to Find Them were enthusiastic. Before Regulation, this book included far more detailed handling notes, breeding observations, and do not attempt unless qualified instructions that students absolutely attempted anyway. Can you blame them? These versions described creatures like dragons, acroantulas, and nifflers with a tone that suggested curiosity first, consequences later. Hogwarts kept the early editions for academic completeness. And because rewriting magical zoology history would have been suspicious. The forbidden edge isn't dark magic, it's overconfidence. This book makes magical creatures sound manageable, which is how you end up with burns, bites, and at least one detention involving a forest. Later editions were softened for a reason. The originals, they trusted readers far too much. Landing at 14th place, Hogwarts: A History. The book that knows everything and tells you almost nothing. Hogwarts: A History is required reading, heavily referenced, and strategically incomplete. It documents the castle's founding, expansions, and notable events. While carefully skipping anything that might encourage exploration, secret passages, vague, forbidden rooms, footnotes, actual dangers glossed over politely. Hogwarts keeps this book public because controlling the narrative is easier than controlling teenagers. The forbidden aspect isn't what's written, it's what's missing. Entire sections of castle history are sanitized, minimized, or quietly omitted, which is why Hermione reads it obsessively and still misses half of Hogwarts secrets. This book isn't dangerous because it reveals too much. It's dangerous because it convinces you that you already know enough. You don't. Sliding into 13th place, The Tales of Beetle the Bard: Original Wizarding Edition. Fairy Tales then really should have stayed bedtime stories. The original wizarding edition of The Tales of Beetle the Bard isn't cute. It's dark, moralistic, and casually cruel. These stories were written to warn young witches and wizards, not comfort them. And they don't soften the lesson. This addition includes the tale of the three brothers, which introduces the Deathly Hallows like a fable and then quietly changes wizarding history forever. Hogwarts keeps the original because it's culturally important and because pretending the hallows don't exist went terribly last time. The forbidden edge is subtle. These stories normalize death, sacrifice, and consequences far beyond a child's understanding. They're fairy tales. They just happen to be true. At number 12, The Monster Book of Monsters. The only textbook that tries to bite back. This book is literally alive and aggressively so. The Monster Book of Monsters is required reading for care of magical creatures, and it treats students as either threats or snacks. Unless properly restrained, it snaps, lunges, and attempts to maim on contact. Why is this at Hogwarts? Because the subject itself is dangerous. The book teaches students how to handle magical creatures by becoming one. The lesson is clear. Control, patience, and respect, or you lose a finger. The Forbidden Edge isn't dark magic. It's physical harm. This book has injured more students than some curses, and that's before the lesson even starts. Hogwarts allows it because shielding students from danger doesn't prepare them for reality. But maybe one less set of teeth would have helped. At 11th place, ancient runes made easy complete editions. Easy doing a lot of lying. The complete editions go far beyond translating old alphabets. These versions include full rune arrays, binding inscriptions, and sustained enchantment formulas. The kind of magic that doesn't need a wand once it's set. This is the branch of magic used in protective enchantments, cursed objects, ancient vaults, and magical architecture. Runes are how spells stay active for centuries. One wrong symbol doesn't fail. It locks in the mistake forever. Students like Hermione take this subject seriously for a reason. Ancient runes underpin wards, contracts, and magical defenses used across the wizarding world, including Hogwarts itself. Hogwarts restricts these additions because rune magic isn't flashy combat. It's infrastructure. You don't duel with it. You trap, blind, and seal things longterm, which makes mistakes inconvenient. Cracking the top 10, the essential defense against the dark arts. Harry Potter's favorite book. Apparently, this isn't a textbook. It's a survival manual. shield charms, counter curses, anti-jinxes, and fast response spells designed for situations where the other wizard is very much trying to end you. This is where spells like expelarmmus show up in a serious way. And yes, Harry absolutely took that and decided it was the only spell he'd ever need again. Death Eater, Expel Armus. Noseless wizard, expelarmmus. Sleepwalking classmate, still expellus. The book focuses on defensive efficiency, not flashy dark magic. How to block, how to counter, how to stay standing when things go wrong, which they do constantly. It becomes essential once Hogwarts quietly accepts a fact. Their students are growing up during a war, and homework has consequences now. Now, at ninth place, From Egg to Inferno: A Dragon Keeper Guide, the book that assumes you've already accepted death. This is not a student textbook. gets a professional dragon handling manual that somehow exists at Hogwarts. Anyway, the book covers dragon breeding, egg incubation, feeding cycles, and very casually, how not to be incinerated. It details dragon species, flame temperatures, aggression triggers, and containment failures. All written with a calm tone of someone who has absolutely lost colleagues. And yes, this is basically the book Hagrid was using while illegally raising Norbert in a wooden hut, which explains a lot. It even worked right up until the dragon had to be smuggled off to Romania because keep it warm turns out not to be a long-term plan. This book doesn't teach magic. It teaches risk management badly. At eighth place, Spellman's syllibary, the spell book Hogwarts hopes you'll never browse. This isn't a how-to guide. It's a historical registry of spells, charms, jinxes, hexes, and curses listed alphabetically with minimal context. Some are obsolete, some are unstable, some are illegal for very good reasons. Professors use this book to identify spell effects after something has already gone wrong. Because when magic explodes, knowing what caused it matters more than knowing who cast it. The problem? The book doesn't separate useful from catastrophic. A cleaning charm can sit right next to something that permanently alters a human body. No warnings, no skill checks, just names. The Forbidden Edge is access. This book treats all magic equally. Hogwarts locks it up because history matters. But flipping through it feels like scrolling a menu you were never meant to order from. At seventh place, Most Potente Potions, the book that ruined a perfectly good bathroom. This is the authorative guide to extremely advanced potions, including Polyjuice Potion, the one Hermione used in second year to impersonate Slytherins and spy on Malfoy about the Chamber of Secrets. It worked well mostly. The book gives exact instructions for identity altering bruise. Assuming the reader is skilled, supervised, and capable of distinguishing ingredients correctly, Hermione nailed everything except one detail. Human hair is not the same as cat hair. result, weeks in the hospital wing and a very important lesson in ingredient verification. Hogwarts keeps this book restricted so professors can recognize illegal brews before they spread. Not so students can try them in bathrooms. The Forbidden Edge isn't power, it's confidence, and confidence plus potions is how disasters brew. Entering sixth place, Advanced Potion Making Half-Blood Prince Edition. The moment potions stopped being polite. This is the same sixthear textbook everyone else gets. Except this copy has been aggressively upgraded by the half-blood prince. The handwritten notes don't suggest improvements. They overwrite the rules. Stir this way. Add that later. Ignore the instructions entirely. The result? Faster bruise. Cleaner reactions. Potions that actually work the way they're supposed to. Harry follows the notes blindly and suddenly outperforms students who've been studying for years, including Hermione, which should have raised more questions than it did. The margins also contain original spells, experimental tweaks, and general tone of who approved this nonsense. Classic Snape. Top five. Nature's Nobility. The book that ruins pure blood dinner parties. This is the definitive genealogy of wizarding families, tracing bloodlines, marriages, and magical inheritance across generations. It's dry, it's factual, and it's socially explosive. Flip through it, and you'll find the Blacks, the Weasley's, the Malfoys, and a lot of inconvenient overlap. Families obsessed with purity appear connected to muggles, half-bloods, and each other in ways they'd rather not discuss. It's also used by scholars to study inherited magical traits, recurring abilities, and long-term family patterns. Hogwarts restricts access, not because it causes fights, but because once you read it, you can't unsee where everyone really comes from. At fourth place, a study of recent developments in wizardry. The book Hogwarts doesn't love, but can't ignore. This isn't ancient theory. It's magic gossip in textbook form. New spells, experimental enchantments, controversial breakthroughs, everything that's changing right now before the ministry decides whether it's legal. This book tracks modern magic responding to war, muggle technology, and whatever bizarre Tik Tok dance they're doing this week, and political pressure. Some spells inside are half-tested. Others openly contradict what professors taught a few years ago. And yes, some are quietly removed in later printings. This book doesn't feel like homework. It feels like reading notes you weren't meant to see yet, which explains why it's restricted and why everyone wants access anyway. Top three. Magic Most Evil. This is a book that should come with gloves, warning, and maybe a therapist. This ancient text predates Hogwarts and was donated to the library as a cautionary artifact, not a teaching manual. The idea was simple. Know what dark magic looks like so you don't accidentally recreate it. Irony noted. This is the book Harry opens in Philosopher Stone. The one that immediately screams, a built-in security system that somehow passed inspection. The spells inside focus on curses meant to linger, escalate, and scar magically and emotionally. Hogwarts keeps it because destroying dark magic text doesn't erase dark magic. It just makes it harder to recognize. So, they lock it up, label it forbidden, and hope no one's curious enough to open it. Well, spoiler, hope has never worked at Hogwarts. At number two, Secrets of the Darkest Art. The book that turned library research into a lifelong villain arc. This is the most complete written source on Horcruxes ever allowed inside Hogwarts. It didn't end up there by accident. Early headmasters kept it as a reference, not for students, but so professors could recognize dark magic before it showed up in practice. In theory, Tom Riddle found it anyway. He later tells Slugghorn he'd been reading about Horcruxes in the library, which is Hogwarts code for someone messed up access control. The book is terrifying because of how calmly it explains soul spplitting. No drama, no warnings, just process, cost, outcome. It reads like dark magic has already won the debate. Hogwarts keeps it locked up because banning knowledge entirely has historically gone poorly. They keep it hidden to understand the enemy. Unfortunately, curiosity lives here, too. And finally, first place, the book that decides your Hogwarts letter before you can even hold a wand. The book of admittance works alongside the quill of acceptance. And together they do one thing. Decide who is magical enough to be invited to Hogwarts. The moment a child shows signs of magic, the quill attempts to write their name. Strong magic, the book slams shut and locks it in permanently. Weak, unstable, or accidental magic, the quill hesitates, sometimes for years. That hesitation has consequences. If the quill never commits, the name never appears. No letter, no Hogwarts, no explanation. The system was enchanted by the founders themselves and has been operating autonomously ever since. No headmaster control, no ministry oversight, no appeals process. Double door didn't manage it. McGonagal doesn't audit it. They trust it because arguing with it has never gone well. This book is kept hidden deep within Hogwarts, protected by enchantments older than the school's staircases. It has recorded centuries of witches and wizards and quietly ignored countless others. Students think their journey begins with a letter. It doesn't. It begins with Ink deciding your entire life. But don't worry if you didn't get your Hogwarts letter. We've got options. At 12th place, Seis Snape. The poorest rich guy on this list. Let's be honest, Snape didn't grow up wealthy. === ALL Sacred Pure Blood Families in Harry Potter EXPLAINED in Detail ===
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=720X0G9hqwg

Abbott. You probably know that in Harry Potter there are pureb blood families like the Malfoys, the Blacks, or even the Weasley's. Yeah, shocking. But what you might not know is that in the 1930s, a deeply prejudiced wizard named Canankerous not sat down, grabbed a quill, and published what he called the Sacred Pure Blood Directory, better known as the Sacred 28. Basically, if you were on this list, your great greatgrandparents were all witches and wizards. But if your name wasn't on it, you'd be judged for mixing with muggles. Very Malfoy energy. And opening the sacred 28 list, Abbott. They ruined the vibe instantly. They're technically ancient, technically pure blood until the early 1930s when the Abbotts collectively decided that marrying muggles and halfbloods was in fact fine, actually scandalous. Their most famous descendant, Hannah Abbott, attended Hogwarts with Harry Potter and embodied everything pure blood purists hate. Hufflepuff loyalty, kindness, and zero interest in supremacy nonsense. She joined Dumbledore's army, fought in the Battle of Hogwarts, and lost her mother to Death Eaters, making it very clear where the family stood when things got real. Hannah later married Neville Longbottom, officially sealing the Abbott legacy as emotionally healthy and wildly disappointing to pure blood extremists. Other Abbotts exist, including a Hogwarts portrait named Gford Abbott and Theophilus Abbott, an American wizard possibly related to the clan. In short, the Abbotts prove that common sense ages far better than blood purity, and also that being first of the list doesn't mean you understood the assignment. Avery. Avery is one of those sacred 28 names that sounds important until you realize their main hobby was lurking behind masks and calling it legacy. Known for being chronically on the wrong side of history, the Avery show up in Voldemort's orbit more than they show up in anything respectable. An Avery was part of Voldemort's inner circle in the First War, and another crawled back in the Second War with the classic excuse, "I was under the Imperious curse." Voldemort, master of unforgivable curses, was not emotionally moved. The Avery brand is basically this pedigree, entitlement, and zero backbone when consequences arrive. Pure blood obsession didn't make them powerful. It just made them predictable. Black. If wizarding families were fireworks, the blacks would be the kind that explode beautifully and then set the house on fire. Obsessed with lineage, image, and reminding everyone they were better than them. The House of Black treated blood purity like a competitive sport. Their family motto, Tujour Pure, wasn't aspirational. It was aggressive. For centuries, they curated marriages, hoarded dark artifacts, and blasted relatives off the family tapestry like unwanted stains. That tapestry hanging in 12 Grimmald place doubled as both genealogy and warning label. Then, serious black happened. sorted into Gryffindor, allergic to supremacy, best friends with James Potter, and a proud member of the Order of the Phoenix. Basically, the family's worst nightmare wrapped in leather jackets and rebellion. Meanwhile, cousins Bellatrix, Lrange, and Narcissa Malfoy went allin on Voldemort, proving the family could still produce top tier villains. In the end, the Black spent centuries protecting their legacy, only for it to collapse under its own arrogance. Tragic, hilarious, extremely onbrand. Bolstrode. The Bolstrode family technically qualified for the sacred 28. And honestly, everyone seems a bit confused about how they were mostly pure-blood right up until the point where they very clearly weren't. Like the Abbotts, Bolstrods began marrying muggles and half-blood shortly after the list was published, quietly disqualifying themselves while still enjoying the label. Their most famous descendant, Millisent Bolstro, attended Hogwarts with Harry and is remembered less for blood status and more for accidentally turning Hermione Granger into a cat via Apollu's potion disaster. It's unclear if the Bolstrods ever cared deeply about purity politics, but by Millison's era, the family's pure reputation was already extinct, which is fair. No one was flexing that surname anyway. Burke. If Hogwarts had a gift shop for cursed nightmares, the Burke family would be running it. Pure Blood by lineage, morally bankrupt by choice. The Burks made their fortune through Borggan and Burks. Nocturnal's favorite place to buy objects that whisper, bite, or ruin your life. Carrick Tacis Burke didn't just sell dark artifacts. He lowballed them, famously buying Salazar Slytherin's locket from Voldemort's desperate mother for spare change and zero shame. Later, the shop conveniently supplied the vanishing cabinet Draco Malfoy used to smuggle Death Eaters into Hogwarts. Because why stop at unethical when you can be catastrophic? Even their portrait, Elizabeth Burke, spent her afterlife encouraging Slytherins to be nasty to mud bloods. The Burks weren't loyal to blood, ideology, or dark lords, only to profit. If evil had a loyalty card, they'd already have a free curse. Caro. The Caro family proves that pureb blood fanaticism can in fact get worse. Their dark streak goes back to the 1920s with ties to Grindleald, but they truly peaked in cruelty with siblings Alleto and Amicus, two death eataters who treated Hogwarts like a torture workshop, appointed professors during Voldemort's takeover. They used the cruciis curse as a teaching method and called it education. Even by Death Eater standards, they were considered excessive. When everyone hates you, including other villains, that's not legacy. That's a warning label. Crouch. The Crouch family is what happens when you mistake rules for parenting. Ancient, pure blood, and deeply respected, they sat comfortably among Britain's wizarding elite until everything exploded. Barmus Crouch Senior built his legacy on law, order, and punishing literally everyone, which made him powerful at the ministry and emotionally unavailable at home. Shockingly, this did not produce a well-adjusted child. His son, Barty Crouch Jr., responded by becoming an unhinged death eater, getting sent to Aszkaban, then escaping via a polyjuice potion swap involving his dying mother. Healthy family dynamics. Disguised as Madi Moody, Barty Jr. infiltrated Hogwarts, rigged the Goblet of Fire, traumatized an entire school year, and personally delivered Harry Potter to Voldemort. Eventually, he murdered his own father and received the dementor's kiss. neatly wiping out the entire Crouch bloodline. Once admired for discipline and respectability, the family ended as a cautionary tale. Pure blood doesn't prevent catastrophic parenting or spectacular self-destruction. Folly. The Folly family proves that being on the sacred 28 didn't require drama, just persistence in a talent for not being noticed. Pure blood frequently sorted into Hufflepuff and aggressively mild. The Follys were more about paperwork than power. Their most notable member, Hector Folly, served as Minister for Magic in the 1920s and 30s, right before Grindleald caused chaos, yet somehow managed to leave absolutely no impression. No dark lords, no rebellions, no scandals. By wizarding standards, that's either admirable or suspicious. Flint. The Flint family is ancient, pure blood, and best remembered for having the subtlety of a bludger to the face. While their lineage stretches back centuries, most people hear Flint and immediately picture Marcus Flint, Slytherin's aggressively violent Quidditch captain, who treated rules like optional suggestions. Historically, the family did have influence. Josephina Flint served as minister for magic in the early 1800s, a rare achievement that deserves credit. They also married into the House of Black when Ursula Flint wed Phineas Nigel Black, securing respectable connections without the drama. The Flints maintained pure blood status, but never reached Malfoy level notoriety or black family infamy. Instead, they mastered quiet ambition, relevant, connected, and just intimidating enough to avoid scrutiny. Subtle? Never. Effective? Unfortunately, yes. Gaunt. If the Sacred 28 had a lesson in what not to do carved into stone, it would be the Gaunt family. Descended directly from Salazar Slytherin and Cadmus Peverell, the Gaunt had everything pure blood fanatics dream of and then spectacularly ruined it. Obsessed with lineage, they treated blood purity like a DIY project and promptly turned their family tree into a circle. Cousin marriages became tradition. Mental stability became optional, and basic hygiene apparently offensive. Once wealthy and powerful, the Gaunt spiraled into isolation and poverty, ending up in a collapsing shack near Little Hangleton that screamed ancestral shame. Marvllo Gaunt ruled through violence and paranoia. His daughter, Maropi, lived abused and broken, and her brother Morphin took the blame for a murder his nephew, Tom Riddle, actually committed. Yes, that Tom Riddle. The family also guarded the chamber of secrets for generations and preserved the ability to speak parcel tongue, clinging to it like proof they were still special. They even kept Slytherin's locket. Later turned into a horcrux because of course they did. By the end, the Gans had achieved the ultimate irony, a pure blood legacy so obsessed with survival that it produced the very wizard who destroyed it completely, Greengrass. The Greengrass family is what happens when a pureb blood line quietly realizes it's been on the wrong side of history and decides to stop. Originally proud members of the sacred 28, they later evolved into something shocking, common sense. Their biggest plot twist came with Atoria Greengrass, who married Draco Malfoy and then proceeded to reject the entire anti-muggle playbook. She even raised Scorpius with compassion and tolerance, much to the visible discomfort of the Malfoy dinner table. The green grasses never loudly supported Voldemort, never chased dark glory and never imploded spectacularly. And here's the real evidence Draco actually changed. Scorpius's best friend at Hogwarts is Albus Potter. Yes, that Potter. If that's not a character arc, nothing is. Honestly, good for them. Lrange. The Lrange family is pure blood elitism turned into a lifestyle choice and then taken way too far. Filthy rich and proudly tangled through marriages with families like the Blacks and Rosier. The Langranges treated inbreeding like tradition, not a warning sign. Their fascination with dark magic was less academic curiosity and more hobby that ruins everyone's life. Historically, they produced figures like Rulus Lrange, a 19th century minister for magic and Lita Lrange, whose tragic Hogwarts years accidentally got N's commander expelled. But let's be honest, the name became legendary thanks to Bellatrix Lrange, born Bellatrix Black. She married Rulus purely to tick the respectable pureb blood husband box while saving her actual passion for Voldemort. Romantic, I know. Together, the Lrangers became some of the most devoted and unhinged death eataters, specializing in torture, murder, and screaming enthusiasm. In the end, their obsession with purity consumed them entirely. No he errors, no redemption, just a legacy proving that madness unfortunately can be hereditary. Long Bottom. The Longbottom family is proof that being pure blood doesn't automatically turn you into a lunatic with a family tapestry and a cursed collection. The Long Bottoms built their reputation on service, bravery, and actually doing their jobs. An extremely rare flex among the sacred 28. Many became orers and ministry officials known for integrity rather than superiority complexes. And then there's Neville Longbottom, the boy who lost his toad, forgot passwords, and somehow ended up destroying Voldemort's final whorcs. Character development of the century. Neville's parents, Frank and Alice, were brilliant orers before being tortured into insanity by Death Eaters, leaving Neville to be raised by his terrifyingly strict grandmother, Augusta. Expectations were high. Confidence was low. Courage, however, was quietly loading in the background. By the battle of Hogwarts, Neville went from background character to absolute menace, killing Nagini and leading the resistance inside Hogwarts itself. The Long Bottoms never obsessed over blood purity. They were on the Sacred 28 by ancestry alone, not ideology. In a wizarding world addicted to arrogance, the Long Bottoms accidentally became the gold standard. Decent, brave, and devastating when it actually mattered. McMillan. If the Sacred 28 were a party, the McMillan would be the ones standing near the snacks, quietly judging everyone else. Ancient and pure blood, yes, but aggressively neutral. No Dark Lords, no manifestos, no cursed heirlooms screaming in the attic. Their standout member, Ernie McMillan, proudly reminded people his family was pure blood and then immediately joined Dumbledore's army just to keep things balanced. A Hufflepuff through and through. Ernie backed Dumbledore during Voldemort's rise and proved that ancestry didn't require cruelty. The McMillan showed you could be on the list, stay respectable, and still avoid becoming a walking red flag. Malfoy. The Malfoys are hands down the most dramatic pure-blood family on the Sacred 28. And it's all thanks to the very first Malfoy who ever decided proximity to power was better than actually earning it. That honor goes to Arman Malfoy, who arrived in Britain with William the Conqueror and immediately mastered the Malfoy family motto. Stand next to the throne, not on it. He flattered muggle kings, collected land, and secured a lifestyle that future Malfoys would coast on for centuries. From there, the formula never changed. Malfoy Manor became less a home and more a permanent networking venue for influential people and morally questionable decisions. generation after generation was sorted into Slytherin, raised on ambition, manipulation, and an almost medical allergy to humility. And by the modern era, Lucius Malfoy became the family's modern mascot. Wealthy, loud, and deeply committed to being intimidating right up until real danger appeared. Narcissus perfected selective loyalty, and Draco inherited the arrogance without the stomach for actual evil. When Voldemort stopped looking like a winning investment, the Malfoys instantly rebranded as just trying to survive. And the final insult, after centuries guarding Pure Blood, Draco married Atoria Greengrass and raised Scorpius to be kind and be best friends with Harry Potter's son. Lucius is somewhere screaming internally and pretending this was always part of the plan. Not pure blood, Slytherin, and allergic to attention. The knot name has always hovered uncomfortably close to power without ever stepping fully into the light. Generations of knots passed through Hogwarts keeping their hands down and their opinions sharp. Canankerous knot is widely believed to have written the sacred 28 itself which tracks because it reads like something penned by a bitter man with too much time and way too many grudges. In Harry's year, Theodore Knot was quiet, intelligent, and notably uninterested in Draco's theatrics. Despite his father being an enthusiastic death eater loyal to Voldemort, when the war ended, the Knots survived the only way they knew how, by saying nothing, doing nothing, and pretending they were never involved. Cowardly, maybe effective, unfortunately. Ollivander. Long before wizarding families started arguing about blood status, the Ollivanders were busy doing something far more dangerous. handing everyone a weapon. Their lineage dates back to 382 BC, making them older than the Ministry of Magic and most family grudges combined. Legend says an early Ollivander arrived in Britain with the Romans. Took one look around and thought, "Yes, this place needs better wands." From that moment on, the name Ollivander became synonymous with wand making perfection. Generations refined the craft until Garrick Ollivander turned it into an art form. Quiet, obsessive, and slightly unsettling, he personally made the wands of Harry Potter and Lord Voldemort, complete with twin Phoenix feather cores, accidentally binding their destinies forever. Oops. Unlike other pureb blood families, the Ollivanders avoided politics, prejudice, and dark lord loyalty. They sold to everyone. Gryffindor heroes, Slytherin villains, ministry officials. Same smile, same receipt. While others fought over superiority, the Ollivanders calmly supplied the tools that made the wars possible. Honestly, terrifying business model. Parkinson. The Parkinson family loved the idea of being important far more than the work required to, you know, actually be important. An old pure blood name with fading relevance. Their modern reputation rests almost entirely on Pansy Parkinson. Slytherin, Draco Malfoy enthusiast, faced like a pug, and living proof that confidence and courage are not the same thing. Her proudest historical moment was suggesting Harry Potter be handed over to Voldemort during the Battle of Hogwarts. Brave stuff. Centuries later, Perseus Parkinson served as Minister for Magic. But by Harry's era, the family legacy had devolved into gossip, sneering, and shouting from the back row. More attitude than achievement. Puit. The Puit family technically qualified as pure blood and then immediately ignored everything pure blood culture stood for. Closely related to the Weasley's, they valued courage over pedigree and decency over delusions of superiority. Scandalous behavior for sacred 28 standards. Their most famous descendant, Molly Puit, married Arthur Weasley, a union that likely caused several aristocratic monles to shatter on impact. Her brothers Gideon and Fabian Puit joined the Order of the Phoenix and were brutally killed during the First Wizarding War. A loss so heavy even Madi Moody kept their names engraved as a reminder. Red hair, big noses, and absolutely zero tolerance for blood purity nonsense. The Puits proved that legacy isn't about keeping bloodlines clean, but about standing your ground while the so-called noble families lost their mind. Rosier. The Rosier were old, elegant, and extremely confident that being French and pure blood somehow made them unstoppable. Spoiler, it did not. Proud of their lineage, and deeply invested in dark politics, they managed to back every wrong wizarding trend. When Rosier supported Grindleald in the early 1900s, and decades later, Evan Rosier joined Voldemort's Death Eaters, famously losing a duel to Madi Moody after managing to remove part of Moody's nose. impressive but not survivable. The Rosier believed ancestry equaled immunity. History disagreed. Role. The Ro family mastered the rare art of making the same bad choices across multiple centuries. In the 1700s, Damakles role served as minister for magic and decided muggles were the problem. Classic overcorrection. Lots of paranoia, zero chill. Fast forward and Thorin role kept the tradition alive by becoming a death eater under Voldemort, bringing brute force where thinking might have helped. To really seal the deal, Euphemia role later became guardian to Delinei, Voldemort's daughter. Ancient lineage, impressive consistency, and absolutely no evidence that wisdom improves with age. Selwin. The Selwins were pure blood aristocracy with a capital A. status, influence, and the kind of name Voldemort himself liked to drop for credibility, which should definitely already worry you. Ministry adjacent and painfully self-important. They carried just enough clout to be dangerous. At least one Selwin became a Death Eater and helped kidnap Luna Loveg Good, instantly upgrading the family name from respectable to actively awful. And then there's Dolores Umbridge, who claimed Selwin ancestry. Never proven, but deeply believable given her obsession with authority and blood status. The Selwin legacy boils down to this impressive name, terrible judgment, and a habit of choosing the worst possible side while insisting they're the good ones. Shacklebolt. Pure blood. Yes. Smug about it? Absolutely not. The Shacklebolts somehow dodged the sacred 28 ego epidemic and focused on competence instead. Their crown jewel, Kingsley Shacklebolt, was a top tier or order of the Phoenix member and literal bodyguard to the Muggle Prime Minister because someone had to be the adult in the room. After Voldemort fell, Kingsley became minister for magic, proving a radical idea. Leadership works better when you're not racist, corrupt, or unhinged. Truly shocking behavior for a pureb blood family. Sheffic. The Sheffix are the sacred 28's greatest magic trick. Being important and then vanishing completely. Confirmed. Pure Bloods when the directory dropped in the 1930s and then poof, gone. No scandals, no dark lords, no embarrassing heirs shouting at school children. Their name suggests non-British roots possibly tied to ancient magical trade networks long before the ministry tried to organize everything. Whether they intermarried, moved abroad, or simply chose peace over pure blood nonsense, the Shafix pulled off the smartest move on the list. They left before things got stupid. Slugghorn. If pure blood families were potions, the slug horns would be liquid luck. Not flashy, not evil, just suspiciously well positioned at all times. Wealthy and pure blood, yes, but far more interested in influence than ideology. Their most famous member, Horus Slugghorn, perfected the art of surviving history by knowing the right people and never picking the wrong side too loudly. as head of Slytherin and potions master twice. Slugghorn collected talented students the way others collected dark artifacts. His infamous slug club included Lily Evans and Tom Riddle, which in hindsight feels like inviting Felix Felicis and Poison to the same dinner party and hoping for balance. He tolerated muggleborns, admired brilliance over blood, and still managed to accidentally give Voldemort Horcrux advice. Oops. The Slugghorns weren't fanatics. They were opportunists with manners. Proof that ambition doesn't always lead to tyranny. Sometimes it just leads to exclusive sofas, candied pineapple, and being on the right side of history. Eventually, Travers, pure blood status, zero charm, maximum brutality. That's the Travers brand. While other families hid behind politeness, the Travers name showed up where things got bloody. A death eater known simply as Travers helped murder the McKinnon family during the First Wizarding War, proving subtlety was never on the syllabus. Somehow the line survived long enough for Torquill Travers to resurface in the 1990s as head of magical law enforcement, which is concerning. The Travers strategy was simple. Commit atrocities for Lord Voldemort, then use influence to dodge consequences. History repeated. Lessons were not learned. Weasley, if the Sacred 28 were a snoody dinner party, the Weasley's were the family everyone whispered about because they talked to muggles. And Merlin forbid liked them. impeccable pureb blood lineage, absolutely zero interest in acting superior about it. Naturally, this made them public enemy number one for families who mistook cruelty for class. They lived in the burrow, a house held together by magic, love, and whatever Arthur Weasley dragged home from the muggle world. Arthur himself collected plugs like rare artifacts, while Molly ran the place with terrifying efficiency and the quiet knowledge that she could defeat most dark witches using only rage and maternal instinct. Seven children followed. Bill, Charlie, Percy, Fred, George, Ron, and Jinny. Each proving in their own way that courage, chaos, and questionable decision-making are hereditary. Nearly all of them fought Voldemort, joined the Order of the Phoenix, or married directly into the prophecy just to annoy the Malfoys one last time. They were mocked for handme-down robes, red hair, and an alarming lack of gold. And yet, when the war came, the Weasley showed up every single time, together, loyal, and unbreakable. Funny how the so-called blood traitors ended up being the bravest, kindest, and most genuinely noble family on the entire list. Almost like blood purity was never the point. Yaxley. The Yaxley's were what happens when a pureb blood family decides paperwork is just as evil as curses. Not much is known about their ancient glory. Mostly because Corbin Yaxley did such an excellent job ruining the brand. A death eater in both wars, he skipped dramatic duels and went straight for the real villain arc, bureaucracy. While others waved wands, Yakley smiled politely and passed laws that wrecked lives, infiltrating the ministry and turning magical law enforcement into Voldemort's customer service desk from hell. Raids, arrests, torture, signed, stamped, and approved. After the dark lord fell, Yakley landed in Aszkaban, proving once again that pure blood pairs terribly with absolute power. A cautionary tale, really. Sometimes the scariest death eaters don't shout, they file forms. And if you survive this wicked list, as Ron would say, then you're clearly ready for more Potterhead Gossip. All 12 richest wizarding families in Harry Potter explained. Click now before Ron remembers Malfoy had a mansion and still complained.


=== All 10 MAJOR Events After The Deathly Hallows EXPLAINED in Detail ===
https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=9Utwf4tHg9A

Bye-bye Dementors. For centuries, Azkaban's entire security system was basically, let's hire soul-sucking depression ghosts and hope for the best. And shockingly, that went terribly. Dementors weren't just guards, they were 10-ft tall cloaked nightmares whose job description was emotionally destroy you until you forget what happiness is. Prisoners stopped eating, lost their minds. Some didn't last months. As Sirius Black casually put it, if Dementors were getting excited, yeah, someone was about to die. So, after Voldemort finally got defeated, again, no nose, big ego, classic mistake, the Ministry had a rare moment of clarity. Kingsley Shacklebolt looked at Azkaban and went, maybe outsourcing justice to creatures that literally feed on suffering isn't great policy. Wild idea, I know. Dementors were kicked out permanently. Turns out they were also terrible at loyalty, since they happily joined Voldemort the moment he promised them unlimited snacking rights. With them gone, Azkaban got a actual security upgrade. Trained Aurors, layered magical wards, enchanted constructs, and get this, non-lethal restraint. Revolutionary. The prison shifted from eternal psychological torture to something resembling justice. For the first time in centuries, Azkaban stopped being a horror story parents whispered about and started being a prison. Sadly, this reform happened before Dolores Umbridge was sent to Azkaban. Lucky for her, unlucky for everyone else. Don't worry, we'll get back to Umbridge later. Hogwarts after the war. After the Battle of Hogwarts, the castle looked less like a school and more like the world's oldest magical disaster zone. Towers collapsed, courtyards were blasted apart. The Astronomy Tower had been through so much drama, it honestly deserved a memorial plaque. And the Great Hall, usually reserved for feasts, shouting, and Dumbledore's speeches, had hosted an actual war. Classic Hogwarts. The real problem, though, wasn't just the broken stone, it was the magic. Professors, Order members, and several extremely brave people had layered so many defensive enchantments onto the castle that Hogwarts basically became magically unstable. The solution? Strip it all down and rebuild everything from scratch. Very calming, very safe. Reconstruction started immediately under Kingsley Shacklebolt's new ministry. Curse-breakers were called in to scrub out dark magic residue. Unstable artifacts left behind by Death Eaters were removed quietly before any curious first-years could poke them. Magical engineers reinforced the castle using upgraded stone conjuration charms and weatherproofing, spells that even Muggles would approve of. Minerva McGonagall stayed on as Headmistress because if anyone could rebuild a sentient death trap into a functioning school, it was definitely her. She restored the wards, strengthened boundaries, and redesigned Hogwarts defenses to stop wars before they happened, not during lunch. Classes returned, studying resumed, Quidditch was back. Defense Against the Dark Arts finally stopped being a cursed job, and History of Magic was forced to acknowledge recent events, including a decades-long war caused by a bald, noseless man nobody was allowed to name. Progress. Hagrid returned to his hut, ghosts kept gossiping, and somewhere in the walls Snape's legacy lingered, uncomfortable, complicated, and impossible to ignore. Same Hogwarts, stronger walls, harder lessons, and slightly fewer explosions, probably. The Golden Trio. While the rest of Hogwarts prepared to return to class, three students were noticeably missing. Not sick, not expelled, just busy destroying pieces of the Dark Lord's soul. Casual. Harry Potter, Ron Weasley, and Hermione Granger never returned for their seventh year. No N.E.W.T.s, no exams, no revision timetables. Turns out hunting Horcruxes across the country is not compatible with essay deadlines. Shocking. After Voldemort's defeat, the wizarding world had to face an awkward question. So, do we fail them? Kingsley Shacklebolt, now Minister for Magic and officially the most reasonable man alive, decided that surviving a war and ending a Dark Lord probably counted as work experience. Harry and Ron were invited straight into the Auror Office, no qualifications required, which is hilarious considering what most Aurors actually go through to get in. Harry became a full-time Auror specializing in tracking down escaped Death Eaters. Over time, he rose through the ranks and eventually became head of the Auror Office, responsible for training, strategy, and making sure no one ever ignored obvious dark signs again. Ron joined too, briefly. He helped clean up the mess Voldemort left behind, then wisely decided that risking his life daily was less appealing than running a joke shop. Ron left the Ministry and joined George at Weasleys' Wizard Wheezes, where many harmless joke products were absolutely inspired by wartime inventions. For legal reasons, we'll say adapted. Hermione, meanwhile, did the sensible thing. She went back to Hogwarts, finished her seventh year, sat her N.E.W.T.s, and then entered the Ministry to fix it from the inside. She worked on magical creature rights, reformed discriminatory laws, rose through magical law enforcement, and eventually became Minister for Magic. Different paths, same result. Turns out saving the world looks great on a resume. Post-war romance. After the war, the wizarding world did what it always does after surviving mass trauma. It immediately started dating the nearest emotionally compatible person and hoped therapy would magically happen later. Let's begin with George Weasley, because feelings are expensive. George married Angelina Johnson, Gryffindor Chaser, absolute legend, and yes, once Fred's girlfriend. Awkward? Well, only if you ignore the part where Angelina understood the twins before the war and George after it. They named their son Fred the Second because some legacies refused to disappear quietly, and a daughter, Roxanne, who definitely inherited the watch first strike later gene. George kept Weasleys' Wizard Wheezes running proving laughter was never just comedy, it was resistance. Ron helped for a bit which explains why several products now come with warning labels. Luna Lovegood shockingly did exactly what everyone should have expected and married Rolf Scamander, magizoologist, grandson of Newt and completely unfazed by reality. They traveled the world studying magical creatures and raised twins, Lorcan and Lysander, in an environment where being weird was encouraged and shoes were optional. Hogwarts was not prepared. Neville Longbottom married Hannah Abbott and quietly completed the greatest character arc in wizarding history. From almost forgotten kid to herbology professor to head of Gryffindor house. Meanwhile, Hannah ran The Leaky Cauldron like a boss. Plants upstairs, pub downstairs, zero nonsense. The softest power couple. Draco Malfoy married Astoria Greengrass who openly rejected blood purity, an act that probably caused at least three pureblood relatives to faint. Their son, Scorpius, grew up kind, awkward and painfully self-aware. After Astoria's early death, Draco raised him alone officially ending centuries of Malfoy emotional constipation. And Percy Weasley married Audrey, had two daughters, Molly and Lucy, and continued working at the Ministry with significantly fewer speeches and much more humility. Growth is possible. Post-war relationships weren't about fireworks, they were about survival, shared scars and choosing someone who knew exactly who you were when everything fell apart. Honestly, that's the most magical part. Petunia Dursley's quiet secret. Petunia Dursley spent most of her life pretending magic didn't exist or that if it did, it was Harry's fault. She complained about owls, hated letters and treated her nephew like an inconvenience that never learned to leave, which is why this part hurts. Petunia kept the blanket, the same blanket Harry was wrapped in the night Dumbledore left him on Privet Drive, the same one she was given when her world cracked open, when her sister was gone and magic became permanent. Decades later, after Petunia's death, Dudley sorted through her things and found it still there, folded, kept, not thrown out in one of her many absolutely no magic cleanouts. Dudley returned it to Harry, said she'd kept it all those years. In The Cursed Child, Harry admits he keeps that blanket close, that it brings him luck, comfort, a reminder that even the coldest places sometimes hide something warm. Petunia never apologized, never softened, never said the words that mattered, but she kept proof. And that's the thing about the Dursleys, they never said what they felt, they just hid it in cupboards, in silence, in a blanket they couldn't bring themselves to throw away. It wasn't love the way Harry deserved, but it was love the only way Petunia knew how. Harry and Dudley, no one expected this. Not Harry, not Dudley, and definitely not the universe. When the Dursleys went into hiding during the Second Wizarding War, Harry and Dudley shared one final moment outside Privet Drive. No shouting, no insults, just Dudley, yes, that Dudley, awkwardly offering Harry his hand. The same cousin who once chased him around playgrounds now quietly acknowledged something important, Harry had saved their lives. It wasn't dramatic, it was emotional, which somehow made it worse. That handshake mattered, it wasn't forgiveness, it was friendship, it was recognition. Dudley finally understood that the weird cousin in the cupboard wasn't the problem, the world was, and Harry had stood between it and them anyway. According to Rowling, this wasn't a one-time miracle. Harry and Dudley reached what can only be described as Christmas card terms, occasional visits, polite silence, children playing while adults pretend not to remember 20 years of trauma. Painfully awkward, deeply British. Dudley grew up, married, had two children, and this is the real plot twist, he didn't pass on his parents' hatred. He treated Harry's kids like family. No fear, no prejudice, no comments about magic ruining everything. They never came close, but they stopped being enemies. And for two boys raised in the same house under very different rules, that's kind of huge. Death Eaters, pure bloods, and Snape. After Voldemort fell, the wizarding world didn't just clean up rubble. It quietly realized that three words no longer meant what they used to. First, Death Eater. Before the war, that title meant fear, influence, people pretending not to see the dark mark glowing under the sleeve. After the war, it meant paperwork, trials, awkward explanations that always started with, "I was Imperiused, actually." Suddenly, being a Death Eater didn't make doors open. It made Aurors take notes. Power vanished the moment the Dark Lord did. Then came the pure bloods. For centuries, they ran things by doing absolutely nothing except being born into the right family. Ancient names, older portraits, opinions that should have retired centuries ago. After the war, blood purity stopped sounding impressive and started sounding suspicious. The Sacred Twenty-Eight went from elitist list to historical embarrassment. Turns out backing a noseless tyrant is terrible for your social standing. Who knew? And then there was Severus Snape, the one who didn't fit cleanly into either category. Snape died labeled a traitor by the world and a hero by the few who knew the truth. After the war, his name became a battleground. Villain, martyr, something far more uncomfortable. Students learned what he actually did years later and immediately argued about it forever. And that's the point. Snape proved something the war made impossible to ignore, that loyalty doesn't always look noble, that heroism isn't always kind, and that people who saved the world don't always get statues or applause. Umbridge gets exactly what she deserves. Dolores Umbridge did not quietly disappear after the war, which is unfortunate for her and excellent for everyone else. During Voldemort's reign, Umbridge didn't just follow orders, she thrived. She wrapped the Dark Lord's ideology in pink cardigans, kitten plates, and bureaucratic paperwork, proving once and for all that evil doesn't need robes and skulls. Sometimes it just needs forms, lots of forms. So, when Voldemort fell and Kingsley Shacklebolt took over the Ministry, the cleanup was swift. Collaborators were identified, trials were held, and Umbridge, shockingly, could not just "I'm just doing my job" her way out of this one. === All 15 RICHEST Witches in Harry Potter EXPLAINED in Detail ===
